HW-vector"] flat --> mlp["MLP / SVM / RF"] flat --> bad["spatial relationships
(adjacency, locality)
are LOST"] classDef warn fill:#fdd class bad warn ``` The model has to *re-learn* "pixel (i,j) is next to pixel (i,j+1)" from scratch — using up data and parameters that should go to actual classification. ### Inductive bias of CNNs (slide 8) ```mermaid flowchart TD a["Assumption 1
locality
(features detectable from small windows)"] --> small["small kernels
3×3 / 5×5"] b["Assumption 2
translation invariance
(same pattern, anywhere)"] --> share["shared weights
across spatial positions"] small --> efficient["fewer parameters
less data needed
better generalisation"] share --> efficient ``` Both biases together: convolution is **translation-equivariant** (shift the input ↔ shift the output), and pooling makes the eventual classification **translation-invariant**. ### Generic CNN classifier (slides 9, 21–22) ```mermaid flowchart LR in([Input image
3×H×W]) --> c1[Conv 3×3, 16 ch] c1 --> bn1[BatchNorm + ReLU] bn1 --> p1[MaxPool 2×2] p1 --> c2[Conv 3×3, 32 ch] c2 --> bn2[BatchNorm + ReLU] bn2 --> p2[MaxPool 2×2] p2 --> c3[Conv 3×3, 64 ch] c3 --> bn3[BatchNorm + ReLU] bn3 --> p3[MaxPool 2×2] p3 --> gap[Global AvgPool] gap --> flat[Flatten] flat --> fc1[Linear 64→512 + ReLU + Dropout 0.5] fc1 --> fc2[Linear 512→num_classes] fc2 --> out([Logits → softmax]) classDef conv fill:#e3f2fd classDef pool fill:#fff3e0 classDef fc fill:#f3e5f5 class c1,c2,c3,bn1,bn2,bn3 conv class p1,p2,p3,gap pool class fc1,fc2,flat fc ``` Convolutional trunk does feature extraction; classifier head does the categorical decision. ### Standard vs. depthwise-separable convolution (slide 13) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Std[Standard conv] ss["input C×H×W"] --> sk["one filter:
K×K×C"] sk --> so["output 1×H'×W'
(times C_out filters)"] end subgraph DW[Depthwise separable] ds["input C×H×W"] --> dwf["depthwise:
per-channel K×K×1
(C filters total)"] dwf --> dom["output C×H'×W'"] dom --> pwf["pointwise 1×1×C
(C_out filters)"] pwf --> dout["output C_out×H'×W'"] end ``` Depthwise separable replaces $K^2 C C_\text{out}$ multiplications with $K^2 C + C C_\text{out}$ — used in MobileNet/EfficientNet for efficiency. ### Pooling (slides 17–18) ```mermaid flowchart LR fmap["feature map
e.g. 4×4"] --> mp["MaxPool 2×2
→ 2×2 (max in window)"] fmap --> ap["AvgPool 2×2
→ 2×2 (mean in window)"] fmap --> sp["SumPool 2×2
→ 2×2 (sum in window)"] mp --> note1["fires if any pixel
in window is bright"] ap --> note2["smoothed downsample"] sp --> note3["preserves total energy"] ``` Pooling has no learnable parameters. Max-pool dominates in CNNs because it extracts the strongest activation in a window — robust to small shifts. ### Receptive-field growth (slide 15, 18) ```mermaid flowchart LR p0["pixel
at layer 0"] --> rf1["after 1st 3×3 conv:
3×3 RF"] rf1 --> rf2["after 2nd 3×3 conv:
5×5 RF"] rf2 --> rf3["after 2×2 pool (stride 2):
doubles RF growth rate"] rf3 --> rfd["after L convs + pools:
RF covers most/all of image"] ``` Pooling speeds receptive-field growth multiplicatively because subsequent convs operate at lower resolution where each pixel already corresponds to a larger input region. ## Open questions - Why does max-pool win over average-pool in classifier trunks but average-pool dominates as the *global* pool before the FC head? - How does the inductive bias of CNNs (locality + translation invariance) relate to that of vision transformers, which dispense with both? - Lecture says CNN was state-of-the-art until ~2021 — what changed with ViT, and on which problem regimes do CNNs still win? ## Connections to other lectures - Builds on [[backpropagation]], [[gradient-descent]], [[batch-normalization]], [[activation-functions]] from lecture 15. - Image-domain machinery (Sobel/Laplacian/Gaussian filters from lectures 11–12) is the *hand-designed* version of what conv layers learn. - Image moments / structure tensor (lectures 12–13) summarise spatial structure with a 2×2 covariance — conv kernels generalise that idea to learned filters. - Lecture 17 will extend this with **architectural patterns** (deeper stacks, residuals, inception, dense connections).

content.newText:
---
name: Convolution operation
description: Sliding dot-product between a learnable kernel and image patches — the core CNN primitive
type: concept
courses: [Computer-Vision]
source: [16_CNNs.pdf]
created: 2026-04-26
tags: [concept, deep-learning, cnn]
---

# Convolution operation (CNN)

A convolutional layer slides a small **learnable kernel** $w$ over an input image $x$ and computes a dot product at each position. The output is a **feature map** ([[feature-map]]).

## Definition

For a 2D input $x$ and kernel $w$ of size $H \times W$:

$$y(i, j) = \sum_{m=-H}^{H}\sum_{n=-W}^{W} x(i-m,\, j-n)\, w(m, n) + b$$

For multi-channel input (depth $C$):

$$y(i, j, k) = \sum_{c=0}^{C-1}\sum_{m, n} x(i-m,\, j-n,\, c)\, w_k(m, n, c) + b_k$$

One filter $w_k$ per output channel $k$, each filter has depth $C_\text{in}$. The total parameter count of a layer is $K \times K \times C_\text{in} \times C_\text{out} + C_\text{out}$ (bias).

## Convolution vs cross-correlation

True convolution flips the kernel; what neural network libraries actually compute is **cross-correlation**:

$$y(i, j) = \sum_{m, n} x(i+m,\, j+n)\, w(m, n) + b$$

It doesn't matter — because the kernel is **learned**, training will simply produce a flipped version of whatever true-convolution would have learned. The two parametrise the same family.

## Why kernels are small

Inductive bias ([[inductive-bias]]): meaningful image features are **local** (an edge, a corner, a small texture patch) and the **same pattern can appear anywhere**. So:

- **Locality** → small kernels (3×3, 5×5) suffice.
- **Translation invariance** → the same kernel weights are reused at every spatial location (parameter sharing).

Compared to a fully connected layer that would need $H \cdot W \cdot H' \cdot W'$ parameters for the same receptive area, conv reuses $K^2$ weights everywhere.

## In PyTorch

```python
nn.Conv2d(in_channels, out_channels, kernel_size,
          stride=1, padding=0, dilation=1, groups=1, bias=True)
```

- `groups=1` standard conv; `groups=in_channels` is **depthwise** conv (see [[depthwise-separable-conv]]).
- `padding=(k-1)//2, kernel_size=k` (odd k) gives "same" output spatial size.
- `dilation>1` spreads the kernel out — used for atrous/dilated convolutions in segmentation.

## Properties

- **Translation equivariance**: shifting the input by $(\Delta x, \Delta y)$ shifts the feature map by $(\Delta x, \Delta y)$. Combined with [[pooling]], the final classifier becomes translation invariant.
- **Linear**: the conv layer itself is linear in $x$; non-linearity comes from the activation that follows.
- **Compatible with batchnorm**: BN re-centres and rescales each output channel — see [[batch-normalization]].

## Related

- [[feature-map]] — the per-channel output
- [[padding-stride]] — controls output size
- [[receptive-field]] — input region a deep neuron sees
- [[depthwise-separable-conv]] — efficient channel-wise factorisation
- [[inductive-bias]] — why conv wins over MLP on images

content.newText:
--- name: Inductive bias description: Built-in assumptions of a model that shrink the hypothesis space — locality and translation invariance for CNNs type: concept courses: [Computer-Vision] source: [16_CNNs.pdf] created: 2026-04-26 tags: [concept, deep-learning, theory] --- # Inductive bias The **assumptions a model makes about the data** before any training. A strong inductive bias shrinks the space of representable functions, so: - if the assumptions match reality → model trains faster, with less data, and generalises better; - if they're wrong → the model is fundamentally limited (e.g. CNNs struggle on data without spatial structure). A model's inductive bias is encoded in its **architecture**, not its weights. ## Examples | Model | Inductive bias | |---|---| | MLP / random forest | none beyond "smoothness" — works on arbitrary feature vectors | | **CNN** | **locality + translation invariance** (spatial) | | RNN / LSTM | sequential / temporal locality | | Transformer | learned all-pairs attention; weak inductive bias → needs lots of data | | Graph NN | invariance to node ordering, message-passing locality | ## CNN's inductive biases ### 1. Locality Meaningful features can be detected from a small window of pixels — an edge, a corner, a texture patch. So a $3\times 3$ kernel is enough at the lowest level; larger features emerge through stacking. ### 2. Translation invariance The same pattern (a furry texture, an edge of a particular orientation) can appear anywhere in the image. So the **same kernel weights** are reused at every spatial location — parameter sharing. ```mermaid flowchart TD bias1["Locality"] --> small["small kernels
(3×3, 5×5)"] bias2["Translation invariance"] --> share["shared weights
across spatial positions"] small --> e1["fewer parameters"] share --> e1 e1 --> e2["less data needed,
better generalisation"] ``` These biases imply: - $|\text{CNN}| \ll |\text{MLP}|$ on parameter count for the same receptive area - training converges faster on natural images - the model doesn't have to *re-discover* spatial structure ## Translation equivariance vs invariance - The conv operation itself is **equivariant**: $\mathrm{shift}(f(x)) = f(\mathrm{shift}(x))$. Shifting the input shifts the feature map. - Combined with **global pooling** (see [[pooling]]), the final classification becomes **invariant** — same prediction regardless of object position. Strict translation invariance only emerges after the spatial dimension is collapsed (Global Average Pooling or Flatten + classifier head). ## When CNN's bias is wrong - Tabular data: no spatial structure → CNN's bias is irrelevant or harmful. Use MLPs / GBDTs. - Long-range dependencies (e.g. matching pixel A in the corner with pixel B on the other side): CNN's locality bias delays this until very deep layers. Transformers' all-pairs attention handles it directly. ## Related - [[convolution-operation]] — implementation of locality + sharing - [[receptive-field]] — how locality "grows" with depth - [[cnn-architecture]] — the full pattern

content.newText:
--- name: Feature map description: The output of a convolutional layer — one channel per filter, one activation value per spatial location type: concept courses: [Computer-Vision] source: [16_CNNs.pdf] created: 2026-04-26 tags: [concept, deep-learning, cnn] --- # Feature map The 3D activation tensor produced by a convolutional layer. Shape: $C_\text{out} \times H_\text{out} \times W_\text{out}$ (PyTorch channels-first). Each **channel** is the response of one filter applied across all spatial positions of the input — a 2D map showing where in the image that filter's pattern is present and how strongly. ## Reading a feature map | Position | Meaning | |---|---| | Channel index $k$ | which learned filter ("edge detector at 45°", "round-blob detector", …) | | Spatial coords $(i, j)$ | where in the input the filter is matching | | Value at $(k, i, j)$ | strength of that match | Early-layer feature maps respond to **low-level** patterns (edges, gradients, simple textures). Deeper-layer feature maps respond to **higher-level**, more abstract patterns (eyes, wheels, faces). ## Hierarchy of feature maps ```mermaid flowchart LR img([RGB image
3×H×W]) --> c1[Conv1] --> f1["64 feature maps
edges, blobs"] f1 --> c2[Conv2] --> f2["128 feature maps
textures, shapes"] f2 --> c3[Conv3] --> f3["256 feature maps
parts, motifs"] f3 --> c4[Conv4] --> f4["512 feature maps
objects, scenes"] f4 --> head[Classifier] ``` This hierarchy emerges *from training* — it isn't programmed, but consistently appears across well-trained CNNs. ## Why visualise Looking at an early-layer feature map directly (as a 2D image) is one of the few honest ways to inspect what a CNN has learned. A bright value at $(k, i, j)$ means "this filter strongly matches at this location." This is one of the few cases CLAUDE.md's diagram allowlist permits a slide image rather than a Mermaid diagram — the actual learned filter responses on real pixels are the content. ## Related - [[convolution-operation]] — produces feature maps - [[pooling]] — downsamples them - [[receptive-field]] — input region each feature-map cell summarises - [[cnn-architecture]] — the stack that grows the feature-map hierarchy

content.newText:
---
name: Padding & stride
description: Two knobs that control conv output size — padding adds zero borders, stride skips positions
type: concept
courses: [Computer-Vision]
source: [16_CNNs.pdf]
created: 2026-04-26
tags: [concept, deep-learning, cnn]
---

# Padding & stride

Two parameters of a convolution that determine the output spatial size and how the kernel walks across the input.

## Output-shape formula

$$L_\text{out} = \left\lfloor \frac{L_\text{in} + 2p - d(k-1) - 1}{s} + 1\right\rfloor$$

where $p$ = padding, $d$ = dilation (1 by default), $k$ = kernel size, $s$ = stride.

Simple case ($d=1$, $s=1$): $L_\text{out} = L_\text{in} + 2p - k + 1$.

## Padding

Adds $p$ rows/columns of (usually) zeros around the input borders before the kernel slides.

| Padding mode | Effect |
|---|---|
| **Valid** ($p=0$) | output shrinks: $L_\text{out} = L_\text{in} - k + 1$. Borders are visited fewer times. |
| **Same** ($p = (k-1)/2$, $s=1$, $k$ odd) | output keeps the input spatial size: $L_\text{out} = L_\text{in}$. |
| **Full** ($p = k-1$) | output grows to $L_\text{in} + k - 1$. Rarely used. |

Why "same" matters: stacking many "valid" convs slowly eats away at the spatial dims (each layer trims $k-1$ pixels), making receptive-field bookkeeping awkward and breaking architectures that need spatial alignment (U-Net, ResNet's residual addition).

`padding_mode='zeros'` is the default. Other modes: `'reflect'`, `'replicate'`, `'circular'` — useful when zero borders would create spurious edge responses.

## Stride

How many pixels the kernel jumps between successive applications.

- $s = 1$: standard, every position.
- $s = 2$: output is roughly half-size in each spatial dim — used as an alternative to pooling for downsampling.
- $s > k$: gaps in coverage — generally avoided.

Strided conv vs pooling for downsampling:

| Strided conv | Pooling |
|---|---|
| Has parameters; can learn the downsampling | No parameters; fixed reduction (max / avg) |
| Common in modern detectors and ResNets | Common in classic CNNs (VGG, AlexNet) |

## Quick examples

| Layer | $L_\text{in}$ | $k$ | $p$ | $s$ | $L_\text{out}$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| `Conv2d(3, 16, 3, padding=1)` | 32 | 3 | 1 | 1 | 32 (same) |
| `Conv2d(3, 16, 3)` | 32 | 3 | 0 | 1 | 30 (valid) |
| `Conv2d(3, 16, 5, stride=2, padding=2)` | 32 | 5 | 2 | 2 | 16 (downsample) |
| `Conv2d(3, 16, 7, stride=2, padding=3)` | 224 | 7 | 3 | 2 | 112 (ResNet stem) |

## Related

- [[convolution-operation]] — what padding and stride parametrise
- [[pooling]] — alternative way to downsample
- [[receptive-field]] — affected by both stride and dilation
- [[cnn-architecture]] — typical stride/padding patterns layer by layer

content.newText:
---
name: Receptive field
description: Region of the input image that a particular CNN neuron is connected to (directly or transitively)
type: concept
courses: [Computer-Vision]
source: [16_CNNs.pdf]
created: 2026-04-26
tags: [concept, deep-learning, cnn]
---

# Receptive field

The patch of the **input image** that influences the value of a particular neuron in some deeper layer. As you go deeper into the CNN, the receptive field grows — each neuron sees a larger and larger window of the original image.

This is what gives CNNs their hierarchical perception: shallow neurons see edges, deep neurons see whole objects.

## Single conv layer

A $k \times k$ conv on a layer with receptive field $r$ produces a layer with receptive field $r + (k-1)$. Each application of a $k \times k$ kernel extends the window by $\lfloor k/2 \rfloor$ on each side.

Stack of $L$ identical $3\times 3$ convs (no pooling, $s=1$):

$$\text{RF}_L = 1 + L \cdot (k - 1) = 1 + 2L$$

So a 10-layer 3×3 conv stack sees a $21\times 21$ window. To see all of a $224\times 224$ image purely with conv, you'd need $\sim 100$ layers — too many.

## With pooling and stride

Pooling and strided convs **multiply** the receptive field's growth rate, because subsequent convs operate at lower spatial resolution where each pixel already covers a larger input region.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    p0[("input pixel")] --> c1["3×3 conv → RF 3×3"]
    c1 --> p1["MaxPool 2×2 stride 2"]
    p1 --> c2["3×3 conv → RF 7×7 (in input)"]
    c2 --> p2["MaxPool 2×2 stride 2"]
    p2 --> c3["3×3 conv → RF 15×15 (in input)"]
    c3 --> deeper["..."]
```

After $n$ pool/stride-2 stages, each subsequent conv's $(k-1)$ extension is multiplied by $2^n$ in the input image.

## General recurrence

For a stack of layers each with kernel $k_i$ and stride $s_i$:

$$r_i = r_{i-1} + (k_i - 1) \prod_{j < i} s_j$$

The cumulative product of strides is the "jump" between adjacent neurons — once you've downsampled by 4×, every subsequent conv neuron is 4 input pixels apart.

## Why it matters

- A neuron whose RF doesn't cover the object can't recognise the object. Architectural depth + downsampling exists to make the final classification neurons see the whole image.
- For **segmentation**, you want a large RF *per output pixel* — but also fine spatial resolution. Tension between these two motivates **dilated/atrous convolutions** and **U-Net**'s encoder–decoder structure (lecture 18).
- For **detection**, anchor-based methods place object proposals at every spatial position; the RF must cover at least the object size you want to detect.

## How to enlarge RF

| Option | Cost |
|---|---|
| Stack more conv layers | More parameters, deeper network |
| Increase kernel size (5×5, 7×7) | Quadratic parameter growth |
| Add pooling / strided convs | Loses spatial resolution |
| Dilated/atrous convolution | Cheap; expands RF without losing resolution |

## Effective vs theoretical RF

The formula gives the **theoretical** RF — every input pixel that *could* influence the neuron. In practice the **effective** RF is smaller and roughly Gaussian, with most influence concentrated near the centre. Most pixels in the theoretical RF contribute very little to the final activation.

## Related

- [[convolution-operation]] — primitive that grows RF
- [[pooling]] — the multiplier on RF growth
- [[padding-stride]] — stride enters the RF recurrence
- [[cnn-architecture]] — design choices balance RF vs spatial resolution

---
name: Receptive field
description: Region of the input image that a particular CNN neuron is connected to (directly or transitively)
type: concept
courses: [Computer-Vision]
source: [16_CNNs.pdf]
created: 2026-04-26
tags: [concept, deep-learning, cnn]
---

# Receptive field

The patch of the **input image** that influences the value of a particular neuron in some deeper layer. As you go deeper into the CNN, the receptive field grows — each neuron sees a larger and larger window of the original image.

This is what gives CNNs their hierarchical perception: shallow neurons see edges, deep neurons see whole objects.

## Single conv layer

A $k \times k$ conv on a layer with receptive field $r$ produces a layer with receptive field $r + (k-1)$. Each application of a $k \times k$ kernel extends the window by $\lfloor k/2 \rfloor$ on each side.

Stack of $L$ identical $3\times 3$ convs (no pooling, $s=1$):

$$\text{RF}_L = 1 + L \cdot (k - 1) = 1 + 2L$$

So a 10-layer 3×3 conv stack sees a $21\times 21$ window. To see all of a $224\times 224$ image purely with conv, you'd need $\sim 100$ layers — too many.

## With pooling and stride

Pooling and strided convs **multiply** the receptive field's growth rate, because subsequent convs operate at lower spatial resolution where each pixel already covers a larger input region.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    p0[("input pixel")] --> c1["3×3 conv → RF 3×3"]
    c1 --> p1["MaxPool 2×2 stride 2"]
    p1 --> c2["3×3 conv → RF 7×7 (in input)"]
    c2 --> p2["MaxPool 2×2 stride 2"]
    p2 --> c3["3×3 conv → RF 15×15 (in input)"]
    c3 --> deeper["..."]
```

After $n$ pool/stride-2 stages, each subsequent conv's $(k-1)$ extension is multiplied by $2^n$ in the input image.

## General recurrence

For a stack of layers each with kernel $k_i$ and stride $s_i$:

$$r_i = r_{i-1} + (k_i - 1) \prod_{j < i} s_j$$

The cumulative product of strides is the "jump" between adjacent neurons — once you've downsampled by 4×, every subsequent conv neuron is 4 input pixels apart.

## Why it matters

- A neuron whose RF doesn't cover the object can't recognise the object. Architectural depth + downsampling exists to make the final classification neurons see the whole image.
- For **segmentation**, you want a large RF *per output pixel* — but also fine spatial resolution. Tension between these two motivates **dilated/atrous convolutions** and **U-Net**'s encoder–decoder structure (lecture 18).
- For **detection**, anchor-based methods place object proposals at every spatial position; the RF must cover at least the object size you want to detect.

## How to enlarge RF

| Option | Cost |
|---|---|
| Stack more conv layers | More parameters, deeper network |
| Increase kernel size (5×5, 7×7) | Quadratic parameter growth |
| Add pooling / strided convs | Loses spatial resolution |
| Dilated/atrous convolution | Cheap; expands RF without losing resolution |

## Effective vs theoretical RF

The formula gives the **theoretical** RF — every input pixel that *could* influence the neuron. In practice the **effective** RF is smaller and roughly Gaussian, with most influence concentrated near the centre. Most pixels in the theoretical RF contribute very little to the final activation.

## Related

- [[convolution-operation]] — primitive that grows RF
- [[pooling]] — the multiplier on RF growth
- [[padding-stride]] — stride enters the RF recurrence
- [[cnn-architecture]] — design choices balance RF vs spatial resolution

--- name: Pooling description: Parameter-free downsampling operator (max / average / sum / global) — shrinks feature maps and grows receptive field type: concept courses: [Computer-Vision] source: [16_CNNs.pdf] created: 2026-04-26 tags: [concept, deep-learning, cnn] --- # Pooling A **non-trainable** layer that reduces the spatial size of a feature map by aggregating values within sliding windows. No weights to learn. ## Three local variants | Variant | Window aggregator | Use | |---|---|---| | **Max pool** | $\max$ over window | most common; "did this feature appear *anywhere* in the window?" | | **Average pool** | mean over window | smoother downsample; common in modern nets and as global pool | | **Sum pool** | sum over window | preserves total energy | For a $2\times 2$ pool with stride 2 on a $4\times 4$ input → $2\times 2$ output. Reduces spatial dim by factor 2 in each axis. ```mermaid flowchart LR fmap[("4×4 feature map")] --> mp["MaxPool 2×2"] mp --> small[("2×2 output")] small --> note["activates if any pixel
in the window was active"] ``` ## Why pool 1. **Reduces parameters / compute downstream**: each subsequent conv operates on a smaller map. 2. **Grows the receptive field multiplicatively** (see [[receptive-field]]). 3. **Adds translation invariance**: small shifts in the input produce identical pooled output, because max/avg are insensitive to where in the window the activation sits. 4. **Reduces overfitting**: discards spatial precision that shouldn't matter for classification. The cost: you **lose spatial resolution**. Pooling doesn't store *which* pixel in the window had the max value — bad for segmentation where pixel-level output is needed. For that reason, U-Net etc. either avoid pooling or store pooling indices to undo it (max-unpooling). ## Global pooling `AdaptiveAvgPool2d(1)` / `AdaptiveMaxPool2d(1)` reduces the entire spatial map to a $1\times 1\times C$ vector — one number per channel. ```python nn.AdaptiveAvgPool2d(output_size=1) # H×W → 1×1 ``` Used at the end of the convolutional trunk, **before** the fully connected classifier. Replaces the older "flatten the entire feature map → huge FC layer" pattern with a much smaller `Linear(C, num_classes)` — drastically fewer parameters, and works at any input resolution. ## In PyTorch ```python nn.MaxPool2d(kernel_size, stride=None, padding=0, dilation=1, return_indices=False, ceil_mode=False) nn.AvgPool2d(kernel_size, stride=None, padding=0, ceil_mode=False, count_include_pad=True, divisor_override=None) nn.AdaptiveAvgPool2d(output_size=1) # global pooling ``` `stride=None` defaults to `kernel_size` (non-overlapping windows). ## Pool vs strided conv Both halve spatial dim. Strided conv has parameters and can *learn* the downsampling pattern; pooling is parameter-free. Modern architectures (ResNet, ViT) often replace pooling with strided conv except for the global pool at the end. ## Related - [[convolution-operation]] — what feeds the pool - [[receptive-field]] — pool multiplies its growth rate - [[feature-map]] — the input - [[cnn-architecture]] — typical placement

--- name: CNN architecture description: Standard pattern of a CNN classifier — repeating Conv+BN+ReLU+Pool blocks, then GlobalPool, Flatten, FC head type: concept courses: [Computer-Vision] source: [16_CNNs.pdf] created: 2026-04-26 tags: [concept, deep-learning, cnn, architecture] --- # CNN architecture The canonical CNN classifier is a **convolutional trunk** (feature extractor) followed by a **classifier head** (fully connected layers). ## The pattern ```mermaid flowchart LR in([Input image
3×H×W]) --> b1 subgraph b1[Block 1] c1[Conv 3×3] --> bn1[BatchNorm] --> r1[ReLU] --> p1[MaxPool 2×2] end b1 --> b2 subgraph b2[Block 2] c2[Conv 3×3] --> bn2[BatchNorm] --> r2[ReLU] --> p2[MaxPool 2×2] end b2 --> b3 subgraph b3[Block N] cN[Conv 3×3] --> bnN[BatchNorm] --> rN[ReLU] --> pN[MaxPool 2×2] end b3 --> gap[Global AvgPool] gap --> flat[Flatten] flat --> head subgraph head[Classifier head] fc1[Linear C→512] --> r["ReLU + Dropout"] --> fc2[Linear 512→num_classes] end head --> out([Logits]) ``` Three layer **types**, three roles: | Layer | Role | |---|---| | **Convolutional** (+ BatchNorm + ReLU) | learns translation-equivariant features | | **Pooling** | downsamples, grows receptive field, adds translation invariance | | **Fully connected** | maps the final feature vector to class scores | ## Channel growth A standard heuristic: **double channels each time you halve spatial size**. Keeps total work per layer roughly constant. | Stage | Spatial | Channels | |---|---|---| | Input | $32\times 32$ | 3 | | Block 1 | $16\times 16$ | 16 | | Block 2 | $8\times 8$ | 32 | | Block 3 | $4\times 4$ | 64 | | GAP | $1\times 1$ | 64 | ## SimpleCNN reference (slide 21–22) ```python class SimpleCNN(nn.Module): def __init__(self, num_classes=10, channels=(3, 16, 32, 64), kernel_size=3, pool_size=2): super().__init__() layers = [] for i in range(len(channels) - 1): layers.extend([ nn.Conv2d(channels[i], channels[i+1], kernel_size, padding=1), nn.BatchNorm2d(channels[i+1]), nn.ReLU(), nn.MaxPool2d(pool_size), ]) layers.append(nn.AdaptiveAvgPool2d(1)) self.features = nn.ModuleList(layers) self.classifier = nn.Sequential( nn.Linear(channels[-1], 512), nn.ReLU(), nn.Dropout(0.5), nn.Linear(512, num_classes), ) def forward(self, x): for layer in self.features: x = layer(x) x = x.flatten(start_dim=1) return self.classifier(x) ``` ## Order of ops in a block Three near-equivalent options seen in the wild; "Conv → BN → ReLU" is the most common: | Pattern | Where | |---|---| | Conv → BN → ReLU → Pool | classic CNN, this lecture | | Conv → ReLU → BN | older pre-2015 | | BN → ReLU → Conv (pre-activation) | ResNet v2 | ## Variants of the same pattern - **VGG**: lots of small 3×3 convs, max-pool every 2 blocks, large FC head. - **ResNet**: same pattern + [[residual-connection]] across each pair of convs. - **MobileNet / EfficientNet**: each conv replaced by [[depthwise-separable-conv]] for efficiency. - **U-Net** (lecture 18): symmetrical encoder/decoder with skip connections from encoder to decoder — same blocks, different topology. ## Why GAP + small FC instead of Flatten + huge FC Flatten on a $7\times 7\times 512$ feature map produces a 25 088-vector → an FC layer to 1000 classes is 25M parameters. Global Avg Pool collapses spatial to $512$ first → FC is 0.5M parameters. Same accuracy, vastly fewer parameters, and the network now works on **any input resolution** (because GAP doesn't care about the spatial size). ## Related - [[convolution-operation]], [[pooling]], [[batch-normalization]] — building blocks - [[activation-functions]] — ReLU between layers - [[receptive-field]], [[feature-map]] — what each block produces - [[nn-module]] — how to compose this in PyTorch - [[residual-connection]] — modern modification (lecture 17)

--- name: Data augmentation description: Synthetic training-time transformations (flip, rotate, crop, jitter) that multiply effective dataset size type: concept courses: [Computer-Vision] source: [16_CNNs.pdf] created: 2026-04-26 tags: [concept, deep-learning, regularisation] --- # Data augmentation Apply random label-preserving transformations to training images on the fly. Each epoch, the model sees a slightly different version of every example — effectively turning $N$ training images into a much larger virtual dataset. A free regulariser, often the difference between catastrophic overfitting and a useful model. ## Common transformations | Transform | Use | |---|---| | **Horizontal flip** | symmetry along the vertical axis (cats are left-right symmetric) | | **Vertical flip** | rare — only valid when up/down symmetry holds (medical, microscopy) | | **Rotation** ±15°, ±45° | object orientation isn't fixed | | **Crop / random crop** | object location isn't fixed; also enables larger images during training | | **Brightness / contrast / hue jitter** | robustness to lighting changes | | **Gaussian blur** | robustness to focus / motion blur | | **Grayscale** | colour shouldn't be the only cue | | **Noise** | input robustness | | **CutMix / Mixup** | mix two images / labels — strong regulariser for classification | ## What it does — and what it doesn't ✅ Improves generalisation when the augmented samples remain plausible variations of real data. ❌ Doesn't help when augmented samples are unrealistic (e.g. flipping a "left arrow" road sign produces a "right arrow" — wrong label). ❌ Doesn't add information that wasn't already implicit. It encodes **invariances** (rotation, flip, brightness) that you've decided the task should be invariant to. ## In PyTorch (slide 24) ```python import torch from torchvision.transforms import v2 from torchvision.datasets import CIFAR10 preprocess = v2.Compose([ v2.ToTensor(), v2.Resize((64, 64)), v2.ToDtype(torch.float32, scale=True), # 0..255 → 0..1 ]) augment = v2.Compose([ v2.RandomHorizontalFlip(p=0.5), v2.RandomRotation(degrees=15), ]) train = CIFAR10(root="/tmp/train", train=True, download=True, transform=v2.Compose([preprocess, augment])) val = CIFAR10(root="/tmp/val", train=False, download=True, transform=preprocess) # NO augmentation on val/test ``` ## Critical rule: training only Augmentation runs **only on the training set**. Validation/test must use the same deterministic preprocessing (resize + normalize) so metrics are reproducible and reflect real-world performance. ```mermaid flowchart LR img[("Raw image")] --> tr{"Train or val/test?"} tr -->|train| pre1["preprocess
(resize, normalize)"] --> aug["augment
(flip, rotate, jitter)"] --> mtrain["model"] tr -->|"val / test"| pre2["preprocess
(resize, normalize)
NO augmentation"] --> mval["model"] ``` ## Strength tuning - Light augmentation (basic flip + crop) almost always helps. - Heavy augmentation (RandAugment, AutoAugment) helps when data is scarce; can hurt when there's lots of data. - Adversarial / weird augmentations: only when validated against task semantics. ## Related - [[dropout]] — alternative regulariser; complementary - [[batch-normalization]] — also has a regularising side effect; combine all three thoughtfully - [[loss-functions]] — augmentation is the main reason train/val loss diverge less

--- name: Computer Vision tasks description: Taxonomy — classification, classification + localisation, object detection, instance segmentation type: concept courses: [Computer-Vision] source: [16_CNNs.pdf] created: 2026-04-26 tags: [concept, deep-learning, computer-vision, taxonomy] --- # Computer Vision tasks The four basic supervised problems studied in modern computer vision, ordered by output complexity. | Task | Input | Output | One/many objects | Spatial granularity | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Classification** | image | 1 categorical label | one | none | | **Classification + Localisation** | image | 1 categorical + 4 floats (bbox) | one | bbox | | **Object Detection** | image | $N \times$ (categorical + 4 floats) | many | per-bbox | | **Instance Segmentation** | image | per-pixel class label, per-instance | many | per-pixel | (Two related tasks not covered above: **semantic segmentation** = per-pixel class without distinguishing instances; **panoptic** = both.) ## Decision tree ```mermaid flowchart TD img[("Image input")] --> q1{One object
or many?} q1 -->|one| q2{Need bbox
output?} q1 -->|many| q3{Bbox or
per-pixel?} q2 -->|"label only"| cls["Classification
1 categorical
(ResNet, ConvNeXt, ViT)"] q2 -->|"label + bbox"| loc["Classification + Localisation
classifier + regression head"] q3 -->|"bboxes"| det["Object Detection
(YOLO, Faster R-CNN, DETR)"] q3 -->|"per-pixel mask"| seg["Instance Segmentation
(U-Net, Mask R-CNN, MaskFormer, SAM)"] ``` ## Representative architectures (slide 5) | Task | CNN-era | ViT-era | |---|---|---| | Classification | ResNet, EfficientNet, ConvNeXt | ViT, Swin Transformer | | Localisation | classifier + regression head | same | | Detection | YOLO, Faster R-CNN | DETR / RT-DETR | | Instance seg. | U-Net, Mask R-CNN | MaskFormer, SAM | CNNs were SOTA until ~2021; transformers now lead on most large-scale benchmarks but require significantly more data. CNNs remain competitive and easier to train on smaller datasets. ## Loss functions per task | Task | Typical loss(es) | |---|---| | Classification | categorical cross-entropy ([[loss-functions]]) | | Localisation | cross-entropy + smooth-L1 / IoU loss for the bbox | | Detection | classification CE + bbox regression + objectness; matched against anchors or queries | | Segmentation | per-pixel cross-entropy or Dice / Tversky loss | ## Related - [[cnn-architecture]] — backbone for most of these tasks - [[loss-functions]] — task-specific objectives - [[non-maximum-suppression]] — used in detection to deduplicate boxes - Lecture 18 — segmentation and object detection in detail

--- name: Computer Vision tasks description: Taxonomy — classification, classification + localisation, object detection, instance segmentation type: concept courses: [Computer-Vision] source: [16_CNNs.pdf] created: 2026-04-26 tags: [concept, deep-learning, computer-vision, taxonomy] --- # Computer Vision tasks The four basic supervised problems studied in modern computer vision, ordered by output complexity. | Task | Input | Output | One/many objects | Spatial granularity | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Classification** | image | 1 categorical label | one | none | | **Classification + Localisation** | image | 1 categorical + 4 floats (bbox) | one | bbox | | **Object Detection** | image | $N \times$ (categorical + 4 floats) | many | per-bbox | | **Instance Segmentation** | image | per-pixel class label, per-instance | many | per-pixel | (Two related tasks not covered above: **semantic segmentation** = per-pixel class without distinguishing instances; **panoptic** = both.) ## Decision tree ```mermaid flowchart TD img[("Image input")] --> q1{One object
or many?} q1 -->|one| q2{Need bbox
output?} q1 -->|many| q3{Bbox or
per-pixel?} q2 -->|"label only"| cls["Classification
1 categorical
(ResNet, ConvNeXt, ViT)"] q2 -->|"label + bbox"| loc["Classification + Localisation
classifier + regression head"] q3 -->|"bboxes"| det["Object Detection
(YOLO, Faster R-CNN, DETR)"] q3 -->|"per-pixel mask"| seg["Instance Segmentation
(U-Net, Mask R-CNN, MaskFormer, SAM)"] ``` ## Representative architectures (slide 5) | Task | CNN-era | ViT-era | |---|---|---| | Classification | ResNet, EfficientNet, ConvNeXt | ViT, Swin Transformer | | Localisation | classifier + regression head | same | | Detection | YOLO, Faster R-CNN | DETR / RT-DETR | | Instance seg. | U-Net, Mask R-CNN | MaskFormer, SAM | CNNs were SOTA until ~2021; transformers now lead on most large-scale benchmarks but require significantly more data. CNNs remain competitive and easier to train on smaller datasets. ## Loss functions per task | Task | Typical loss(es) | |---|---| | Classification | categorical cross-entropy ([[loss-functions]]) | | Localisation | cross-entropy + smooth-L1 / IoU loss for the bbox | | Detection | classification CE + bbox regression + objectness; matched against anchors or queries | | Segmentation | per-pixel cross-entropy or Dice / Tversky loss | ## Related - [[cnn-architecture]] — backbone for most of these tasks - [[loss-functions]] — task-specific objectives - [[non-maximum-suppression]] — used in detection to deduplicate boxes - Lecture 18 — segmentation and object detection in detail

--- name: Depthwise separable convolution description: Factor a standard conv into a per-channel spatial conv + a 1×1 cross-channel conv — drops parameters/FLOPs by ~10× type: concept courses: [Computer-Vision] source: [16_CNNs.pdf] created: 2026-04-26 tags: [concept, deep-learning, cnn, efficiency] --- # Depthwise separable convolution A factorisation of standard convolution into two simpler operations: 1. **Depthwise convolution** — a $K\times K$ kernel applied to **each input channel independently** (no mixing across channels). 2. **Pointwise convolution** — a $1\times 1$ conv that mixes channels into the desired number of output channels. Used by MobileNet, EfficientNet, Xception. Trades a small amount of accuracy for ~10× fewer parameters and FLOPs. ## Standard vs separable ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Std[Standard conv] si["C_in × H × W"] --> sk["C_out filters,
each K×K×C_in"] sk --> so["C_out × H' × W'"] end subgraph DW[Depthwise separable] di["C_in × H × W"] --> dwc["Depthwise:
C_in filters,
each K×K×1"] dwc --> dwo["C_in × H' × W'"] dwo --> pwc["Pointwise 1×1×C_in,
C_out filters"] pwc --> dso["C_out × H' × W'"] end ``` ## Parameter count | Method | Parameters | FLOPs (per output spatial location) | |---|---|---| | Standard $K\times K\times C_\text{in} \to C_\text{out}$ | $K^2 C_\text{in} C_\text{out}$ | $K^2 C_\text{in} C_\text{out}$ | | Depthwise + Pointwise | $K^2 C_\text{in} + C_\text{in} C_\text{out}$ | $K^2 C_\text{in} + C_\text{in} C_\text{out}$ | For typical $K=3$, $C_\text{in} = C_\text{out} = 256$: - Standard: $9 \cdot 256 \cdot 256 \approx 590\,000$ params. - Separable: $9 \cdot 256 + 256 \cdot 256 \approx 68\,000$ params. About 8.7× fewer. ## Why it works Standard convolution mixes **two** kinds of structure simultaneously: - spatial structure (within a $K\times K$ window), - channel structure (across all input channels). Separable convolution **decouples** them: depthwise handles spatial, pointwise handles channels. In practice, factoring makes a more parameter-efficient model with marginal accuracy loss. ## In PyTorch A depthwise conv is just a `Conv2d` with `groups=in_channels` (each group has 1 input channel and 1 output channel): ```python # Depthwise then pointwise nn.Conv2d(C_in, C_in, kernel_size=3, padding=1, groups=C_in, bias=False), nn.BatchNorm2d(C_in), nn.ReLU(), nn.Conv2d(C_in, C_out, kernel_size=1, bias=False), nn.BatchNorm2d(C_out), nn.ReLU(), ``` This pattern is the **inverted residual block** when wrapped with a residual connection — the heart of MobileNetV2. ## When to use - Mobile / edge deployment. - Real-time inference (any FPS-bound task). - Initial layers of larger models for speed (e.g. EfficientNet stem). When you have abundant compute, plain conv tends to give a small accuracy edge. ## Related - [[convolution-operation]] — what gets factored - [[cnn-architecture]] — typical placement - [[batch-normalization]] — usually placed between depthwise and pointwise

---
tags: [flashcards, review]
course: Computer-Vision
lecture: 16
source: 16_CNNs.pdf
created: 2026-04-26

# Lecture 16 — CNNs (flashcards)

Name the four classical computer-vision tasks, ordered by output complexity.
?
1) Classification (1 categorical), 2) Classification + Localisation (1 categorical + 4-float bbox), 3) Object Detection ($N$ × (categorical + 4 floats)), 4) Instance Segmentation (per-pixel class).

Why does flattening an image and feeding it to an MLP / SVM / random forest work poorly?
?
Flattening destroys spatial relationships — adjacency between pixels is lost. The model has to re-learn "pixel $(i,j)$ is next to pixel $(i,j+1)$" from scratch, wasting parameters and data.

What two inductive biases does a CNN encode, and how does each show up in the architecture?
?
**Locality** → small kernels (e.g. $3\times 3$) that only look at a local window. **Translation invariance** → the same kernel weights are reused at every spatial position (parameter sharing).

Differentiate translation **equivariance** and translation **invariance** in a CNN.
?
Convolution is *equivariant*: a shift in the input produces the same shift in the feature map. After global pooling collapses spatial dims, the final classifier output is *invariant* — same prediction regardless of where the object appears.

Write the 2D convolution formula for a single input channel.
?
$y(i, j) = \sum_{m, n} x(i-m,\, j-n)\, w(m, n) + b$, summed over $-H \le m \le H$, $-W \le n \le W$.

Why does it not matter that NN libraries actually compute cross-correlation rather than true convolution?
?
The kernel is **learned**. True convolution and cross-correlation parametrise the same family of functions — training a cross-correlation layer with kernel $w$ is equivalent to training a true-convolution layer with the flipped kernel.

Output-shape formula for a 1D conv layer with input $L_\text{in}$, padding $p$, kernel $k$, stride $s$, dilation $d$.
?
$L_\text{out} = \lfloor (L_\text{in} + 2p - d(k-1) - 1)/s + 1 \rfloor$.

What padding gives "same" output spatial size for stride 1, odd kernel $k$?
?
$p = (k-1)/2$. For $k=3$ → $p=1$; for $k=5$ → $p=2$.

How many parameters does a `Conv2d(C_in, C_out, kernel_size=K)` layer have (with bias)?
?
$K^2 \cdot C_\text{in} \cdot C_\text{out} + C_\text{out}$. The $C_\text{out}$ filters each have shape $K\times K\times C_\text{in}$, plus one bias per output channel.

Define the receptive field of a CNN neuron in one sentence.
?
The region of the input image whose pixels can influence that neuron's value (directly or transitively through earlier layers).

If you stack $L$ layers of $3\times 3$ convs (stride 1, no pooling), what's the receptive field?
?
$1 + 2L$. Each $3\times 3$ conv extends the window by $\lfloor k/2 \rfloor = 1$ pixel on each side.

Why does pooling grow the receptive field faster than stacking convs alone?
?
After a 2× pool, every subsequent conv neuron is 2 input pixels apart. The receptive-field extension $(k-1)$ from each new conv is multiplied by the cumulative product of strides — pooling multiplies the growth rate.

Compare max-pool, average-pool, and sum-pool in one sentence each.
?
Max-pool: outputs the strongest activation in the window — "did the feature appear *anywhere*?". Avg-pool: smoothed downsample. Sum-pool: preserves total activation energy.

Why does Global Average Pool + small FC outperform Flatten + huge FC in modern CNNs?
?
GAP first collapses $H\times W \times C$ to $C$, so the classifier is `Linear(C, num_classes)` — far fewer parameters. Bonus: the network now works at any input resolution, since GAP doesn't depend on spatial size.

Standard CNN block has four ops in what order?
?
Conv → BatchNorm → ReLU → MaxPool. Pre-activation variants (ResNet v2) reorder to BN → ReLU → Conv → Pool.

Heuristic for channel growth across the convolutional trunk.
?
Double the number of channels every time you halve the spatial dimensions. Keeps total per-layer compute roughly constant.

Standard conv parameter cost vs. depthwise-separable conv ($K\times K$, $C_\text{in}\to C_\text{out}$).
?
Standard: $K^2 \cdot C_\text{in} \cdot C_\text{out}$. Depthwise separable: $K^2 \cdot C_\text{in} + C_\text{in} \cdot C_\text{out}$. Roughly 8–10× cheaper for typical $K=3$ and $C\sim 256$.

How is a depthwise conv expressed in PyTorch?
?
`nn.Conv2d(C_in, C_in, kernel_size=K, groups=C_in)` — `groups=in_channels` makes each filter operate on a single input channel.

What's the difference between a strided conv and a pool layer for downsampling?
?
Strided conv has learnable parameters and can learn the downsampling pattern. Pool is parameter-free and applies a fixed reduction (max/avg). Modern architectures often replace pooling with strided conv except for the final global pool.

State the rule about applying data augmentation to train vs val/test sets.
?
Augmentation is **training-only**. Val/test must use the same deterministic preprocessing (resize, normalize) so metrics are reproducible and reflect real performance.

Name three augmentations that almost always help on natural-image classification.
?
RandomHorizontalFlip, RandomCrop (after resize-up), and slight ColorJitter / brightness perturbation. Random ±15° rotation is also widely useful.

Why is vertical flip usually a bad augmentation for natural images?
?
Most natural scenes aren't up/down symmetric — a flipped sky/ground destroys label semantics. Use vertical flip only when up/down symmetry holds (microscopy, top-down maps).

Architecturally, when does a transformer (ViT) tend to beat a CNN, and when does the CNN still win?
?
ViT wins with very large datasets and large compute budgets. CNN wins on small/medium datasets thanks to its locality + translation-invariance inductive bias, which acts as a free regulariser.

Why is a $1\times 1$ convolution useful even though it doesn't aggregate spatial information?
?
It mixes channels at each spatial location. Used to change the channel count cheaply (bottleneck blocks), implement the pointwise step of depthwise-separable conv, and add per-pixel non-linear capacity without expanding the receptive field.

Name three architectural changes that help a CNN see "long-range" structure (large receptive field) without going extremely deep.
?
1) Stride-2 convs / pooling (multiply RF growth). 2) Dilated/atrous convolutions (expand RF without losing resolution or adding params). 3) Larger kernels (e.g. $7\times 7$ stem). Also: skip connections + depth.

content.text: ### Lecture 17 — Advanced architectures

### Lecture 17 — Advanced architectures file plan **Lecture note:** `lecture-17-advanced-architectures.md` **New concepts (12):** `vggnet`, `inception-module`, `densenet`, `squeeze-excitation`, `self-attention`, `multi-head-attention`, `positional-encoding`, `transformer-encoder`, `vision-transformer`, `swin-transformer`, `convnext`, `transfer-learning` **Extended concepts (1):** `residual-connection` (add ResNet macro-architecture) **Flashcards:** ~30 **Flagship Mermaids:** ResNet-18 macro (slide 6–7): ```mermaid flowchart LR in([224×224×3]) --> stem[7×7 Conv stride 2] stem --> bn[BN + ReLU] bn --> mp[3×3 MaxPool stride 2] mp --> s1[Stage 1: ×2 residual blocks
64 ch] s1 --> s2[Stage 2: ×2 residual blocks
128 ch, downsample 1st] s2 --> s3[Stage 3: ×2 residual blocks
256 ch, downsample 1st] s3 --> s4[Stage 4: ×2 residual blocks
512 ch, downsample 1st] s4 --> gap[Global AvgPool] gap --> fc[FC 1000] fc --> out([logits]) ``` Scaled dot-product attention (slide 18, 26): ```mermaid flowchart LR X[Input X
L×d] --> wq[× W^Q] X --> wk[× W^K] X --> wv[× W^V] wq --> Q[Q L×d_k] wk --> K[K L×d_k] wv --> V[V L×d_v] Q --> qk["Q · Kᵀ"] K --> qk qk --> scl["÷ √d_k"] scl --> sm["softmax"] sm --> A["A L×L
(attention)"] A --> mul["A · V"] V --> mul mul --> Z([Output Z
L×d_v]) ``` ViT (slide 37): ```mermaid flowchart LR img[Image H×W×3] --> patch[Patchify P×P → N patches] patch --> embed[Linear projection
each patch → d-vector] embed --> cls["+ [CLS] token"] cls --> pos["+ positional embedding"] pos --> enc["Transformer Encoder ×L
(MHA + MLP, pre-norm, residual)"] enc --> sel["take [CLS] output"] sel --> mlp[MLP head] mlp --> logits([class logits]) ``` Writing files now.

content.newText:
--- tags: [lecture] course: Computer-Vision source: 17_advanced_architectures(1).pdf lecture: 17 created: 2026-04-26 --- # Lecture 17 — Advanced architectures ## Overview A timeline of the architectural ideas that drove ImageNet from 8 layers (AlexNet 2012) to today's vision transformers, plus the practical machinery (transfer learning) needed to use them. 1. **ImageNet timeline** — AlexNet → VGG → Inception → ResNet → SENet → ViT → ConvNeXt. 2. **VGG (2013)** — radical simplification: blocks of 3×3 convs only. 3. **Inception (2014)** — multi-scale parallel branches in a single block; auxiliary losses (now obsolete); GAP instead of huge FC. 4. **ResNet (2015)** — [[residual-connection]] makes 100+-layer training stable. Two block forms (no downsample / with $1\times 1$ projection). 5. **DenseNet (2016)** — every layer connected to every later layer; concatenation-based. 6. **SENet (2017)** — channel attention — squeeze (global pool) + excite (per-channel sigmoid weights). 7. **Transformers (2017)** — attention is all you need. Encoder/decoder; ViT uses encoder only. 8. **Self-attention** — Q/K/V from learned projections; scaled dot-product; multi-head. 9. **Positional encoding** — sinusoidal, learned absolute, relative, rotary (RoPE). 10. **Transformer block** — MHA + MLP with residual + layer norm; pre-norm vs post-norm. 11. **ViT (2020)** — image as sequence of patches; CLS token or pooling for classification. 12. **Swin (2021)** — windowed attention with shifts; CNN-like hierarchy reintroduced. 13. **ConvNeXt (2022)** — modernised CNN matching ViT performance: $7\times 7$ depthwise convs, layer norm, GELU. 14. **Transfer learning** — feature extraction (freeze backbone) and fine-tuning (low LR on backbone, normal LR on head). ## Key concepts - [[vggnet]] — uniform 3×3-conv blocks - [[inception-module]] — parallel branches at multiple kernel sizes - [[residual-connection]] — skip-add; backbone of ResNet (extended this lecture) - [[densenet]] — feature reuse via concatenation - [[squeeze-excitation]] — channel attention - [[self-attention]] — scaled dot-product Q/K/V - [[multi-head-attention]] — many self-attention heads in parallel - [[positional-encoding]] — restoring order info into attention - [[transformer-encoder]] — full block (MHA + MLP + residual + LN) - [[vision-transformer]] — patches as tokens - [[swin-transformer]] — shifted-window attention, hierarchical - [[convnext]] — modernised CNN - [[transfer-learning]] — feature extraction, fine-tuning, freezing in PyTorch ## Equations ### Self-attention update (slides 17, 24) $$Z_t^{l+1} = \sum_{t' = 1}^{T} a^l_{t, t'} \cdot V^l_{t'}$$ where the attention weights $a^l_{t, t'}$ come from the scaled dot-product: $$\text{Attention}(Q, K, V) = \mathrm{softmax}\!\left(\frac{Q K^\top}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right) V$$ with $Q = X W^Q$, $K = X W^K$, $V = X W^V$ and $W^Q, W^K \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times d_k}$, $W^V \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times d_v}$. ### Multi-head attention $$\mathrm{MHA}(X) = \mathrm{Concat}(\text{head}_1, \ldots, \text{head}_h)\, W^O,\qquad \text{head}_i = \text{Attention}(X W^Q_i, X W^K_i, X W^V_i)$$ ### Sinusoidal positional encoding (slide 31) $$\mathrm{PE}_{(\text{pos}, 2i)} = \sin\!\left(\frac{\text{pos}}{10000^{2i/d}}\right),\qquad \mathrm{PE}_{(\text{pos}, 2i+1)} = \cos\!\left(\frac{\text{pos}}{10000^{2i/d}}\right)$$ Added to each token embedding before the first transformer block. ### Relative positional bias (slide 33) $$\mathrm{Attention}(i, j) = \frac{Q_i K_j^\top}{\sqrt{d_k}} + B_{i, j}$$ $B_{i,j}$ depends only on the *distance* between positions, not absolute index — gives translation equivariance. ### ResNet residual block Without downsampling: $\text{out} = \mathrm{ReLU}(x + \text{block}(x))$ With downsampling: $\text{out} = \mathrm{ReLU}(\text{Conv}_{1\times 1}(x) + \text{block}(x))$ ## Diagrams ### ImageNet timeline (slide 2) ```mermaid flowchart LR a["2012
AlexNet
8 layers"] --> b["2013
ZNet / VGG
16-19 layers, 3×3 only"] b --> c["2014
Inception
parallel branches"] c --> d["2015
ResNet
100+ layers, residual"] d --> e["2016
DenseNet, ResNet variants"] e --> f["2017
SENet
channel attention"] f --> g["2020
ViT
patches as tokens"] g --> h["2021
Swin
windowed attention"] h --> i["2022
ConvNeXt
CNN strikes back"] ``` ### VGG block (slide 3) ```mermaid flowchart LR in([224×224×3]) --> b1["Block 1: 2× Conv 3×3, 64 ch"] b1 --> p1[MaxPool 2×2] p1 --> b2["Block 2: 2× Conv 3×3, 128 ch"] b2 --> p2[MaxPool 2×2] p2 --> b3["Block 3: 3× Conv 3×3, 256 ch"] b3 --> p3[MaxPool 2×2] p3 --> b4["Block 4: 3× Conv 3×3, 512 ch"] b4 --> p4[MaxPool 2×2] p4 --> b5["Block 5: 3× Conv 3×3, 512 ch"] b5 --> p5[MaxPool 2×2] p5 --> fc6["FC 4096"] fc6 --> fc7["FC 4096"] fc7 --> fc8["FC 1000"] fc8 --> out([softmax]) ``` ### Naive Inception module (slide 5) ```mermaid flowchart TD in[Previous layer] --> b1[1×1 conv] in --> b2[3×3 conv] in --> b3[5×5 conv] in --> b4[3×3 max pool] b1 --> cat[Channel concat] b2 --> cat b3 --> cat b4 --> cat cat --> next[Next layer] ``` The $1\times 1$ convs in the actual Inception v1 also appear *inside* the 3×3 and 5×5 branches as bottlenecks to reduce channel count before the expensive convolution. ### ResNet macro-architecture (slides 6–7) ```mermaid flowchart LR in([224×224×3]) --> stem[7×7 Conv stride 2] stem --> bn[BN + ReLU] bn --> mp[3×3 MaxPool stride 2] mp --> s1["Stage 1: 2× residual
64 ch"] s1 --> s2["Stage 2: 2× residual
128 ch, downsample 1st"] s2 --> s3["Stage 3: 2× residual
256 ch, downsample 1st"] s3 --> s4["Stage 4: 2× residual
512 ch, downsample 1st"] s4 --> gap[Global AvgPool] gap --> fc[FC 1000] fc --> out([logits]) ``` ### Two ResNet block forms (slide 7) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph A[No downsample] x1[x] --> c1a[Conv 3×3] c1a --> bn1a[BN + ReLU] bn1a --> c2a[Conv 3×3] c2a --> bn2a[BN] bn2a --> add1((+)) x1 -->|"identity"| add1 add1 --> r1[ReLU] r1 --> y1[y] end subgraph B[With downsample] x2[x] --> c1b[Conv 3×3 stride 2] c1b --> bn1b[BN + ReLU] bn1b --> c2b[Conv 3×3] c2b --> bn2b[BN] bn2b --> add2((+)) x2 -->|"1×1 Conv stride 2"| add2 add2 --> r2[ReLU] r2 --> y2[y] end ``` ### DenseNet connectivity (slide 8) ```mermaid flowchart LR x0[x_0] --> h1[H_1] h1 --> x1[x_1] x0 --> x1 x1 --> h2[H_2] h2 --> x2[x_2] x0 --> x2 x1 --> x2 x2 --> h3[H_3] h3 --> x3[x_3] x0 --> x3 x1 --> x3 x2 --> x3 ``` Each $H_i$ takes the **concatenation** of all earlier feature maps. Maximum feature reuse → fewer parameters, but memory grows quadratically with depth. ### SENet "squeeze-and-excitation" (slide 9) ```mermaid flowchart LR fmap["feature map H×W×C"] --> sq["Squeeze:
Global Avg Pool
→ 1×1×C"] sq --> mlp["FC → ReLU → FC → Sigmoid
→ 1×1×C weights"] mlp --> ex["Excite:
multiply weights
per channel"] fmap --> ex ex --> out["recalibrated H×W×C"] ``` A learned per-channel gating: *which channels matter most for this input?* Plug-in module — adds minor parameters, frequently improves accuracy. ### Scaled dot-product attention pipeline (slides 18, 26) ```mermaid flowchart LR X["Input X
L×d"] --> wq["× W^Q"] X --> wk["× W^K"] X --> wv["× W^V"] wq --> Q["Q L×d_k"] wk --> K["K L×d_k"] wv --> V["V L×d_v"] Q --> qk["Q · Kᵀ"] K --> qk qk --> scl["÷ √d_k"] scl --> sm["softmax (row-wise)"] sm --> A["A L×L
(attention matrix)"] A --> mul["A · V"] V --> mul mul --> Z(["Output Z
L×d_v"]) ``` Memory cost: $O(L^2)$ for the attention matrix → quadratic in sequence length. ### Multi-head attention summary (slide 29) ```mermaid flowchart LR X["X (L×d)"] --> head1["Head 1: Q/K/V → Z₀
(L×d/h)"] X --> head2["Head 2: Q/K/V → Z₁"] X --> headh["Head h: Q/K/V → Z_h-1"] head1 --> cat["Concat (L×d)"] head2 --> cat headh --> cat cat --> proj["× W^O"] proj --> Z(["Z (L×d)"]) ``` $h$ heads each ask a different "question". Concatenated outputs are projected back to the model dimension. ### Pre-norm vs post-norm transformer block (slide 35–36) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Post["Post-norm (original 2017)"] i1[Input] --> a1[Attention] a1 --> add1((+)) i1 --> add1 add1 --> ln1[LayerNorm] ln1 --> m1[MLP] m1 --> add2((+)) ln1 --> add2 add2 --> ln2[LayerNorm] ln2 --> o1[Output] end subgraph Pre["Pre-norm (modern default)"] i2[Input] --> ln3[LayerNorm] ln3 --> a2[Attention] a2 --> add3((+)) i2 --> add3 add3 --> ln4[LayerNorm] ln4 --> m2[MLP] m2 --> add4((+)) add3 --> add4 add4 --> o2[Output] end ``` Pre-norm trains more stably for very deep transformers and is now standard. ### Vision Transformer (slide 37) ```mermaid flowchart LR img["Image H×W×3"] --> patch["Patchify P×P
→ N patches"] patch --> embed["Linear projection
each patch → d-vector"] embed --> cls["prepend [CLS] token"] cls --> pos["+ positional embedding"] pos --> enc["Transformer Encoder × L
(MHA + MLP, pre-norm, residual)"] enc --> sel["take [CLS] output
(or pool all tokens)"] sel --> mlp[MLP head] mlp --> logits([class logits]) ``` ### Transfer-learning recipes (slides 42–46) ```mermaid flowchart LR pretrained["Pretrained model
(ImageNet)"] --> q{Recipe} q -->|"feature extraction"| fe["Freeze backbone
train new head only
(low data, distant domain)"] q -->|"fine-tuning"| ft["Replace head
unfreeze (some) backbone
train at LOW learning rate
(more data, closer domain)"] fe --> tgt["Target task"] ft --> tgt ``` ## Open questions - ConvNeXt closed the CNN-vs-ViT gap by porting ViT tricks into a CNN. Did the architectural advantage of attention come from (a) the global receptive field, (b) the layer norm + GELU, (c) better training recipes, or (d) something else? - Self-attention is $O(L^2)$ memory. What's the simplest practical fix for very long sequences (long-context LLMs, high-res images)? — windowed attention (Swin), linearised attention, FlashAttention, sparse patterns. - When does feature extraction beat fine-tuning, and vice versa? ## Connections to other lectures - Lecture 16 [[cnn-architecture]] is the substrate every CNN block here builds on. - [[batch-normalization]] (lecture 15) is replaced by **LayerNorm** in transformers and ConvNeXt — why? Because batch statistics misbehave on small or variable-length batches. - [[residual-connection]] (lecture 15) appears in every block from ResNet onwards, including transformers. - Lectures 18–20 will build *on top of* these architectures (segmentation, self-supervised pretraining, generative).

---
name: VGGNet
description: 2013 CNN architecture using stacked 3×3 conv blocks; first to demonstrate that depth + uniform small kernels work
type: concept
courses: [Computer-Vision]
source: [17_advanced_architectures(1).pdf]
created: 2026-04-26
tags: [concept, deep-learning, cnn, architecture, history]
---

# VGGNet

A 2013/2014 ImageNet competitor (Simonyan & Zisserman, Oxford **V**isual **G**eometry **G**roup). Its central architectural claim: **stack many small $3\times 3$ convolutions instead of using one large kernel.**

## The architecture (slide 3)

VGG-16 (the most-used variant): 13 conv layers + 3 FC layers.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    in([224×224×3]) --> b1["Block 1: 2× Conv 3×3, 64 ch"]
    b1 --> p1[MaxPool 2×2]
    p1 --> b2["Block 2: 2× Conv 3×3, 128 ch"]
    b2 --> p2[MaxPool 2×2]
    p2 --> b3["Block 3: 3× Conv 3×3, 256 ch"]
    b3 --> p3[MaxPool 2×2]
    p3 --> b4["Block 4: 3× Conv 3×3, 512 ch"]
    b4 --> p4[MaxPool 2×2]
    p4 --> b5["Block 5: 3× Conv 3×3, 512 ch"]
    b5 --> p5[MaxPool 2×2]
    p5 --> fc6["FC 4096"]
    fc6 --> fc7["FC 4096"]
    fc7 --> fc8["FC 1000"]
    fc8 --> out([softmax])
```

Five blocks, each ending in a 2× max-pool. Channels double each block (64 → 128 → 256 → 512 → 512). Spatial dim halves each block (224 → 112 → 56 → 28 → 14 → 7).

## Why $3\times 3$ instead of $5\times 5$ or $7\times 7$?

Two stacked $3\times 3$ convs have:
- Same effective receptive field as one $5\times 5$ conv ($5\times 5$ window).
- More non-linearity (two ReLUs instead of one).
- Fewer parameters: $2 \cdot 3^2 \cdot C^2 = 18 C^2$ vs $5^2 \cdot C^2 = 25 C^2$.

Three stacked $3\times 3$ convs match a $7\times 7$ window with $27 C^2$ vs $49 C^2$ parameters.

This insight became the default: from VGG onwards, virtually every CNN uses small kernels stacked deep.

## Strengths

- Architecturally **simple and uniform** — easy to implement, modify, and analyse.
- Strong feature extractor (still occasionally used as a perceptual-loss backbone).

## Weaknesses

- **Massive parameter count** (138M for VGG-16) — most of it in the three FC layers (`FC 4096` × 2 alone is ~120M parameters).
- Slow to train and inference-hungry.
- Deeper variants (>19 layers) failed to train without [[residual-connection]] — VGG hit the depth ceiling that ResNet would later break.

## Modern relevance

Largely superseded by ResNet and its descendants for accuracy/efficiency, but its design rule — **stack small convs, double channels when halving spatial size** — is still applied universally.

## Related

- [[cnn-architecture]] — the canonical pattern VGG codified
- [[residual-connection]] — what enabled the next leap in depth
- [[inception-module]] — alternative 2014 design (parallel branches instead of uniform stack)

```mermaid
    fc8 --> out([softmax])
```

Five blocks, each ending in a 2× max-pool. Channels double each block (64 → 128 → 256 → 512 → 512). Spatial dim halves each block (224 → 112 → 56 → 28 → 14 → 7).

## Why $3\times 3$ instead of $5\times 5$ or $7\times 7$?

Two stacked $3\times 3$ convs have:
- Same effective receptive field as one $5\times 5$ conv ($5\times 5$ window).
- More non-linearity (two ReLUs instead of one).
- Fewer parameters: $2 \cdot 3^2 \cdot C^2 = 18 C^2$ vs $5^2 \cdot C^2 = 25 C^2$.

Three stacked $3\times 3$ convs match a $7\times 7$ window with $27 C^2$ vs $49 C^2$ parameters.

This insight became the default: from VGG onwards, virtually every CNN uses small kernels stacked deep.

## Strengths

- Architecturally **simple and uniform** — easy to implement, modify, and analyse.
- Strong feature extractor (still occasionally used as a perceptual-loss backbone).

## Weaknesses

- **Massive parameter count** (138M for VGG-16) — most of it in the three FC layers (`FC 4096` × 2 alone is ~120M parameters).
- Slow to train and inference-hungry.
- Deeper variants (>19 layers) failed to train without [[residual-connection]] — VGG hit the depth ceiling that ResNet would later break.

## Modern relevance

Largely superseded by ResNet and its descendants for accuracy/efficiency, but its design rule — **stack small convs, double channels when halving spatial size** — is still applied universally.

## Related

- [[cnn-architecture]] — the canonical pattern VGG codified
- [[residual-connection]] — what enabled the next leap in depth
- [[inception-module]] — alternative 2014 design (parallel branches instead of uniform stack)

---
name: Inception module
description: 2014 CNN block with parallel branches at multiple kernel sizes — multi-scale features in one layer
type: concept
courses: [Computer-Vision]
source: [17_advanced_architectures(1).pdf]
created: 2026-04-26
tags: [concept, deep-learning, cnn, architecture, history]
---

# Inception module

The defining block of GoogLeNet / Inception (2014, Szegedy et al). Departs from the uniform stack of [[vggnet]]: a single block runs **parallel branches** with different kernel sizes and pooling, then concatenates them along the channel dimension.

## Naive form (slide 5)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    in[Previous layer] --> b1[1×1 conv]
    in --> b2[3×3 conv]
    in --> b3[5×5 conv]
    in --> b4[3×3 max pool]
    b1 --> cat[Channel concatenation]
    b2 --> cat
    b3 --> cat
    b4 --> cat
    cat --> next[Next layer]
```

Different branches see the input at different scales. The output is the channel-concatenation of all four — the next layer can decide which branch's features matter.

## With $1\times 1$ bottlenecks (Inception v1, the actual GoogLeNet)

The naive version is too expensive — the 5×5 branch has $5^2 \cdot C_\text{in} \cdot C_\text{out}$ parameters at full input depth. Inception v1 inserts $1\times 1$ convolutions before the expensive ones to *reduce channels*:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    in[Previous layer] --> b1[1×1 conv]
    in --> r3[1×1 conv reduce] --> b2[3×3 conv]
    in --> r5[1×1 conv reduce] --> b3[5×5 conv]
    in --> p[3×3 max pool] --> b4[1×1 conv]
    b1 --> cat[Channel concat]
    b2 --> cat
    b3 --> cat
    b4 --> cat
    cat --> next[Next layer]
```

The $1\times 1$ "reduce" convolutions are bottlenecks (e.g. shrink 256 channels → 64) — slashing the cost of the subsequent $3\times 3$ / $5\times 5$ convs by 4× without losing information.

## Other innovations from Inception (slide 5)

- **Auxiliary loss heads** attached at intermediate layers, summed into the total loss. The intent was to push gradient signal back into early layers. Now obsolete — [[residual-connection]] does this better and more uniformly.
- **Global Average Pooling + single linear classifier** at the end, instead of two huge FC layers. This pattern became universal (see [[cnn-architecture]]).

## Trade-offs

- **Pro**: multi-scale features in a single block; flexible, parameter-efficient with bottlenecks.
- **Con**: architecturally complex, fiddly to design (hand-tuned channel allocations per branch). Variants (Inception v3, v4) added more options and got messier.

## Modern descendants

- **Xception** (2017): replace each Inception branch with depthwise-separable conv (see [[depthwise-separable-conv]]).
- **MixNet, EfficientNet** keep the multi-scale spirit but search over branch widths automatically.

## Related

- [[vggnet]] — the simpler contemporary
- [[residual-connection]] — what replaced auxiliary losses
- [[depthwise-separable-conv]] — modernisation of the branch idea
- [[cnn-architecture]] — Inception's GAP+Linear ending became the standard

Add --> Output ``` ## ResNet macro-architecture (lecture 17, slides 6–7) ResNet stitches residual blocks into a hierarchical CNN trunk: ```mermaid flowchart LR in([224×224×3]) --> stem[7×7 Conv stride 2] stem --> bn[BN + ReLU] bn --> mp[3×3 MaxPool stride 2] mp --> s1["Stage 1 — 64 ch
2× (or 3×) blocks"] s1 --> s2["Stage 2 — 128 ch
downsample on 1st block"] s2 --> s3["Stage 3 — 256 ch
downsample on 1st block"] s3 --> s4["Stage 4 — 512 ch
downsample on 1st block"] s4 --> gap[Global AvgPool] gap --> fc[FC → num_classes] ``` Two block forms inside each stage: | Form | Skip path | |---|---| | **Identity skip** (no spatial/channel change) | $y = \mathrm{ReLU}(x + \text{block}(x))$ | | **Projection skip** (when stride 2 or channel count changes) | $y = \mathrm{ReLU}(\mathrm{Conv}_{1\times 1}(x) + \text{block}(x))$ | The $1\times 1$ projection on the skip path matches dimensions when the residual path downsamples or changes depth. Always stride 2 on both the projection and the first conv in the block, never on the second. ## Related - [[batch-normalization]] — typically applied inside the residual block before activation - [[backpropagation]] — explains why the addition gives a gradient shortcut - [[vanishing-exploding-gradients]] — what skip connections cure - [[densenet]] — alternative to addition: concatenation-based skip - [[transformer-encoder]] — uses residual + LayerNorm around each sublayer

Stop. Read this before continuing the ingest. The Mermaid diagrams you've been generating have a recurring rendering problem: they include Markdown syntax that Mermaid doesn't parse (blockquotes >, bullets - , **bold**, _italic_, # headers, , raw $LaTeX$ in node labels). These produce "Unsupported markdown" errors in Obsidian. A separate agent is fixing the existing broken diagrams. Do not touch any previously-generated content. Your only job is to not produce more of the problem going forward. For the remaining lectures, follow these diagram rules strictly: Inside a ```mermaid block, the following are FORBIDDEN — they break rendering: > at the start of a line (blockquote syntax) **bold**, _italic_, ==highlight== anywhere in node labels - bullet or * bullet lines # header lines (use %% Mermaid comment instead) Raw LaTeX $...$ inside unquoted node labels Allowed inside Mermaid: HTML for emphasis inside quoted labels: A["bold text"], A["italic"] Line breaks inside labels: A["line one
line two"] Mermaid comments: %% this is a comment Subgraphs for grouping: subgraph encoder [Encoder] ... end Edge labels for annotations: A -->|"stride 2"| B Dotted-edge note nodes for callouts: Main -.-> Note["note text"] When you'd want a feature Mermaid doesn't support — LaTeX inside a node, internal [[wikilinks]], tables in nodes, multi-line lists — use ```mehrmaid instead of ```mermaid. Mehrmaid is a plugin that renders full Obsidian markdown inside node labels when the content is wrapped in ("double-quoted parens"). Example: ```mehrmaid graph LR A("$\nabla I = [I_x, I_y]$") --> B("[[gradient-magnitude]]") ``` Default to plain mermaid. Only reach for mehrmaid when the node content genuinely needs markdown features. Most architectural flows don't. Restructure before reaching for markdown. If you're tempted to put a bullet list inside a node, that's almost always a signal the node should be a subgraph with multiple smaller nodes, not one node with a list. Restructure the diagram instead of needing markdown to support a bad structure. Equations: keep LaTeX in surrounding prose around the diagram, not inside it. The diagram references the equation by reference ("see equation above"); the equation itself lives in the markdown above or below. Only put an equation inside a node — using mehrmaid — when the equation IS the concept the node represents. Acknowledge by listing back, in your own words: The five Mermaid anti-patterns you'll stop using When you'll switch from mermaid to mehrmaid Where equations should live (in the diagram vs. around it) Then resume the ingest from wherever you were. Do not retroactively edit previous lectures — the other agent has that.

Acknowledged.

**Five Mermaid anti-patterns I'll stop using:**

1. **Blockquote `>` at line start** inside node labels — Mermaid parses these as syntax errors, not as quoted text.
2. **Markdown emphasis** (`**bold**`, `_italic_`, `==highlight==`) inside node labels — use HTML tags (`<b>`, `<i>`) inside quoted labels instead.
3. **List markers** (`-`, `*`) and **header `#` lines** inside node labels — restructure as subgraphs with separate nodes, not lists in one node.
4. **HTML comments** `<!-- ... -->` inside the diagram — use Mermaid's `%%` comment syntax.
5. **Raw LaTeX `$...$`** inside unquoted node labels — keep equations in the surrounding prose; only use them inside `mehrmaid` when the equation *is* the node.

**When I switch from `mermaid` to `mehrmaid`:** only when a node genuinely needs markdown features — LaTeX inside a node, internal `[[wikilinks]]` inside a node, tables inside a node, or multi-line bullet content that can't be cleanly restructured into separate nodes. Wrap such labels in `("...")` (double-quoted parens). Default stays plain `mermaid`.

**Where equations live:** in the markdown prose around the diagram (above or below it), referenced from the diagram in plain text. The diagram says "Q · Kᵀ" or "loss block"; the LaTeX `$\mathrm{softmax}(QK^\top/\sqrt{d_k})V$` lives in the prose. Only put an equation *inside* a node — and only via `mehrmaid` — when that equation **is** the concept the node represents.

Resuming lecture 17 ingest. I had finished the lecture note, vggnet.md, inception-module.md, and the residual-connection.md extension. Continuing with the remaining concepts.

content.newText:
---
name: DenseNet
description: 2016 CNN where every layer concatenates features from all earlier layers — maximum feature reuse
type: concept
courses: [Computer-Vision]
source: [17_advanced_architectures(1).pdf]
created: 2026-04-26
tags: [concept, deep-learning, cnn, architecture, history]
---

# DenseNet

A 2016 architecture (Huang et al). Generalises [[residual-connection]] from "skip + add" to "skip + concatenate," and applies it densely: **each layer receives the concatenation of every earlier layer's feature maps as its input.**

## Connectivity (slide 8)

Within a "dense block," for the $\ell$-th layer:

$$x_\ell = H_\ell\!\left([x_0, x_1, \ldots, x_{\ell-1}]\right)$$

where $[\cdots]$ is channel concatenation and $H_\ell$ is a small composite (BN + ReLU + 3×3 Conv).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    x0[x0] --> h1[H1]
    h1 --> x1[x1]
    x0 --> x1
    x1 --> h2[H2]
    h2 --> x2[x2]
    x0 --> x2
    x1 --> x2
    x2 --> h3[H3]
    h3 --> x3[x3]
    x0 --> x3
    x1 --> x3
    x2 --> x3
```

Each $H_\ell$ produces only a small fixed number of new channels (the "growth rate" $k$, e.g. 32). Even though every layer sees all previous features, the marginal cost per layer is modest.

## Add vs concatenate

| Method | Mixing | Memory |
|---|---|---|
| ResNet (add) | $y = F(x) + x$ — overwrites with sum | small (no extra channels) |
| DenseNet (concat) | $y = [x, F(x)]$ — preserves both | grows quadratically with depth |

Concatenation never destroys information; addition can. But the channel count grows linearly with each new $H_\ell$, and the memory footprint of all the concatenated activations is the main practical drawback.

## Architecture

Stack of **dense blocks** separated by **transition layers** (1×1 conv + 2×2 avg-pool) that downsample and shrink channels.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    in([Image]) --> stem[Stem Conv]
    stem --> db1[Dense Block 1]
    db1 --> t1[Transition: 1×1 Conv + AvgPool]
    t1 --> db2[Dense Block 2]
    db2 --> t2[Transition]
    t2 --> db3[Dense Block 3]
    db3 --> t3[Transition]
    t3 --> db4[Dense Block 4]
    db4 --> gap[Global AvgPool]
    gap --> fc[FC]
```

## Trade-offs

- **Pro**: Strong feature reuse → fewer parameters than a comparable ResNet for the same accuracy.
- **Pro**: Implicit deep supervision (every later layer can directly see early features → gradient flow is good).
- **Con**: Memory-heavy. Every concatenation is materialised in activations, so VRAM use is high during training.
- **Con**: Slower training in practice than ResNet despite fewer parameters.

## Modern relevance

Largely superseded by ResNeXt and EfficientNet on accuracy/efficiency, but the "concatenation as skip" idea persists in U-Net (lecture 18) and feature-pyramid networks for detection.

## Related

- [[residual-connection]] — the additive precursor
- [[cnn-architecture]] — stage / transition pattern is shared
- [[squeeze-excitation]] — orthogonal channel attention add-on, often combined

content.newText:
--- name: Squeeze-and-Excitation description: 2017 channel-attention block — learn per-channel rescaling weights from a global descriptor of the feature map type: concept courses: [Computer-Vision] source: [17_advanced_architectures(1).pdf] created: 2026-04-26 tags: [concept, deep-learning, cnn, attention] --- # Squeeze-and-Excitation (SE) block A plug-in module (Hu et al, 2017) that adds **channel attention** to a CNN. After every conv block, an SE module learns "which channels are most relevant for this input?" and rescales channels accordingly. ## The block (slide 9) ```mermaid flowchart LR fmap["feature map H × W × C"] --> sq["Squeeze
Global AvgPool
1 × 1 × C"] sq --> mlp["FC C to C/r
ReLU
FC C/r to C
Sigmoid"] mlp --> w["weights 1 × 1 × C"] fmap --> mul[multiply per-channel] w --> mul mul --> out["recalibrated H × W × C"] ``` Three steps: 1. **Squeeze** — collapse the spatial dimensions of the feature map into a 1-vector per channel via global average pooling: $z_c = \frac{1}{HW}\sum_{i,j} u_c(i, j)$. 2. **Excite** — feed $z$ through a small bottleneck MLP: $\mathrm{FC}(C \to C/r) \to \mathrm{ReLU} \to \mathrm{FC}(C/r \to C) \to \mathrm{Sigmoid}$, producing a $C$-vector $s$ in $[0, 1]$. 3. **Reweight** — multiply each channel of the original feature map by its weight: $\tilde u_c = s_c \cdot u_c$. The reduction ratio $r$ (typical: 16) keeps the SE module cheap. ## Why it helps A standard conv layer treats all channels equally — every output uses the same filter weights regardless of which other channels happened to fire. SE adds an *input-dependent* per-channel gate. For one image the "fur texture" channel matters more; for another the "metallic gleam" channel does. The network learns to amplify or suppress channels per sample. This is the **first form of attention** that became broadly used in CNNs — predates the ViT explosion by several years. ## Where it goes Drop into any existing block as the last sublayer: - After the residual addition in a ResNet block → SE-ResNet. - Inside an inverted residual in MobileNetV3 → significant improvement. - Inside an Inception branch → SE-Inception. ## Trade-offs - **Pro**: Cheap (a few hundred parameters per block; sub-1% extra FLOPs); reliably improves accuracy by ~1% on ImageNet. - **Pro**: Simple drop-in. - **Con**: Adds a sequential dependency (the gate is computed from a global pool, blocking parallelism somewhat). ## Variants - **CBAM**: SE + spatial attention (where in the feature map matters). - **ECA**: replaces the 2-FC bottleneck with a 1D conv on the channel axis — fewer parameters. ## Related - [[self-attention]] — the more general spatial-attention mechanism - [[residual-connection]] — SE is usually placed inside a residual block - [[cnn-architecture]] — drop-in addition to any backbone
