SE adds an *input-dependent* per-channel gate. For one image the "fur texture" channel matters more; for another the "metallic gleam" channel does. The network learns to amplify or suppress channels per sample. This is the **first form of attention** that became broadly used in CNNs — predates the ViT explosion by several years. ## Where it goes Drop into any existing block as the last sublayer: - After the residual addition in a ResNet block → SE-ResNet. - Inside an inverted residual in MobileNetV3 → significant improvement. - Inside an Inception branch → SE-Inception. ## Trade-offs - **Pro**: Cheap (a few hundred parameters per block; sub-1% extra FLOPs); reliably improves accuracy by ~1% on ImageNet. - **Pro**: Simple drop-in. - **Con**: Adds a sequential dependency (the gate is computed from a global pool, blocking parallelism somewhat). ## Variants - **CBAM**: SE + spatial attention (where in the feature map matters). - **ECA**: replaces the 2-FC bottleneck with a 1D conv on the channel axis — fewer parameters. ## Related - [[self-attention]] — the more general spatial-attention mechanism - [[residual-connection]] — SE is usually placed inside a residual block - [[cnn-architecture]] — drop-in addition to any backbone

content.newText:
---
name: Self-attention
description: Update each token by a weighted average of all other tokens, with weights computed from learned Query/Key/Value projections
type: concept
courses: [Computer-Vision]
source: [17_advanced_architectures(1).pdf]
created: 2026-04-26
tags: [concept, deep-learning, transformer]
---

# Self-attention

The core operation in transformers. Each token's new representation is a **weighted average of all tokens** in the sequence, where the weights are computed from learned projections of the same tokens (hence "self").

## Intuition (slides 14–15, 19–22)

A token's meaning depends on its context. "It" in "The cat saw a bird, and it was thinking of eating it" can refer to either the cat or the bird depending on which other words it attends to.

Self-attention lets every token *ask a question* about every other token and receive a weighted answer.

The DB analogy: each token has a **Query** ("what am I looking for?"), a **Key** ("what kind of thing am I?"), and a **Value** ("here's the information I carry"). For each query, we compute a similarity (dot product) against every key, softmax those into weights, and use them to mix values.

## Pipeline (slide 18, 26)

For input matrix $X \in \mathbb{R}^{L \times d}$ ($L$ tokens, model dim $d$):

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    X["Input X (L by d)"] --> wq["multiply by W_Q"]
    X --> wk["multiply by W_K"]
    X --> wv["multiply by W_V"]
    wq --> Q["Q (L by d_k)"]
    wk --> K["K (L by d_k)"]
    wv --> V["V (L by d_v)"]
    Q --> qk["Q times K transpose"]
    K --> qk
    qk --> scl["divide by sqrt d_k"]
    scl --> sm[softmax row-wise]
    sm --> A["A (L by L) attention matrix"]
    A --> mul["A times V"]
    V --> mul
    mul --> Z(["Output Z (L by d_v)"])
```

The full equation (kept in surrounding prose, not in the diagram):

$$\text{Attention}(Q, K, V) = \mathrm{softmax}\!\left(\frac{Q K^\top}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right) V$$

## Why each piece

| Component | Role |
|---|---|
| $Q$, $K$, $V$ separate | decouples "what to ask," "what to advertise," "what to deliver" |
| Dot product $QK^\top$ | similarity score: large when query and key align, negative when opposed |
| Divide by $\sqrt{d_k}$ | keeps softmax inputs at unit-ish scale; without this, large $d_k$ pushes softmax into saturating regimes |
| Row-wise softmax | each query's weights sum to 1, attention is a convex combination |
| Multiply by $V$ | weighted average of values produces the new token representation |

## Cost

- **Memory**: $O(L^2)$ — the attention matrix $A$ is $L \times L$.
- **Compute**: $O(L^2 \cdot d)$.

This quadratic cost is the central bottleneck of transformers on long sequences. Mitigations: windowed attention (Swin), linear attention, FlashAttention's tiled-IO trick, sparse / sliding-window patterns.

## Permutation invariance

The dot products and softmax are **symmetric in the ordering of the keys**: shuffling tokens shuffles rows and columns of $A$ but doesn't change *which* value each query collects. So self-attention by itself is **permutation-equivariant** — order doesn't matter unless it's injected via [[positional-encoding]].

## Self vs cross attention

- **Self-attention**: $Q$, $K$, $V$ all from the same sequence $X$. Used inside encoder blocks and inside decoder self-attention.
- **Cross-attention**: $Q$ from one sequence, $K$, $V$ from another. Used in encoder-decoder transformers (decoder queries the encoder).

## Related

- [[multi-head-attention]] — run several self-attentions in parallel
- [[positional-encoding]] — restore order information
- [[transformer-encoder]] — wraps self-attention with residual + LayerNorm + MLP
- [[squeeze-excitation]] — channel-attention, an earlier and simpler attention idea

content.newText:
--- name: Multi-head attention description: Run h parallel self-attentions with smaller dimensions, concatenate, and project — lets each head ask a different question type: concept courses: [Computer-Vision] source: [17_advanced_architectures(1).pdf] created: 2026-04-26 tags: [concept, deep-learning, transformer] --- # Multi-head attention (MHA) Standard [[self-attention]] computes one attention pattern per layer. Multi-head attention runs $h$ self-attentions **in parallel**, each with a smaller per-head dimension, then concatenates and linearly projects the results. ## The mechanism (slides 27–29) For $h$ heads with per-head dimension $d_k = d/h$: ```mermaid flowchart LR X["X (L by d)"] --> head1["Head 1
own W_Q, W_K, W_V"] X --> head2["Head 2
own W_Q, W_K, W_V"] X --> headh["Head h
own W_Q, W_K, W_V"] head1 --> z1["Z_1 (L by d/h)"] head2 --> z2["Z_2 (L by d/h)"] headh --> zh["Z_h (L by d/h)"] z1 --> cat["concat (L by d)"] z2 --> cat zh --> cat cat --> proj["multiply by W_O"] proj --> Z(["Z (L by d)"]) ``` In equations (kept in prose, outside the diagram): $$\text{head}_i = \text{Attention}(X W^Q_i,\, X W^K_i,\, X W^V_i)$$ $$\mathrm{MHA}(X) = \mathrm{Concat}(\text{head}_1, \ldots, \text{head}_h)\, W^O$$ ## Why multiple heads A single attention matrix forces the layer to commit to *one* mixing pattern. With $h$ heads, each can specialise: - one head tracks **syntactic agreement** ("which verb does this noun match?") - another tracks **coreference** ("which entity does this pronoun refer to?") - another tracks **positional patterns** ("the previous token") - in vision, different heads learn to attend at different spatial scales The model learns this division of labour without supervision — it just emerges from training. ## Why dimensions split as $d/h$ - Total parameter count of the $W^Q_i, W^K_i, W^V_i$ across all heads is $h \cdot d \cdot (d/h) = d^2$ each — same as a single full-dimension self-attention. - Total compute also stays roughly the same. - So MHA is "free" in terms of total cost relative to single-head; you're just slicing the same compute differently. ## Typical sizes | Model | $d$ | $h$ | $d/h$ | |---|---|---|---| | ViT-Base | 768 | 12 | 64 | | ViT-Large | 1024 | 16 | 64 | | GPT-3 175B | 12288 | 96 | 128 | 64 has been a common per-head dimension across many transformer variants. ## In PyTorch ```python mha = nn.MultiheadAttention(embed_dim=768, num_heads=12, batch_first=True) out, attn_weights = mha(query=x, key=x, value=x) # self-attention ``` For self-attention pass the same tensor as Q/K/V; for cross-attention pass different ones. ## Related - [[self-attention]] — the per-head primitive - [[positional-encoding]] — added before the first MHA layer - [[transformer-encoder]] — wraps MHA with residual + LayerNorm + MLP

content.newText:
---
name: Positional encoding
description: Inject token order into transformers — sinusoidal, learned absolute, relative bias, or rotary (RoPE)
type: concept
courses: [Computer-Vision]
source: [17_advanced_architectures(1).pdf]
created: 2026-04-26
tags: [concept, deep-learning, transformer]
---

# Positional encoding

[[Self-attention]] is permutation-equivariant — it has no notion of token order. "The cat is sat" and "Sat cat is the" produce identical attention output. Sequences need order; positional encoding is how we add it back.

## Four common variants

| Variant | What | Where used |
|---|---|---|
| **Sinusoidal absolute** | fixed sinusoid of the position index | original Transformer (2017), some ViTs |
| **Learned absolute** | one learnable vector per position | BERT, original ViT |
| **Relative bias** | a learned scalar bias dependent on distance, added inside attention | T5, Swin |
| **Rotary (RoPE)** | rotate the Q and K vectors by an angle proportional to position | LLaMA, modern LLMs, recent ViTs |

## Sinusoidal absolute (slide 31)

Each position $\mathrm{pos}$ gets a fixed $d$-dimensional vector:

$$\mathrm{PE}_{(\mathrm{pos},\, 2i)} = \sin\!\left(\frac{\mathrm{pos}}{10000^{2i/d}}\right),\qquad \mathrm{PE}_{(\mathrm{pos},\, 2i+1)} = \cos\!\left(\frac{\mathrm{pos}}{10000^{2i/d}}\right)$$

Different frequencies along the embedding dim let the model resolve both fine-grained adjacency and long-range distances. The positional vector is **added** to the token embedding before the first transformer block.

Property: relative offsets $\Delta$ produce a linear transformation of the encoding, so the model can learn to attend by relative position even though the encoding itself is absolute.

## Learned absolute (slide 32)

A learnable matrix $E_\mathrm{pos} \in \mathbb{R}^{L \times d}$. Each row is the embedding of one position. Like sinusoidal in placement (added to token embedding), but the values come from training data instead of being hand-designed.

- Slightly outperforms sinusoidal on fixed-length tasks.
- Cannot extrapolate beyond sequence lengths seen during training (sinusoidal can, in principle).

## Relative positional bias (slide 33)

Modify the attention score itself by a distance-dependent learnable bias $B_{i, j}$:

$$\mathrm{Attention}(i, j) = \frac{Q_i K_j^\top}{\sqrt{d_k}} + B_{i, j}$$

where $B_{i, j}$ depends only on $|i - j|$ (or, in 2D for ViT, on the horizontal and vertical distance). Adds **translation equivariance** to attention: shifting the whole sequence shifts every $i$ and $j$ together so the bias is preserved.

Used by Swin Transformer; gives a CNN-like spatial inductive bias to attention.

## Rotary positional embedding (RoPE, slide 34)

Apply a position-dependent rotation $R(\mathrm{pos})$ to the Query and Key vectors before the dot product:

$$Q' = R(\mathrm{pos})\, Q,\qquad K' = R(\mathrm{pos})\, K$$

The rotation is a block-diagonal matrix of 2D rotations whose angles increase with position. The dot product $Q'^\top K'$ then implicitly depends on the **relative** position $\mathrm{pos}_q - \mathrm{pos}_k$.

- Strong empirical performance.
- Excellent length extrapolation (attention generalises to sequence lengths beyond training).
- Default in modern LLMs.

## In ViT

The original ViT used learned absolute embeddings, one per patch position. Swin uses relative positional bias. More recent vision transformers are adopting RoPE-style mechanisms.

## When you need positional encoding and when you don't

- Sequence models (text, time series, speech): always.
- ViT (image patches in raster order): always.
- Set-based models (point clouds, particle physics): typically not — the data is genuinely orderless.

## Related

- [[self-attention]] — what positional encoding fixes
- [[multi-head-attention]] — typically receives positionally-encoded inputs
- [[vision-transformer]] — applies positional encoding to image patches
- [[swin-transformer]] — uses 2D relative positional bias

content.newText:
---
name: Transformer encoder block
description: Multi-head self-attention plus MLP, each wrapped with residual connection and layer norm — the workhorse layer
type: concept
courses: [Computer-Vision]
source: [17_advanced_architectures(1).pdf]
created: 2026-04-26
tags: [concept, deep-learning, transformer]
---

# Transformer encoder block

The repeating layer at the heart of every transformer (BERT, ViT, GPT). One block contains two sublayers (attention and MLP), each wrapped with a residual connection and a LayerNorm.

## Two sublayers

1. **Multi-head self-attention** — token mixing across the sequence.
2. **Position-wise MLP** — a per-token 2-layer feedforward, typically `Linear(d → 4d) → GELU → Linear(4d → d)`. Operates independently on each token; expands then contracts the channel dimension.

Both sublayers are wrapped:

```
sublayer_output = x + Sublayer(LayerNorm(x))      (pre-norm, modern default)
sublayer_output = LayerNorm(x + Sublayer(x))      (post-norm, original 2017)
```

## Pre-norm vs post-norm (slides 35–36)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph post[Post-norm 2017 original]
        i1[Input] --> a1[Self-Attention]
        a1 --> add1((+))
        i1 --> add1
        add1 --> ln1[LayerNorm]
        ln1 --> m1[MLP]
        m1 --> add2((+))
        ln1 --> add2
        add2 --> ln2[LayerNorm]
        ln2 --> o1[Output]
    end
    subgraph pre[Pre-norm modern default]
        i2[Input] --> ln3[LayerNorm]
        ln3 --> a2[Self-Attention]
        a2 --> add3((+))
        i2 --> add3
        add3 --> ln4[LayerNorm]
        ln4 --> m2[MLP]
        m2 --> add4((+))
        add3 --> add4
        add4 --> o2[Output]
    end
```

Pre-norm trains more stably for very deep transformers (>12 layers), needs less warmup, and is now the default in ViT, GPT-style LLMs, and ConvNeXt.

## Why LayerNorm and not BatchNorm

Sequences are short and variable-length; transformer batches at inference are often size 1 (one sequence at a time). [[batch-normalization]] would compute statistics over batch + sequence dims, which behave erratically when the batch is small. LayerNorm normalises across the **feature dimension within a single token**, so it doesn't depend on the batch.

For each token vector $x \in \mathbb{R}^d$:

$$\hat x_j = \frac{x_j - \mu(x)}{\sqrt{\sigma^2(x) + \epsilon}},\qquad y = \gamma\,\hat x + \beta$$

with $\mu, \sigma^2$ taken over the $d$ feature dimensions and $\gamma, \beta \in \mathbb{R}^d$ learned.

## Why residual connections

Same reason as in ResNet: gradients have an identity shortcut around each sublayer, making deep transformers (24, 48, 96+ layers) trainable. See [[residual-connection]].

## Why the 4× expansion in the MLP

Empirical: every sublayer mixes tokens (attention) and then mixes channels (MLP). The MLP needs enough capacity to do non-trivial per-token computation; 4× expansion is the convention since 2017 and remains standard.

## Block I/O contract

- Input: $L \times d$ (sequence of $L$ tokens, model dim $d$).
- Output: $L \times d$ (same shape).
- Stacked $N$ times to make the full encoder. Each layer is identical in shape but learned independently.

## In PyTorch

```python
encoder_layer = nn.TransformerEncoderLayer(
    d_model=768, nhead=12, dim_feedforward=3072,
    activation="gelu", norm_first=True,    # pre-norm
)
encoder = nn.TransformerEncoder(encoder_layer, num_layers=12)
```

## Related

- [[self-attention]] — the token-mixing sublayer
- [[multi-head-attention]] — what's actually inside that sublayer
- [[positional-encoding]] — added to inputs before the first block
- [[residual-connection]] — wraps each sublayer
- [[batch-normalization]] — what LayerNorm replaces here
- [[vision-transformer]] — stacks transformer encoders on patch embeddings

        ln4 --> m2[MLP]
        m2 --> add4((+))
        add3 --> add4
        add4 --> o2[Output]
    end
```

Pre-norm trains more stably for very deep transformers (>12 layers), needs less warmup, and is now the default in ViT, GPT-style LLMs, and ConvNeXt.

## Why LayerNorm and not BatchNorm

Sequences are short and variable-length; transformer batches at inference are often size 1 (one sequence at a time). [[batch-normalization]] would compute statistics over batch + sequence dims, which behave erratically when the batch is small. LayerNorm normalises across the **feature dimension within a single token**, so it doesn't depend on the batch.

For each token vector $x \in \mathbb{R}^d$:

$$\hat x_j = \frac{x_j - \mu(x)}{\sqrt{\sigma^2(x) + \epsilon}},\qquad y = \gamma\,\hat x + \beta$$

with $\mu, \sigma^2$ taken over the $d$ feature dimensions and $\gamma, \beta \in \mathbb{R}^d$ learned.

## Why residual connections

Same reason as in ResNet: gradients have an identity shortcut around each sublayer, making deep transformers (24, 48, 96+ layers) trainable. See [[residual-connection]].

## Why the 4× expansion in the MLP

Empirical: every sublayer mixes tokens (attention) and then mixes channels (MLP). The MLP needs enough capacity to do non-trivial per-token computation; 4× expansion is the convention since 2017 and remains standard.

## Block I/O contract

- Input: $L \times d$ (sequence of $L$ tokens, model dim $d$).
- Output: $L \times d$ (same shape).
- Stacked $N$ times to make the full encoder. Each layer is identical in shape but learned independently.

## In PyTorch

```python
encoder_layer = nn.TransformerEncoderLayer(
    d_model=768, nhead=12, dim_feedforward=3072,
    activation="gelu", norm_first=True,    # pre-norm
)
encoder = nn.TransformerEncoder(encoder_layer, num_layers=12)
```

## Related

- [[self-attention]] — the token-mixing sublayer
- [[multi-head-attention]] — what's actually inside that sublayer
- [[positional-encoding]] — added to inputs before the first block
- [[residual-connection]] — wraps each sublayer
- [[batch-normalization]] — what LayerNorm replaces here
- [[vision-transformer]] — stacks transformer encoders on patch embeddings

--- name: Vision Transformer (ViT) description: 2020 architecture that treats image patches as tokens and runs a standard transformer encoder over them type: concept courses: [Computer-Vision] source: [17_advanced_architectures(1).pdf] created: 2026-04-26 tags: [concept, deep-learning, transformer, computer-vision] --- # Vision Transformer (ViT) A 2020 architecture (Dosovitskiy et al). Treats an image as a sequence of small patches, embeds each patch as a token, and runs a standard transformer encoder over them. **No convolutions anywhere.** Showed for the first time that pure attention models can match or beat CNNs at scale. ## Pipeline (slide 37) ```mermaid flowchart LR img[Image H by W by 3] --> patch["Patchify into N patches
each P by P"] patch --> embed["Linear projection
each patch becomes a d-vector"] embed --> cls[Prepend CLS token] cls --> pos[Add positional embedding] pos --> enc["Transformer Encoder x L
MHA + MLP, pre-norm, residual"] enc --> sel["take CLS output
(or pool all tokens)"] sel --> mlp[MLP head] mlp --> logits([class logits]) ``` ## Steps 1. **Patchify**. Split the $H\times W\times 3$ image into a grid of $P\times P$ patches, e.g. $P = 16$, giving $N = HW/P^2$ patches. For $224\times 224$ input and $P=16$: 196 patches. 2. **Linear projection**. Flatten each patch to a vector of length $3 P^2$ and project to dimension $d$ (the model dim) via a single learned linear layer. Equivalently: a single $\text{Conv2d}(3, d, \text{kernel}=P, \text{stride}=P)$. 3. **CLS token**. Prepend a single learnable vector ("class token") to the sequence of $N$ patch embeddings. Length is now $N + 1$. 4. **Positional encoding**. Add a learned positional embedding (one $d$-vector per of the $N+1$ positions). Without this, attention is permutation-equivariant and the spatial layout of patches is lost. 5. **Transformer encoder stack**. $L$ identical encoder blocks (see [[transformer-encoder]]) — pre-norm, MHA + MLP with residual connections. 6. **Read out**. The CLS token's final representation is taken as the image-level summary and fed through a small MLP head to produce class logits. Alternative: average-pool all $N+1$ token outputs. ## Why a CLS token A classifier needs **one vector** per image, but the encoder produces $N + 1$ vectors. Two options: - **CLS token**: include a fake "summary" token that has no image content of its own. Self-attention pulls relevant information from real patches into it as the layers progress. Simple, the original ViT choice. - **Mean pool**: average all $N$ patch outputs. Removes the asymmetry of the CLS token. Used in modern ViTs (e.g. DeiT III, several large ViTs). Both work; CLS is slightly more common in research, mean-pool slightly more common in production. ## Sizes | Variant | $L$ | $d$ | $h$ (heads) | params | |---|---|---|---|---| | ViT-Base/16 | 12 | 768 | 12 | 86M | | ViT-Large/16 | 24 | 1024 | 16 | 307M | | ViT-Huge/14 | 32 | 1280 | 16 | 632M | The "/16" or "/14" denotes the patch size. Smaller patches → more tokens → more compute, finer detail. ## Inductive bias and the data-hunger trade-off Where CNNs hard-code locality and translation invariance ([[inductive-bias]]), ViT has neither. It must learn these from data. Result: - **Small datasets** (CIFAR, small medical sets): CNN wins; ViT overfits or fails to learn the spatial structure. - **Medium datasets** (ImageNet 1k): roughly even after careful training (DeiT, augmentations). - **Massive datasets** (JFT-300M, LAION): ViT pulls ahead; lack of inductive bias becomes an advantage because the model can learn task-specific structures the CNN's priors would forbid. ## Trade-offs - **Pro**: simple, uniform architecture; scales well; no hand-designed conv stages. - **Pro**: the same backbone serves as feature extractor for detection (DETR), segmentation (MaskFormer), and self-supervised learning (DINO, MAE). - **Con**: $O(N^2)$ memory per attention layer in the patch count → expensive at high resolution. Mitigations: Swin Transformer's windowed attention (see [[swin-transformer]]), pooling, hybrid CNN-ViT. - **Con**: data-hungry; cannot match CNNs on small datasets without heavy augmentation or pretraining. ## Related - [[transformer-encoder]] — the body of ViT - [[multi-head-attention]] — every encoder block uses this - [[positional-encoding]] — restores spatial layout - [[swin-transformer]] — hierarchical windowed alternative - [[convnext]] — the CNN response that closed the gap - [[inductive-bias]] — CNN vs ViT contrast

--- name: Swin Transformer description: 2021 hierarchical ViT with shifted-window attention — CNN-like inductive bias and linear-in-image-size cost type: concept courses: [Computer-Vision] source: [17_advanced_architectures(1).pdf] created: 2026-04-26 tags: [concept, deep-learning, transformer, computer-vision] --- # Swin Transformer A 2021 vision transformer (Liu et al, Microsoft) that re-introduces two CNN-inherited properties to attention: 1. **Locality** — attention only within a small window of patches, not globally. 2. **Hierarchy** — successive stages downsample (merge patches), so feature maps grow from fine to coarse like in a CNN. The cost is reduced from $O(N^2)$ in patch count to $O(N \cdot M^2)$ where $M$ is the window size — linear in image size, not quadratic. ## Architecture (slide 38) ```mermaid flowchart LR img[H by W by 3] --> pp[Patch Partition] pp --> le[Linear Embedding] le --> s1[Stage 1 Swin Blocks x 2] s1 --> pm1[Patch Merging 2x downsample] pm1 --> s2[Stage 2 Swin Blocks x 2] s2 --> pm2[Patch Merging 2x downsample] pm2 --> s3[Stage 3 Swin Blocks x 6] s3 --> pm3[Patch Merging 2x downsample] pm3 --> s4[Stage 4 Swin Blocks x 2] ``` Spatial dim halves and channel count doubles between each stage — same growth heuristic as a CNN ([[cnn-architecture]]). ## Window attention Within a stage, partition the feature map into non-overlapping $M \times M$ windows ($M = 7$ typical). Self-attention runs **inside each window only**. This is fast ($O(M^4)$ per window, $O(N M^2)$ total) but tokens never see across windows, breaking the global-context property of standard ViT. ## Shifted windows To restore cross-window communication, alternate two block types: | Block type | Window grid | |---|---| | W-MSA (Windowed Multi-head Self-Attention) | aligned to grid | | SW-MSA (Shifted Window Multi-head Self-Attention) | grid shifted by $\lfloor M/2 \rfloor$ | Stacking W-MSA → SW-MSA → W-MSA → SW-MSA gives every patch the chance to attend to every other patch *within a few layers*, while keeping per-layer cost linear. ## Patch merging (downsample) Between stages, group $2 \times 2$ neighbouring patches and concatenate their feature vectors → $4d$ channels. A linear layer projects back to $2d$, so spatial drops by 4× and channels double. This is the transformer analogue of strided convolution: spatial reduction with channel expansion. ## Block layout ```mermaid flowchart LR in[Input] --> ln1[LN] ln1 --> wmsa["W-MSA
or SW-MSA"] wmsa --> add1((+)) in --> add1 add1 --> ln2[LN] ln2 --> mlp[MLP] mlp --> add2((+)) add1 --> add2 add2 --> out[Output] ``` Standard pre-norm transformer block (see [[transformer-encoder]]) with the attention swapped for windowed/shifted variants. ## Why this matters - **Linear cost in image size** → ViT-style models become viable for high-resolution dense prediction (segmentation, detection). - **Hierarchical features** → drop-in backbone for tasks that need multi-scale features (FPN-style heads in detection, U-Net-style decoders in segmentation). - **Local + global mix via shifting** → first 1k+-resolution ViT that competes on COCO and ADE20K. ## Trade-offs - **Pro**: Strong on dense prediction tasks where standard ViT struggles. - **Pro**: CNN-replacement in many production pipelines (e.g. backbone for DINO, MaskFormer). - **Con**: More complex than vanilla ViT; the shifted-window machinery has tricky cyclic-shift implementation details for efficiency. - **Con**: Loses some of ViT's generality — the windowed structure encodes a CNN-like prior. ## Related - [[vision-transformer]] — the global-attention precursor - [[transformer-encoder]] — the per-block primitive - [[positional-encoding]] — Swin uses 2D relative bias - [[convnext]] — the CNN response designed to match Swin's design philosophy

---
name: ConvNeXt
description: 2022 modernised CNN that ports ViT design choices into a ResNet — closes the gap with vision transformers
type: concept
courses: [Computer-Vision]
source: [17_advanced_architectures(1).pdf]
created: 2026-04-26
tags: [concept, deep-learning, cnn, architecture, history]
---

# ConvNeXt

A 2022 architecture (Liu et al, FAIR). The thesis: vision transformers don't beat CNNs because of attention; they win because of *training recipe* and *micro-design* choices. ConvNeXt takes a standard ResNet and systematically ports each ViT-era choice into it. The result matches or beats Swin Transformer on ImageNet.

## What gets ported

| ResNet (2015) | ConvNeXt (2022) | Why |
|---|---|---|
| 3×3 convs | 7×7 depthwise convs | Larger kernel, ViT-like global-ish receptive field per layer; depthwise keeps cost down |
| BatchNorm | LayerNorm | Same change ViT made; insensitive to batch size |
| ReLU | GELU | Smoother activation used in transformers |
| ReLU after the residual sum | No activation after the sum | Cleaner gradient path |
| First conv: 7×7 stride 2 + maxpool | "Patchify" stem: 4×4 stride 4 | Mimics ViT's patch embedding |
| Block: 1×1 → 3×3 → 1×1 (channels narrow then widen) | Block: 7×7 dw → 1×1 (expand 4x) → 1×1 (project back) | Inverts the bottleneck like MobileNetV2; channels expand in the middle |
| SGD with momentum, weight decay 1e-4 | AdamW, weight decay 0.05, mixup, label smoothing | Modern recipe |

## Macro architecture (slide 39)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    in[H by W by 3] --> stem[Patchify Conv 4x4 stride 4]
    stem --> s1[Stage 1 ConvNeXt Block x 3]
    s1 --> ds1[Downsample]
    ds1 --> s2[Stage 2 ConvNeXt Block x 3]
    s2 --> ds2[Downsample]
    ds2 --> s3[Stage 3 ConvNeXt Block x 9]
    s3 --> ds3[Downsample]
    ds3 --> s4[Stage 4 ConvNeXt Block x 3]
    s4 --> gap[Global AvgPool]
    gap --> ln[LayerNorm]
    ln --> head[Linear Head]
```

Same 4-stage hierarchy as ResNet, with stage block-counts ratio (3:3:9:3) tuned to match Swin's compute distribution.

## ConvNeXt block (slide 40)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    x[x 96 ch] --> dw[Depthwise Conv 7x7 96 ch]
    dw --> ln[LayerNorm]
    ln --> pw1[1x1 Conv 384 ch]
    pw1 --> g[GELU]
    g --> pw2[1x1 Conv 96 ch]
    pw2 --> add((+))
    x --> add
    add --> y[y]
```

Key differences from a ResNet bottleneck:

- Depthwise 7×7 first (spatial mixing only, very few params).
- Then 1×1 expand 4× and 1×1 project back (channel mixing, like a transformer MLP).
- Layer norm once after the depthwise, not BN after every conv.
- No ReLU after the residual addition.

## Why this matters

ConvNeXt isn't a new idea — it's a careful empirical study showing that the architectural advantage of transformers in computer vision was small once training recipes and micro-design were equalised. CNNs were not fundamentally inferior; they had simply not absorbed the new tricks.

This reframed the post-2021 landscape: **ViT vs CNN is mostly a training-recipe question, not an architecture question.** Both flavours coexist, with hybrids (Swin → MaxViT → CoAtNet) often outperforming either pure design.

## Trade-offs

- **Pro**: Production-friendly. Pure CNN means hardware-friendly, well-supported, easy to deploy.
- **Pro**: Strong on ImageNet and dense-prediction tasks; competitive with Swin Transformer at the same compute.
- **Con**: Doesn't introduce any single new idea — it's a careful refinement, not a paradigm shift.
- **Con**: Still lacks the global receptive field of full self-attention, so on very large pretrain corpora pure ViTs often pull ahead again.

## Related

- [[vision-transformer]] — the design choices being ported in
- [[swin-transformer]] — the direct comparison target
- [[residual-connection]] — kept from ResNet
- [[depthwise-separable-conv]] — the depthwise primitive used in ConvNeXt blocks
- [[batch-normalization]] — replaced here with LayerNorm

--- name: Transfer learning description: Reuse a model pretrained on a large dataset, then adapt it to a small target task — feature extraction or fine-tuning type: concept courses: [Computer-Vision] source: [17_advanced_architectures(1).pdf] created: 2026-04-26 tags: [concept, deep-learning, training] --- # Transfer learning Using a model pretrained on one task (typically ImageNet classification) as the starting point for a new, smaller task. Without it, modern computer-vision deployment for niche datasets would be impractical — a few hundred labelled examples is rarely enough to train a CNN or ViT from scratch. ## When to use A pretrained backbone almost always beats training from scratch when: - The target dataset has fewer than ~5,000 labelled images per class. - The target domain is in any way related to natural images (photos, medical scans, satellite imagery). - A relevant pretrained model exists. Train from scratch only when the target domain is fundamentally different (e.g. specific scientific instrument data) and you have plenty of labels. ## Two recipes (slides 42–46) ```mermaid flowchart LR pre[Pretrained model on ImageNet] --> q{Recipe choice} q -->|"feature extraction"| fe["Freeze backbone
train new head only"] q -->|"fine-tuning"| ft["Replace head
unfreeze some or all backbone
train at LOW learning rate"] fe --> tgt[Target task] ft --> tgt ``` ### Feature extraction - **Freeze** the convolutional / transformer trunk completely (`requires_grad = False` on all backbone parameters). - **Replace the final classifier head** with one shaped for the new task. - **Train only the head**. Treats the backbone as a fixed feature extractor — it produces a $D$-vector per image, and the small new head learns to classify that vector. Best for: very small datasets (<500/class), distant target domains, when you need fast training. ### Fine-tuning - **Replace the head** for the new task. - **Unfreeze some or all of the backbone** (often: keep the earliest layers frozen, unfreeze the last few stages). - **Train at a much lower learning rate** than from-scratch (e.g. 1e-4 vs 1e-2). The backbone weights move slightly to specialise for the new task without forgetting their pretrained features. Best for: medium datasets, moderate domain shift, when peak accuracy matters. ## Why low learning rate for fine-tuning The pretrained weights are already in a good region of weight space. A normal learning rate would drag them away — *catastrophic forgetting*. A low LR makes small adjustments and preserves the useful features. A common pattern: differential learning rates — backbone at $10^{-4}$, head at $10^{-3}$. The head needs to move faster because it starts random; the backbone needs to move slowly because it starts already useful. ## Practical checklist (slide 44) 1. **Same preprocessing** as the original training (resize, normalisation statistics). 2. **Augmentation** is even more important than usual because of low data. 3. **Pretrained model from a related domain** if available. 4. **Lower learning rate**, optionally with LR scheduling. 5. **Weight decay** keeps weights close to pretrained values. 6. **Early stopping** — overfitting is fast on small datasets. ## In PyTorch Freezing parameters (slide 45): ```python # Method 1: directly freeze for p in model[0].parameters(): p.requires_grad = False ``` Or via parameter groups (gives different LRs per layer): ```python optimizer = torch.optim.Adam([ {"params": model[0].parameters(), "lr": 0.0}, # frozen {"params": model[2].parameters(), "lr": 1e-3}, # trainable ]) ``` ResNet fine-tuning recipe (slide 46): ```python resnet18 = models.resnet18(pretrained=True) # Freeze everything except the final FC layer for name, p in resnet18.named_parameters(): if "fc" not in name: p.requires_grad = False # Replace the head num_ftrs = resnet18.fc.in_features resnet18.fc = nn.Linear(num_ftrs, num_target_classes) # Optimise only the trainable parameters optimizer = torch.optim.SGD( filter(lambda p: p.requires_grad, resnet18.parameters()), lr=1e-3, ) ``` This is **feature extraction** as written. To do fine-tuning, leave the `for` loop out (so all parameters trainable) and use a low LR for the backbone. ## Related - [[data-augmentation]] — critical when fine-tuning on small datasets - [[loss-functions]] — same losses; what changes is the head, not the loss - [[cnn-architecture]] — typical backbones to start from - [[vision-transformer]] — increasingly common as a fine-tuning starting point

--- name: Transfer learning description: Reuse a model pretrained on a large dataset, then adapt it to a small target task — feature extraction or fine-tuning type: concept courses: [Computer-Vision] source: [17_advanced_architectures(1).pdf] created: 2026-04-26 tags: [concept, deep-learning, training] --- # Transfer learning Using a model pretrained on one task (typically ImageNet classification) as the starting point for a new, smaller task. Without it, modern computer-vision deployment for niche datasets would be impractical — a few hundred labelled examples is rarely enough to train a CNN or ViT from scratch. ## When to use A pretrained backbone almost always beats training from scratch when: - The target dataset has fewer than ~5,000 labelled images per class. - The target domain is in any way related to natural images (photos, medical scans, satellite imagery). - A relevant pretrained model exists. train new head only"] q -->|"fine-tuning"| ft["Replace head

Best for: medium datasets, moderate domain shift, when peak accuracy matters. ## Why low learning rate for fine-tuning The pretrained weights are already in a good region of weight space. A normal learning rate would drag them away — *catastrophic forgetting*. A low LR makes small adjustments and preserves the useful features. ---
tags: [flashcards, review]
course: Computer-Vision
lecture: 17
source: 17_advanced_architectures(1).pdf
created: 2026-04-26
---

# Lecture 17 — Advanced architectures (flashcards)

What was VGGNet's central architectural insight?
?
Stack many small $3\times 3$ convolutions instead of using a few large kernels. Two stacked $3\times 3$ convs match the receptive field of a single $5\times 5$ conv with fewer parameters and an extra non-linearity.

Why does VGG-16 have so many parameters (~138M) despite being conceptually simple?
?
The three large fully-connected layers at the end (FC 4096 × 2 + FC 1000) account for ~120M of the 138M. The convolutional trunk is comparatively small.

What's the key idea of the Inception module?
?
Run **parallel branches** with different kernel sizes ($1\times 1$, $3\times 3$, $5\times 5$, plus pooling) on the same input, then concatenate along the channel dimension — multi-scale features in one block.

Why does Inception v1 use $1\times 1$ convolutions before the $3\times 3$ and $5\times 5$ branches?
?
As bottlenecks to reduce channel count before the expensive convolution, slashing parameter and compute cost without losing meaningful information.

What were Inception's two architectural changes that became universal beyond Inception itself?
?
1) Global Average Pooling + a single linear classifier head, replacing huge FC layers. 2) (Originally) auxiliary loss heads — but those were obsoleted by [[residual-connection]].

ResNet block: write the formula for a residual block with downsampling.
?
$y = \mathrm{ReLU}(\mathrm{Conv}_{1\times 1}(x) + \mathrm{block}(x))$. The $1\times 1$ projection on the skip path matches dimensions when the residual path downsamples (stride 2) or changes channel count.

ResNet-18 macro structure?
?
Stem: 7×7 Conv stride 2 + BN + ReLU + 3×3 MaxPool stride 2. Then four stages of residual blocks (channel widths 64, 128, 256, 512) with downsampling at each stage transition. Ends in Global AvgPool + FC.

What's the main difference between ResNet skip connections and DenseNet skip connections?
?
ResNet **adds** the skip to the residual: $y = F(x) + x$. DenseNet **concatenates** along the channel axis: $x_\ell = H_\ell([x_0, x_1, \ldots, x_{\ell-1}])$ — every layer sees all earlier feature maps.

What's the main practical drawback of DenseNet's concatenation-based connectivity?
?
Memory grows quadratically with depth — every concatenated activation is materialised. ResNet's addition uses a fixed channel count and stays cheap.

Squeeze-and-Excitation block: what are the three operations and what does it learn?
?
1) **Squeeze**: global average pool the feature map → 1D channel descriptor. 2) **Excite**: small bottleneck MLP + sigmoid → per-channel weights. 3) **Reweight**: multiply each channel by its weight. Learns input-dependent channel attention.

Write scaled dot-product attention as an equation.
?
$\text{Attention}(Q, K, V) = \mathrm{softmax}(QK^\top / \sqrt{d_k}) V$. With $Q = X W^Q$, $K = X W^K$, $V = X W^V$.

Why divide by $\sqrt{d_k}$ in scaled dot-product attention?
?
For large $d_k$, dot products grow large, pushing softmax into saturating regions where gradients vanish. Scaling by $\sqrt{d_k}$ keeps activations at unit-ish scale.

Why is self-attention permutation-equivariant, and what's the standard fix?
?
The dot products and softmax are symmetric in the ordering of keys — shuffling tokens just permutes rows/columns of the attention matrix. Fix: add positional encoding (sinusoidal, learned absolute, relative bias, or RoPE) before the first attention layer.

Memory cost of self-attention as a function of sequence length $L$.
?
$O(L^2)$ — the attention matrix is $L \times L$. Compute is $O(L^2 d)$. This quadratic scaling is the central bottleneck for long sequences and high-resolution images.

What's multi-head attention buying over single-head?
?
Each head learns a different attention pattern (one might track syntax, another coreference, another local position). Total compute is roughly the same as a single full-dim head because per-head dim is $d/h$ — you just get more diverse mixing patterns for free.

Sinusoidal positional encoding formula.
?
$\mathrm{PE}_{(\mathrm{pos}, 2i)} = \sin(\mathrm{pos} / 10000^{2i/d})$ and $\mathrm{PE}_{(\mathrm{pos}, 2i+1)} = \cos(\mathrm{pos} / 10000^{2i/d})$. Different frequencies along the embedding dim let the model resolve both fine and coarse distances.

What does Rotary Positional Embedding (RoPE) do, and why is it preferred in modern LLMs?
?
Applies a position-dependent rotation to Q and K before the dot product, so $Q'^\top K'$ implicitly depends on the **relative** position. Excellent length extrapolation and strong empirical performance — default in modern LLMs.

What two things sit inside a transformer encoder block, and how is each wrapped?
?
1) Multi-head self-attention. 2) Position-wise MLP (typically `Linear(d→4d) → GELU → Linear(4d→d)`). Each is wrapped with a residual connection and a LayerNorm.

Pre-norm vs post-norm transformer block — which is the modern default and why?
?
Pre-norm (LayerNorm before each sublayer): $x + \mathrm{Sublayer}(\mathrm{LN}(x))$. Modern default because it trains more stably for very deep transformers and needs less LR warmup.

Why do transformers use LayerNorm instead of BatchNorm?
?
Sequences are short and variable-length; transformer batches at inference are often size 1. BatchNorm's batch statistics misbehave then. LayerNorm normalises across the feature dimension within a single token — independent of batch.

Vision Transformer pipeline in five steps.
?
1) Patchify image into $P\times P$ patches. 2) Linearly project each patch to a $d$-vector. 3) Prepend a CLS token. 4) Add positional embedding. 5) Stack $L$ transformer encoder blocks; read out the CLS token through an MLP head.

What's the CLS token in ViT and what's its purpose?
?
A learnable $d$-vector with no image content prepended to the patch sequence. Self-attention pulls relevant info into it across layers, giving a single image-level summary vector for the classifier head. Alternative: mean-pool all patch outputs.

Where does ViT win over CNNs and where does it lose?
?
**Wins**: massive datasets (ImageNet-21k, JFT, LAION), where lack of inductive bias becomes an advantage. **Loses**: small datasets — without baked-in locality and translation invariance, ViT overfits or fails to learn useful spatial structure.

What's the central idea of Swin Transformer?
?
Restrict self-attention to small non-overlapping windows ($M\times M$ patches), so cost is linear (not quadratic) in image size. **Shift the window grid every other block** to enable cross-window communication. Plus hierarchical patch-merging stages for multi-scale features.

What architectural choices distinguish a ConvNeXt block from a ResNet block?
?
1) $7\times 7$ depthwise conv (vs $3\times 3$ standard). 2) LayerNorm (vs BatchNorm). 3) GELU (vs ReLU). 4) Inverted bottleneck — channels expand $4\times$ in the middle 1×1 (vs ResNet's narrow-then-widen). 5) No activation after the residual sum.

ConvNeXt's main lesson about CNNs vs ViTs?
?
The architectural advantage of ViTs in computer vision was largely about **training recipe and micro-design** (LayerNorm, GELU, AdamW, mixup, etc), not the attention mechanism itself. Once those were ported into a CNN, ResNet-style models matched Swin Transformer.

Difference between feature extraction and fine-tuning for transfer learning.
?
**Feature extraction**: freeze the backbone entirely, train only a new head. Best for very small datasets / distant domains. **Fine-tuning**: replace the head, unfreeze some/all of the backbone, train at a much lower learning rate. Best for medium datasets / closer domains.

Why use a much lower learning rate for fine-tuning than from-scratch training?
?
Pretrained weights are already in a useful region of weight space. A normal learning rate drags them away — catastrophic forgetting. A low LR (e.g. $10^{-4}$) makes small adjustments while preserving the useful features.

How do you freeze a parameter in PyTorch, and why are there often two ways used?
?
Set `param.requires_grad = False`, *or* place the layer in an optimizer parameter group with `lr=0.0`. Method 1 is simpler. Method 2 lets you give different layers different non-zero learning rates (e.g. backbone at 1e-4, head at 1e-3) — useful for fine-tuning.

In ResNet's downsampling block, why is the $1\times 1$ projection on the skip path stride 2, not stride 1?
?
The residual path also has stride 2 (to halve spatial dim). The skip path must match: stride-2 $1\times 1$ conv on the skip downsamples spatially, and using more channels matches the new depth. Without it, the addition would have shape mismatch.

content.text: ### Lecture 18 — Segmentation & object detection

### Lecture 18 — Segmentation & object detection file plan

**Lecture note:** `lecture-18-segmentation-and-detection.md`  
**New concepts (12):** `semantic-segmentation`, `fully-convolutional-network`, `unet`, `upsampling`, `transposed-convolution`, `iou`, `dice-loss`, `yolo`, `anchor-boxes`, `detr`, `hungarian-matching`, `sam`  
**Extended concepts (3):** `cv-tasks` (semantic/instance/panoptic), `non-maximum-suppression` (detection context), `loss-functions` (link to segmentation losses)  
**Flashcards:** ~30

**Flagship Mermaids:**

U-Net (slide 13):
```mermaid
flowchart LR
    in([Input image]) --> e1[Conv block 64 ch]
    e1 --> p1[MaxPool] --> e2[Conv block 128 ch]
    e2 --> p2[MaxPool] --> e3[Conv block 256 ch]
    e3 --> p3[MaxPool] --> e4[Conv block 512 ch]
    e4 --> p4[MaxPool] --> b[Bottleneck 1024 ch]
    b --> u4[Upsample] --> d4[Conv block 512 ch]
    e4 -->|skip concat| d4
    d4 --> u3[Upsample] --> d3[Conv block 256 ch]
    e3 -->|skip concat| d3
    d3 --> u2[Upsample] --> d2[Conv block 128 ch]
    e2 -->|skip concat| d2
    d2 --> u1[Upsample] --> d1[Conv block 64 ch]
    e1 -->|skip concat| d1
    d1 --> out[1x1 Conv K classes]
    out --> mask([per-pixel mask])
```

YOLO output cell (slide 26):
```mermaid
flowchart LR
    cell[Grid cell] --> y["Y = pc, bx, by, bh, bw, c1, c2"]
    y --> pc["pc: probability of object presence"]
    y --> bxy["bx, by: object centre relative to cell"]
    y --> bhw["bh, bw: box size as multiple of anchor"]
    y --> cls["c1, c2: class probabilities"]
```

DETR pipeline (slide 33):
```mermaid
flowchart LR
    img([Image]) --> cnn[CNN backbone] --> feat[Feature map]
    feat --> enc[Transformer encoder-decoder]
    enc --> set["Set of N predictions: c_i, b_i"]
    set --> match[Hungarian bipartite matching]
    gt([Ground truth]) --> match
    match --> loss[Set-based loss: CE + L1 + IoU]
```

Writing files now.

content.newText:
--- tags: [lecture] course: Computer-Vision source: 18_segmentation_and_object_detection.pdf lecture: 18 created: 2026-04-26 --- # Lecture 18 — Deep learning for segmentation and object detection ## Overview Two computer-vision tasks where the output is structured (per-pixel mask, or a set of bounding boxes) — not just a class label. Both require new architectural ideas beyond the classifier-style CNN. 1. **Task taxonomy** — semantic, instance, panoptic segmentation; object detection. 2. **Datasets** — CelebA, Pascal VOC, Cityscapes, ImageNet, COCO, Open Images. 3. **Naive segmentation** — sliding window classifier per pixel (correct but extremely slow). 4. **Fully convolutional networks (FCN)** — no FC layers; conv at every layer; output is a $K\times L\times L$ map. 5. **Encoder-decoder architectures** — contracting (downsample) then expanding (upsample) paths. 6. **Upsampling primitives** — nearest neighbour, bed of nails, max-unpooling, transposed convolution. 7. **U-Net** — encoder-decoder + skip connections from encoder to decoder. 8. **Segmentation metrics & losses** — IoU, Dice; pixel-wise cross-entropy (weighted for class imbalance), Dice loss, boundary losses. 9. **Object detection** — single-stage (YOLO, DETR) vs two-stage (Faster R-CNN). 10. **YOLO** — grid cells, per-cell box prediction $Y = (p_c, b_x, b_y, b_h, b_w, c_1, c_2, \ldots)$, multi-anchor, NMS. 11. **DETR** — set prediction with a transformer; Hungarian bipartite matching for label assignment. 12. **SAM** — promptable segmentation foundation model trained on 1B+ masks. ## Key concepts - [[cv-tasks]] — overall task taxonomy (extended this lecture) - [[semantic-segmentation]] — semantic / instance / panoptic - [[fully-convolutional-network]] — pure-conv segmentation network - [[upsampling]] — nearest neighbour, bed of nails, unpooling, transposed conv - [[transposed-convolution]] — learnable upsampling primitive - [[unet]] — encoder-decoder + skip connections - [[iou]] — Intersection over Union metric (segmentation and detection) - [[dice-loss]] — Dice coefficient and its loss form - [[yolo]] — single-stage real-time detector - [[anchor-boxes]] — pre-defined shapes for multi-object grid cells - [[non-maximum-suppression]] — deduplicate overlapping boxes (extended this lecture) - [[detr]] — set-prediction detector - [[hungarian-matching]] — bipartite optimal assignment for set losses - [[sam]] — Segment Anything Model ## Equations ### Pixel-wise cross-entropy (slide 15) For predicted probability $P_{ijk}$ of class $k$ at pixel $(i, j)$ and one-hot ground truth $y_{ijk}$: $$\mathrm{CE} = -\sum_{k=1}^{K}\sum_{i=1}^{I}\sum_{j=1}^{J} y_{ijk}\, \log P_{ijk}$$ Weighted variant for class imbalance (most pixels are background): $$\mathrm{wCE} = -\sum_{k=1}^{K} \frac{1}{|y_{ijk} = k| + \epsilon}\sum_{i, j} y_{ijk}\, \log P_{ijk}$$ ### Intersection over Union (slide 16) $$\mathrm{IoU} = \frac{|\text{prediction} \cap \text{ground truth}|}{|\text{prediction} \cup \text{ground truth}|} = \frac{|A \cap B|}{|A| + |B| - |A \cap B|}$$ ### Dice coefficient (slide 18) $$\mathrm{Dice} = \frac{2\,|A \cap B|}{|A| + |B|},\qquad \mathcal{L}_\text{Dice} = 1 - \mathrm{Dice}$$ ### YOLO loss function (slide 27) $$\mathcal{L} = \lambda_\text{coord}\sum_{i, j} \mathbb{1}^\text{obj}_{i,j}\!\left[(x_i - \hat{x}_i)^2 + (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2\right] + \lambda_\text{coord}\sum_{i, j}\mathbb{1}^\text{obj}_{i,j}\!\left[(\sqrt{w_i} - \sqrt{\hat{w}_i})^2 + (\sqrt{h_i} - \sqrt{\hat{h}_i})^2\right]$$ $$+ \sum_{i, j}\mathbb{1}^\text{obj}_{i,j}(C_i - \hat{C}_i)^2 + \lambda_\text{noobj}\sum_{i, j}\mathbb{1}^\text{noobj}_{i,j}(C_i - \hat{C}_i)^2 + \sum_i \mathbb{1}^\text{obj}_i \sum_{c}(p_i(c) - \hat{p}_i(c))^2$$ Three components: bbox regression (squared coordinates and squared roots of size), object presence/absence, and class probability. ## Diagrams ### Segmentation taxonomy (slide 6) ```mermaid flowchart TD img([Input image]) --> q1{Distinguish
instances?} q1 -->|"no"| sem[Semantic segmentation] q1 -->|"yes"| q2{Cover all pixels
or just things?} q2 -->|"things only"| inst[Instance segmentation] q2 -->|"all pixels things+stuff"| pan[Panoptic segmentation] sem -.-> nsem[same class same colour] inst -.-> ninst[two cats get different IDs
background ignored] pan -.-> npan[two cats different IDs
plus sky water sand classes] ``` "Things" = countable foreground objects (people, cars). "Stuff" = uncountable regions (sky, road, grass). ### Naive sliding-window vs FCN (slides 7–9) ```mermaid flowchart LR naive[Naive: extract patch, run CNN, classify centre pixel, repeat] naive --> bad[O M times N forward passes per image] fcn[FCN: pure conv all the way through] fcn --> good[One forward pass, output is K times L times L] ``` The FCN insight: a CNN with no FC layers naturally outputs a feature map at every layer. Make the final layer a $1\times 1$ conv with $K$ output channels and you get a per-pixel class score. No downsampling means same resolution end-to-end — but receptive field grows slowly and compute is high. ### Encoder-decoder pattern (slide 10) ```mermaid flowchart LR in([3 by L by L]) --> c1[Conv] c1 --> p1[Pool] p1 --> c2[Conv] c2 --> p2[Pool] p2 --> bottle[Bottleneck] bottle --> u1[Upsample] u1 --> c3[Conv] c3 --> u2[Upsample] u2 --> c4[Conv] c4 --> out([K by L by L]) ``` Contracting path: classic CNN, large receptive field, low resolution. Expanding path: upsample back to original resolution. Solves the receptive-field problem of pure-FCN designs. ### Upsampling alternatives (slides 11–12) ```mermaid flowchart LR in[2 by 2 input] --> nn[Nearest neighbour: replicate] in --> bn["Bed of nails: place value, zero rest"] in --> mu[Max-unpool: place value at stored argmax index, zero rest] in --> tc[Transposed conv: learnable upsampling] nn --> nndesc[smooth, parameter-free] bn --> bndesc[fast, sparse] mu --> mudesc[needs argmax indices from earlier MaxPool] tc --> tcdesc[learns the upsample pattern, common in U-Net] ``` Transposed convolution: each input pixel scatters its kernel-weighted contribution into the output, summing contributions from neighbours. Hyperparameters (kernel, stride, padding) are exact analogues of conv (see [[transposed-convolution]]). ### U-Net (slide 13) ```mermaid flowchart LR in([Input image]) --> e1[Conv block 64 ch] e1 --> p1[MaxPool] p1 --> e2[Conv block 128 ch] e2 --> p2[MaxPool] p2 --> e3[Conv block 256 ch] e3 --> p3[MaxPool] p3 --> e4[Conv block 512 ch] e4 --> p4[MaxPool] p4 --> b[Bottleneck 1024 ch] b --> u4[Upsample] u4 --> d4[Conv block 512 ch] e4 -->|"skip concat"| d4 d4 --> u3[Upsample] u3 --> d3[Conv block 256 ch] e3 -->|"skip concat"| d3 d3 --> u2[Upsample] u2 --> d2[Conv block 128 ch] e2 -->|"skip concat"| d2 d2 --> u1[Upsample] u1 --> d1[Conv block 64 ch] e1 -->|"skip concat"| d1 d1 --> out[1x1 Conv K classes] out --> mask([per-pixel mask]) ``` The skip connections from encoder to decoder pass *high-resolution detail* across the network. The decoder's upsampling supplies *semantic context* from deep features. Concatenating them combines both — essential for segmentation where pixel-level boundaries matter. ### Single-stage vs two-stage detectors (slide 22) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph two[Two-stage Faster R-CNN] i1[Image] --> rp[Region Proposal Network] rp --> roi["RoI pool / align"] roi --> cls[Classifier + bbox refiner] end subgraph one[Single-stage YOLO DETR] i2[Image] --> det[Direct prediction] det --> out[Boxes + classes in one pass] end two -.-> tdesc[More accurate, slower] one -.-> odesc[Faster, real-time] ``` ### YOLO grid prediction (slides 23–28) ```mermaid flowchart LR img([Image]) --> grid[Divide into S by S grid] grid --> per[Per cell: predict B anchor boxes] per --> y["Y per box: pc, bx, by, bh, bw, c1, c2..."] y --> raw[Raw predictions] raw --> nms[NMS to remove overlaps] nms --> final[Final detections] ``` | Field | Meaning | |---|---| | $p_c$ | probability that an object centre falls in this cell | | $b_x, b_y$ | object centre relative to the cell (in [0, 1]) | | $b_h, b_w$ | box size as a multiple of anchor dimensions | | $c_1, c_2, \ldots$ | class probabilities | Multiple anchors per cell let one cell predict multiple objects of different shapes. Each ground-truth object is assigned to the anchor with the best IoU. ### DETR pipeline (slide 33) ```mermaid flowchart LR img([Image]) --> cnn[CNN backbone] cnn --> feat[Feature map] feat --> enc[Transformer encoder-decoder] enc --> set["Set of N box predictions"] set --> match[Hungarian bipartite matching] gt([Ground truth boxes]) --> match match --> loss["Loss: classification CE + L1 + IoU"] ``` DETR replaces the anchor + grid-cell + NMS pipeline with **direct set prediction**: $N$ slots, each predicting a class + box. The Hungarian algorithm matches each predicted slot to one ground-truth box (or "no object") at training time so the loss can be computed. NMS becomes unnecessary because the set is already deduplicated by construction. ### Loss landscape for segmentation (slide 14) | Family | Examples | When to use | |---|---|---| | Distribution-based | Pixel-wise CE, weighted CE, focal | default; adjust weights for class imbalance | | Region-based | IoU/Jaccard, Dice, Tversky, Lovasz | imbalanced data, small targets (medical) | | Boundary-based | Boundary loss, HD loss | when crisp object edges matter | | Compound | DiceFocal, Combo, DiceTopK | strong baselines combining the above | ## Open questions - Why does U-Net's skip connection use **concatenation** rather than ResNet-style addition? What does each preserve? - DETR removes NMS and anchors — does the Hungarian matching cost during training outweigh the inference simplification? - SAM is trained on 1B+ masks. How transferable is a "segment anything" prior to specialised domains (medical, satellite)? ## Connections to other lectures - [[cnn-architecture]] (lecture 16) is the encoder backbone. - [[residual-connection]] (lecture 17) appears inside many U-Net blocks (Residual U-Net) and detector heads. - [[transformer-encoder]] (lecture 17) is the body of DETR; [[vision-transformer]] is the basis of MaskFormer / SAM image encoders. - Image moments and structure tensor (lecture 12–13) are the *hand-engineered* analogue of segmentation — same task, different approach.

content.newText:
---
name: Semantic segmentation
description: Per-pixel classification — output is one class label per pixel, no instance distinction
type: concept
courses: [Computer-Vision]
source: [18_segmentation_and_object_detection.pdf]
created: 2026-04-26
tags: [concept, deep-learning, computer-vision]
---

# Semantic segmentation

The task of assigning a class label to **every pixel** of an image. Output shape: $K \times H \times W$ (per-class score map) → argmax → $H \times W$ class-id map.

## Three flavours of segmentation (slide 6)

| Variant | Two cats in one image | Sky / road labelled? |
|---|---|---|
| **Semantic** | both labelled "cat" — same colour | yes (sky, road, etc. labelled) |
| **Instance** | two distinct IDs (cat 1, cat 2) | no — only "things," not "stuff" |
| **Panoptic** | two distinct IDs *and* sky/road labelled | yes (things + stuff together) |

"Things" = countable foreground objects (people, cars, animals).  
"Stuff" = uncountable amorphous regions (sky, road, grass).

Some authors blur "instance" and "panoptic"; treat both as instance-aware.

## Naive approach: sliding window (slide 7)

For every pixel $(i, j)$:
1. Extract a patch centred at $(i, j)$.
2. Run a CNN classifier on the patch.
3. Output the predicted class as the label for that pixel.

Correct, but extremely slow — $O(HW)$ forward passes per image. Adjacent patches share most pixels, so 99% of the compute is duplicated.

## Better: fully convolutional (slide 8–9)

A CNN with **no fully-connected layers** naturally produces a feature map at every layer. The final layer is a $1 \times 1$ conv with $K$ output channels — one per class — giving $K \times H \times W$ output. Argmax over the channel dimension at each pixel produces the class map.

See [[fully-convolutional-network]].

The catch: keeping spatial resolution constant throughout means the receptive field grows slowly (only by $k - 1$ per conv layer). To see large objects you need either many layers (expensive) or downsampling.

## Best: encoder-decoder (slide 10)

Downsample first to grow receptive field cheaply, then upsample back to original resolution to recover pixel-level detail. See [[unet]] and [[upsampling]].

## Standard losses

- Pixel-wise [[loss-functions|cross-entropy]] (and weighted variants for class imbalance — most pixels are background).
- [[dice-loss]] for imbalanced datasets / small target regions.
- Boundary losses for crisp edges.

## Standard metrics

- [[iou]] (per-class), then mean IoU across classes.
- Pixel accuracy (rarely useful alone — biased by background majority class).
- Dice score per class.

## Datasets

- **Pascal VOC** — 20 classes, classic benchmark.
- **Cityscapes** — urban driving scenes; 19 classes, finely annotated.
- **COCO-Stuff** — 80 thing + 91 stuff classes.
- **ADE20K** — 150 classes, indoor + outdoor.

## Related

- [[cv-tasks]] — overall taxonomy
- [[unet]] — workhorse architecture
- [[fully-convolutional-network]] — the foundational design
- [[iou]], [[dice-loss]] — metrics and losses
- [[sam]] — promptable foundation model for segmentation

---
name: Fully Convolutional Network (FCN)
description: CNN with no fully-connected layers — produces a per-pixel score map for dense prediction tasks
type: concept
courses: [Computer-Vision]
source: [18_segmentation_and_object_detection.pdf]
created: 2026-04-26
tags: [concept, deep-learning, computer-vision, segmentation]
---

# Fully Convolutional Network (FCN)

A CNN where **every layer is convolutional** — no fully-connected layers anywhere. The output is a feature map of shape $K \times H' \times W'$ rather than a class-score vector. Used for dense-prediction tasks like [[semantic-segmentation]].

## The idea (slide 8)

A standard classifier ends with `Flatten → FC → softmax`. The Flatten step couples the model to a fixed input size and discards spatial structure. An FCN replaces the FC head with a $1 \times 1$ conv that maps from the final feature map's $C$ channels to $K$ classes:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    in([3 by L by L]) --> c1[Conv 3xCxWxW]
    c1 --> c2[Conv CxCxWxW]
    c2 --> dots["..."]
    dots --> cn[Conv CxCxWxW]
    cn --> head["1x1 conv CxKx1x1"]
    head --> ar["argmax over channels"]
    ar --> out([K class map L by L])
```

The final $1 \times 1$ conv is **equivalent to a per-pixel linear classifier** on the feature vector at that pixel — applied independently and in parallel to every spatial location.

## Why this is much better than sliding window

Compared to running a CNN classifier on each pixel's window separately:

- **One forward pass** instead of $O(HW)$.
- All spatial work is shared via conv.
- Roughly 100×–1000× faster.

## The downside of "no downsampling" (slide 9)

If every layer is conv with stride 1 and padding to keep size, the spatial dim stays at $L \times L$ throughout. Then:

- Receptive field grows only by $k - 1$ per layer. To see large objects (e.g. a 100-pixel-wide car) you'd need ~50 layers.
- Compute per layer is proportional to $L^2 \cdot C^2 \cdot k^2$ — expensive for high-res images.

This is why pure FCN designs are rare in practice — they're superseded by encoder-decoder architectures like [[unet]] that downsample first to grow the receptive field cheaply, then upsample back to the input resolution.

## Where pure FCN still appears

- As the *concept* — most modern segmentation models are still "fully convolutional" in the sense that they have no FC layers, even if they use encoder-decoder structure.
- The original FCN paper (Long et al, 2015) used a VGG-style backbone with stride-1 final layers and bilinear upsampling — the proof-of-concept that defined the era.
- ConvNeXt-style classification stems (without an explicit decoder) when the resolution requirement is modest.

- [[unet]] — the encoder-decoder evolution

---
name: Fully Convolutional Network (FCN)
description: CNN with no fully-connected layers — produces a per-pixel score map for dense prediction tasks
type: concept
courses: [Computer-Vision]
source: [18_segmentation_and_object_detection.pdf]
created: 2026-04-26
tags: [concept, deep-learning, computer-vision, segmentation]
---

# Fully Convolutional Network (FCN)

A CNN where **every layer is convolutional** — no fully-connected layers anywhere. The output is a feature map of shape $K \times H' \times W'$ rather than a class-score vector. Used for dense-prediction tasks like [[semantic-segmentation]].

## The idea (slide 8)

A standard classifier ends with `Flatten → FC → softmax`. The Flatten step couples the model to a fixed input size and discards spatial structure. An FCN replaces the FC head with a $1 \times 1$ conv that maps from the final feature map's $C$ channels to $K$ classes:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    in([3 by L by L]) --> c1[Conv 3xCxWxW]
    c1 --> c2[Conv CxCxWxW]
    c2 --> dots["..."]
    dots --> cn[Conv CxCxWxW]
    cn --> head["1x1 conv CxKx1x1"]
    head --> ar["argmax over channels"]
    ar --> out([K class map L by L])
```

The final $1 \times 1$ conv is **equivalent to a per-pixel linear classifier** on the feature vector at that pixel — applied independently and in parallel to every spatial location.

## Why this is much better than sliding window

Compared to running a CNN classifier on each pixel's window separately:

- **One forward pass** instead of $O(HW)$.
- All spatial work is shared via conv.
- Roughly 100×–1000× faster.

## The downside of "no downsampling" (slide 9)

If every layer is conv with stride 1 and padding to keep size, the spatial dim stays at $L \times L$ throughout. Then:

- Receptive field grows only by $k - 1$ per layer. To see large objects (e.g. a 100-pixel-wide car) you'd need ~50 layers.
- Compute per layer is proportional to $L^2 \cdot C^2 \cdot k^2$ — expensive for high-res images.

This is why pure FCN designs are rare in practice — they're superseded by encoder-decoder architectures like [[unet]] that downsample first to grow the receptive field cheaply, then upsample back to the input resolution.

## Where pure FCN still appears

- As the *concept* — most modern segmentation models are still "fully convolutional" in the sense that they have no FC layers, even if they use encoder-decoder structure.
- The original FCN paper (Long et al, 2015) used a VGG-style backbone with stride-1 final layers and bilinear upsampling — the proof-of-concept that defined the era.
- ConvNeXt-style classification stems (without an explicit decoder) when the resolution requirement is modest.

## Related

- [[semantic-segmentation]] — the canonical task FCN is designed for
- [[unet]] — the encoder-decoder evolution
- [[transposed-convolution]] — the upsampling primitive that decoder paths use
- [[cnn-architecture]] — the trunk an FCN is built on

---
name: Upsampling
description: Spatial upscaling primitives for decoder paths — nearest neighbour, bed of nails, max-unpooling, transposed convolution
type: concept
courses: [Computer-Vision]
source: [18_segmentation_and_object_detection.pdf]
created: 2026-04-26
tags: [concept, deep-learning, segmentation]
---

# Upsampling

A family of operations that grow a feature map's spatial size — the inverse of pooling / strided conv. Decoder paths in encoder-decoder architectures (e.g. [[unet]]) need upsampling to recover the original input resolution after the encoder has downsampled.

## Four primitives (slides 11–12)

| Method | Idea | Learnable | Typical use |
|---|---|---|---|
| Nearest neighbour | Replicate each input pixel into a $k \times k$ block | no | cheap, often combined with a conv layer |
| Bilinear interpolation | Linearly interpolate between input pixels | no | smoother than nearest neighbour |
| Bed of nails | Place each input pixel, fill rest with zeros | no | rarely useful directly; sparse |
| Max-unpooling | Place each input pixel at the index where MaxPool found its max; zeros elsewhere | no | needs argmax indices stored from the matching pool layer |
| Transposed convolution | Each input pixel scatters its kernel-weighted contribution into the output | yes | learned, expressive; default in U-Net decoders |

## Visual reference

```
Input 2x2:
1 2
3 4

Nearest neighbour 4x4:
1 1 2 2
1 1 2 2
3 3 4 4
3 3 4 4

Bed of nails 4x4:
1 0 2 0
0 0 0 0
3 0 4 0
0 0 0 0
```

Max-unpooling places each value at the (i, j) it came from in the matching pool layer:
```
input    indices from earlier MaxPool   output
9 6      (1,1) (0,2)                    0 0 6 0
9 9      (3,1) (3,2)                    0 9 0 0
                                        0 0 0 0
                                        0 9 9 0
```

## Transposed convolution

The "smart" upsample. Each input value is multiplied by the entire kernel and added (with stride-defined offset) into the output map:

For a $2\times 2$ input and $2\times 2$ kernel with stride 1:

- pixel 1 contributes `kernel * 1` placed at top-left.
- pixel 2 contributes `kernel * 2` placed one step right.
- pixel 3 contributes `kernel * 3` placed one step down.
- pixel 4 contributes `kernel * 4` placed one step right and down.

Sum the four placements to get the output. Output size is larger than input — see [[transposed-convolution]] for the formal mechanics and PyTorch usage.

The kernel is **learned**, so transposed conv can adapt the upsampling to the task (sharper edges, smoother gradients, etc.).

## Choosing between them

| Goal | Choice |
|---|---|
| Cheap and reasonable | nearest neighbour upsample + conv |
| Use exact pool locations | max-unpool (requires `return_indices=True` on the pool) |
| Learnable, default for U-Net | transposed conv |
| Avoid checkerboard artefacts | nearest neighbour upsample + conv (or transposed conv with kernel size divisible by stride) |

The "nearest + conv" combo (sometimes called "resize-conv") is increasingly preferred over transposed conv in modern segmentation models because transposed conv with stride > 1 can produce checkerboard artefacts that look like grid patterns in the output.

## Related

- [[transposed-convolution]] — the learnable variant in detail
- [[pooling]] — the inverse operation in the encoder
- [[unet]] — heaviest user of upsampling
- [[fully-convolutional-network]] — the original (no-upsample) alternative

content.newText:
---
name: Transposed convolution
description: Learnable upsampling — each input value scatters its kernel-weighted contribution into a larger output map
type: concept
courses: [Computer-Vision]
source: [18_segmentation_and_object_detection.pdf]
created: 2026-04-26
tags: [concept, deep-learning, segmentation]
---

# Transposed convolution

A convolutional layer that **upsamples** rather than downsamples. The output is **larger** than the input. The learnable kernel weights the contribution each input value scatters into the output map, then overlapping contributions are summed.

Also called: deconvolution (misnomer — not a true mathematical inverse), fractionally-strided convolution, up-conv.

## Mechanics (slide 12)

For a $2\times 2$ input and $2\times 2$ kernel with stride 1, padding 0:

```
Input          Kernel
1 2            a b
3 4            c d
```

Each input pixel multiplies the entire kernel and places the result at the corresponding offset in the output:

- Pixel 1 → place `1·[[a,b],[c,d]]` at top-left.
- Pixel 2 → place `2·[[a,b],[c,d]]` one step right.
- Pixel 3 → place `3·[[a,b],[c,d]]` one step down.
- Pixel 4 → place `4·[[a,b],[c,d]]` one step right and down.

Sum all four placements element-wise to get a $3\times 3$ output. With kernel $[[1,2],[3,4]]$ as both input and kernel, this gives:

```
 1   4   4
 6  20  16
 9  24  16
```

## Output size formula

For input $L_\text{in}$, kernel $k$, stride $s$, padding $p$, output padding $p'$:

$$L_\text{out} = (L_\text{in} - 1)\,s - 2p + k + p'$$

Compare with regular conv: $L_\text{out} = \lfloor (L_\text{in} + 2p - k)/s + 1\rfloor$. Transposed conv "inverts" the size formula.

For doubling: stride 2, kernel 2, padding 0 → $L_\text{out} = 2 L_\text{in}$.  
For doubling without checkerboard: stride 2, kernel 4, padding 1 → $L_\text{out} = 2 L_\text{in}$.

## Why "transposed"

A regular conv layer can be expressed as a sparse matrix $C$ that multiplies a flattened input. The transpose $C^\top$ implements the upsampling pattern of transposed convolution — same weights, different connectivity. Hence the name.

This also explains why backprop through a regular conv has the same shape as a transposed conv on the gradient: the chain-rule transpose pattern is exactly transposed convolution.

## Checkerboard artefacts

When stride > 1 and kernel size is not divisible by stride, the output has uneven sums of contributions across positions — some pixels are written into by 1 kernel placement, others by 4. This produces visible grid / checkerboard patterns.

Mitigations:
- Use kernel size divisible by stride (e.g. stride 2 → kernel 4).
- Replace transposed conv with **resize + conv**: nearest-neighbour upsample, then a stride-1 conv. The conv learns the same kind of refinement without the artefact.

## In PyTorch

```python
nn.ConvTranspose2d(
    in_channels, out_channels, kernel_size,
    stride=1, padding=0, output_padding=0,
)
```

Common pattern in U-Net decoder:

```python
self.up = nn.ConvTranspose2d(in_ch, out_ch, kernel_size=2, stride=2)
```

Doubles spatial size, halves channels (typical decoder convention).

## When to use vs alternatives

- **Use** when you want learnable upsampling with end-to-end gradients.
- **Alternative** in modern code: `nn.Upsample(scale_factor=2, mode='nearest')` followed by a normal `Conv2d` — same expressive power, no checkerboard, slightly more compute.

## Related

- [[upsampling]] — overview of all upsampling methods
- [[convolution-operation]] — the downsampling counterpart
- [[unet]] — heaviest user
- [[padding-stride]] — same hyperparameters apply

content.newText:
---
name: U-Net
description: Encoder-decoder segmentation architecture with skip connections from encoder to decoder — the workhorse of medical and dense-prediction tasks
type: concept
courses: [Computer-Vision]
source: [18_segmentation_and_object_detection.pdf]
created: 2026-04-26
tags: [concept, deep-learning, segmentation, architecture]
---

# U-Net

A 2015 architecture (Ronneberger et al, originally for biomedical image segmentation) that has become the default for any dense per-pixel prediction task. The name comes from its U-shape: a **contracting path** (encoder) and an **expansive path** (decoder), with **skip connections** linking them at matching resolutions.

## Architecture (slide 13)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    in([Input image]) --> e1[Conv block 64 ch]
    e1 --> p1[MaxPool]
    p1 --> e2[Conv block 128 ch]
    e2 --> p2[MaxPool]
    p2 --> e3[Conv block 256 ch]
    e3 --> p3[MaxPool]
    p3 --> e4[Conv block 512 ch]
    e4 --> p4[MaxPool]
    p4 --> b[Bottleneck 1024 ch]
    b --> u4[Upsample]
    u4 --> d4[Conv block 512 ch]
    e4 -->|"skip concat"| d4
    d4 --> u3[Upsample]
    u3 --> d3[Conv block 256 ch]
    e3 -->|"skip concat"| d3
    d3 --> u2[Upsample]
    u2 --> d2[Conv block 128 ch]
    e2 -->|"skip concat"| d2
    d2 --> u1[Upsample]
    u1 --> d1[Conv block 64 ch]
    e1 -->|"skip concat"| d1
    d1 --> out[1x1 Conv K classes]
    out --> mask([per-pixel mask])
```

Each "Conv block" is typically two 3×3 convs with BatchNorm and ReLU. Pooling halves spatial size and doubles channels. Upsampling does the inverse (often via [[transposed-convolution]] or nearest-neighbour + conv).

## Skip connections: encoder → decoder

This is the central innovation. At each decoder level, before applying the conv block, concatenate (along the channel dimension) the matching encoder feature map.

| Path | What it provides |
|---|---|
| Decoder upsample (from below) | Deep semantic features ("this region is a person") with poor spatial precision |
| Encoder skip (from same level) | High-resolution detail ("the boundary is here, not 2 pixels away") with shallow semantics |
| Concatenation | Both at once — semantic + spatial detail |

Without skip connections, decoder output is blurry. With them, edges align cleanly with the input.

## Why concatenation, not addition

ResNet uses additive skips ([[residual-connection]]). U-Net uses concatenation. Why?

- **Addition** assumes the two paths carry compatible semantic content that should be summed. True for residual blocks where both paths come from nearby layers.
- **Concatenation** preserves both paths separately so the next conv layer can *choose* how to combine them. Important here because the encoder and decoder paths carry very different things (raw detail vs deep semantics) — forcing them into a sum would mix and destroy.

## Variants

- **Residual U-Net** — replace the conv blocks with residual blocks; better gradient flow.
- **Attention U-Net** — gate the skip connections with attention to suppress irrelevant encoder activations.
- **3D U-Net** — replace 2D convs with 3D convs for volumetric data (CT, MRI).
- **nnU-Net** (slide 37) — automatic configuration: detects dataset properties, picks patch size / architecture / preprocessing automatically. State-of-the-art on most medical datasets.
- **TransUNet / Swin-UNet** — replace the encoder with a transformer.

## Why it dominates medical imaging

- Works well with **small datasets** (CNN inductive bias).
- Skip connections preserve the fine boundaries clinicians need.
- nnU-Net's automatic configuration removes most of the hyperparameter tuning that other architectures require.

## Related

- [[encoder-decoder]] (concept covered inline here) — the general pattern
- [[fully-convolutional-network]] — the simpler precursor
- [[upsampling]], [[transposed-convolution]] — decoder primitives
- [[semantic-segmentation]] — primary task
- [[dice-loss]] — usually paired with U-Net
- [[residual-connection]] — additive skip alternative

content.newText:
---
name: Intersection over Union (IoU)
description: Overlap-based metric for boxes and masks — area of overlap divided by area of union; 0 to 1
type: concept
courses: [Computer-Vision]
source: [18_segmentation_and_object_detection.pdf]
created: 2026-04-26
tags: [concept, deep-learning, metric, segmentation, detection]
---

# Intersection over Union (IoU)

Also called the **Jaccard index**. Measures overlap between two sets — typically a predicted region (box or mask) and a ground-truth region.

## Definition (slide 16)

$$\mathrm{IoU} = \frac{|A \cap B|}{|A \cup B|} = \frac{|A \cap B|}{|A| + |B| - |A \cap B|}$$

Range: $[0, 1]$. 0 = disjoint, 1 = identical.

| Configuration | IoU |
|---|---|
| No overlap | 0 |
| Slight overlap | ~0.14 |
| Substantial overlap (half each) | ~0.33 |
| Perfect match | 1 |

## Boxes vs masks

Same definition, different intersection/union computation:

- **Bounding boxes**: $|A \cap B|$ is the area of the rectangular overlap; $|A|, |B|$ are box areas.
- **Pixel masks**: $|A|$ is the count of foreground pixels in the predicted mask; intersection and union are computed on the binary masks.

## In PyTorch (slide 17)

```python
pred_mask = (pred_probs > THR)   # binarise predictions, e.g. THR = 0.5
intersection = torch.sum(pred_mask * true_mask, dim=(-1, -2))
union = (
    torch.sum(pred_mask, dim=(-1, -2))
    + torch.sum(true_mask, dim=(-1, -2))
    - intersection
)
iou = (intersection + smooth) / (union + smooth)   # smooth ~1e-6
```

The `smooth` term avoids 0/0 when both prediction and ground truth are empty (no objects in this image).

## Properties and pitfalls

- **Not differentiable** as written (the threshold breaks gradients). Used as a *metric*, not a loss. For a differentiable loss, use [[dice-loss]] or one of the soft-IoU variants.
- **One IoU per class**. To summarise a multi-class segmentation, compute per-class IoU then average → **mean IoU (mIoU)**.
- **Sensitive to small objects**. A 1-pixel offset on a 5×5 object drops IoU by 30%; on a 100×100 object by 0.1%. Tiny structures are harshly penalised — useful for detection, can be misleading in segmentation of fine elongated structures.

## Use cases

- **Detection**: IoU between predicted box and GT box; "true positive" usually means IoU > 0.5 (or 0.75, 0.9 for tighter benchmarks).
- **Segmentation**: per-class IoU on the predicted mask vs GT mask.
- **NMS**: deduplicating overlapping boxes uses pairwise IoU (see [[non-maximum-suppression]]).
- **Tracking**: associating detections frame-to-frame.

## Variants

- **GIoU** (Generalised IoU): adds a penalty for the smallest enclosing box, fixing the gradient when boxes don't overlap.
- **DIoU / CIoU**: include centre-distance and aspect-ratio terms — used as bounding-box regression losses in modern detectors.
- **mAP** (mean Average Precision): aggregates IoU thresholds and confidence rankings — the de facto detection metric.

## Related

- [[dice-loss]] — closely related; differentiable and softer
- [[non-maximum-suppression]] — uses IoU
- [[yolo]] — uses IoU thresholds for anchor assignment
- [[detr]] — uses IoU in the matching cost
