---
name: Intersection over Union (IoU)
description: Overlap-based metric for boxes and masks — area of overlap divided by area of union; 0 to 1
type: concept
courses: [Computer-Vision]
source: [18_segmentation_and_object_detection.pdf]
created: 2026-04-26
tags: [concept, deep-learning, metric, segmentation, detection]
---

# Intersection over Union (IoU)

Also called the **Jaccard index**. Measures overlap between two sets — typically a predicted region (box or mask) and a ground-truth region.

## Definition (slide 16)

$$\mathrm{IoU} = \frac{|A \cap B|}{|A \cup B|} = \frac{|A \cap B|}{|A| + |B| - |A \cap B|}$$

Range: $[0, 1]$. 0 = disjoint, 1 = identical.

| Configuration | IoU |
|---|---|
| No overlap | 0 |
| Slight overlap | ~0.14 |
| Substantial overlap (half each) | ~0.33 |
| Perfect match | 1 |

## Boxes vs masks

Same definition, different intersection/union computation:

- **Bounding boxes**: $|A \cap B|$ is the area of the rectangular overlap; $|A|, |B|$ are box areas.
- **Pixel masks**: $|A|$ is the count of foreground pixels in the predicted mask; intersection and union are computed on the binary masks.

## In PyTorch (slide 17)

```python
pred_mask = (pred_probs > THR)   # binarise predictions, e.g. THR = 0.5
intersection = torch.sum(pred_mask * true_mask, dim=(-1, -2))
union = (
    torch.sum(pred_mask, dim=(-1, -2))
    + torch.sum(true_mask, dim=(-1, -2))
    - intersection
)
iou = (intersection + smooth) / (union + smooth)   # smooth ~1e-6
```

The `smooth` term avoids 0/0 when both prediction and ground truth are empty (no objects in this image).

## Properties and pitfalls

- **Not differentiable** as written (the threshold breaks gradients). Used as a *metric*, not a loss. For a differentiable loss, use [[dice-loss]] or one of the soft-IoU variants.
- **One IoU per class**. To summarise a multi-class segmentation, compute per-class IoU then average → **mean IoU (mIoU)**.
- **Sensitive to small objects**. A 1-pixel offset on a 5×5 object drops IoU by 30%; on a 100×100 object by 0.1%. Tiny structures are harshly penalised — useful for detection, can be misleading in segmentation of fine elongated structures.

## Use cases

- **Detection**: IoU between predicted box and GT box; "true positive" usually means IoU > 0.5 (or 0.75, 0.9 for tighter benchmarks).
- **Segmentation**: per-class IoU on the predicted mask vs GT mask.
- **NMS**: deduplicating overlapping boxes uses pairwise IoU (see [[non-maximum-suppression]]).
- **Tracking**: associating detections frame-to-frame.

## Variants

- **GIoU** (Generalised IoU): adds a penalty for the smallest enclosing box, fixing the gradient when boxes don't overlap.
- **DIoU / CIoU**: include centre-distance and aspect-ratio terms — used as bounding-box regression losses in modern detectors.
- **mAP** (mean Average Precision): aggregates IoU thresholds and confidence rankings — the de facto detection metric.

## Related

- [[dice-loss]] — closely related; differentiable and softer
- [[non-maximum-suppression]] — uses IoU
- [[yolo]] — uses IoU thresholds for anchor assignment
- [[detr]] — uses IoU in the matching cost

content.newText:
---
name: Dice loss
description: Soft, differentiable F1-style overlap metric — workhorse loss for imbalanced segmentation
type: concept
courses: [Computer-Vision]
source: [18_segmentation_and_object_detection.pdf]
created: 2026-04-26
tags: [concept, deep-learning, segmentation, loss]
---

# Dice loss

A region-based segmentation loss derived from the **Dice coefficient** (also called Sørensen–Dice index, F1 score). Designed to handle the severe class imbalance typical of segmentation, where most pixels are background.

## Dice coefficient (slide 18)

$$\mathrm{Dice}(A, B) = \frac{2\,|A \cap B|}{|A| + |B|}$$

Range: $[0, 1]$. 1 = identical regions, 0 = disjoint.

Algebraically related to [[iou]]: $\mathrm{Dice} = \frac{2\,\mathrm{IoU}}{1 + \mathrm{IoU}}$. Both grow monotonically together; Dice penalises moderately overlapping regions less harshly than IoU.

## Dice loss

$$\mathcal{L}_\text{Dice} = 1 - \mathrm{Dice}(A, B) = 1 - \frac{2\,|A \cap B|}{|A| + |B|}$$

To make this differentiable, evaluate $A$ and $B$ as **soft probabilities** (continuous, not thresholded):

$$\mathcal{L}_\text{Dice} = 1 - \frac{2\,\sum_i p_i\, y_i + \epsilon}{\sum_i p_i + \sum_i y_i + \epsilon}$$

where $p_i \in [0, 1]$ is the predicted foreground probability and $y_i \in \{0, 1\}$ is the ground truth, summed over all pixels.

## In PyTorch (slide 18)

```python
intersection = torch.sum(pred_mask * true_mask, dim=(-1, -2))
union = (
    torch.sum(pred_mask, dim=(-1, -2))
    + torch.sum(true_mask, dim=(-1, -2))
    - intersection
)
dice = (2 * intersection + smooth) / (union + smooth)
loss = 1 - dice.mean()
```

The `smooth` (e.g. 1e-6) avoids 0/0 when an image has no foreground pixels.

## Why it handles class imbalance

Pixel-wise cross-entropy on a 1% foreground task is dominated by background pixels — the model can score 99% accuracy by predicting "background everywhere" and learn nothing useful. Weighted CE patches this, but you have to tune the weights per dataset.

Dice loss is **scale-invariant in the foreground**: doubling the size of the target object roughly doubles both numerator and denominator, leaving the loss unchanged. Tiny objects matter as much as large ones.

## Pitfalls

- **Unstable gradient when foreground is empty**. The smoothing term `+ epsilon` is essential.
- **Multi-class**: compute per-class Dice and average. Background may need to be excluded from the average if it dominates.
- **Smaller gradients than CE for confidently-wrong predictions** — Dice tops out around 0.5 difficulty, while CE keeps growing. Many practitioners use a **compound loss**: $\mathcal{L} = \mathcal{L}_\text{CE} + \lambda\,\mathcal{L}_\text{Dice}$ to get the best of both.

## Related losses (slide 14)

| Family | Members | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Distribution-based | CE, weighted CE, Focal | default; class imbalance via weights |
| Region-based | IoU/Jaccard, **Dice**, Tversky, Lovasz | imbalanced data, small targets |
| Boundary-based | Boundary loss, Hausdorff distance | crisp object edges |
| Compound | DiceFocal, Combo, DiceTopK | strong baselines combining the above |

**Tversky loss**: generalises Dice with separate weights $\alpha, \beta$ on false positives and false negatives:

$$\mathrm{Tversky} = \frac{|A \cap B|}{|A \cap B| + \alpha\,|A \setminus B| + \beta\,|B \setminus A|}$$

Setting $\alpha = \beta = 0.5$ recovers Dice. Skewing $\beta$ higher penalises false negatives more — useful in medical imaging where missing a tumour is worse than a small over-segmentation.

## Related

- [[iou]] — the closely-related metric (used as a metric, not a loss)
- [[loss-functions]] — distribution-based alternatives
- [[unet]] — most-common pairing
- [[semantic-segmentation]] — primary application

content.newText:
---
name: YOLO
description: Single-stage real-time object detector — divide the image into a grid, predict objects per cell in one forward pass
type: concept
courses: [Computer-Vision]
source: [18_segmentation_and_object_detection.pdf]
created: 2026-04-26
tags: [concept, deep-learning, detection]
---

# YOLO (You Only Look Once)

A family of single-stage object detectors (Redmon et al, 2016 and onwards — now maintained as YOLOv5/v8/v11 by Ultralytics). Predicts class labels and bounding boxes for *all* objects in **one forward pass** through a CNN. Faster than two-stage detectors (Faster R-CNN), slightly less accurate, dominant for real-time use.

## Core idea (slides 23–24)

1. Divide the image into an $S \times S$ grid (e.g. $4\times 4$ in the slide example, $7\times 7$ in YOLOv1).
2. Each cell predicts $B$ bounding boxes plus class probabilities.
3. The cell *containing the centre* of an object is responsible for predicting that object.

This is single-shot: the whole prediction comes out of one forward pass — no region-proposal stage.

## Per-cell output (slide 26)

For each cell, each predicted box outputs:

$$Y = (p_c,\, b_x,\, b_y,\, b_h,\, b_w,\, c_1,\, c_2,\, \ldots)$$

| Field | Meaning |
|---|---|
| $p_c$ | probability that an object's centre lies in this cell |
| $b_x, b_y$ | object centre relative to the cell's top-left corner, in $[0, 1]$ |
| $b_h, b_w$ | object size as a multiple of the anchor's height/width (can exceed 1 — the box can be larger than the anchor or the cell) |
| $c_1, c_2, \ldots$ | per-class probabilities |

## Multiple objects per cell: anchor boxes (slide 29)

A cell may contain centres of multiple objects (e.g. two pedestrians close together). Solution: each cell predicts $B$ boxes against $B$ pre-defined **anchor shapes**:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    cell[Grid cell] --> a1[Anchor 1: tall narrow]
    cell --> a2[Anchor 2: wide]
    cell --> a3[Anchor 3: square]
    a1 --> p1[Predicts 1 box]
    a2 --> p2[Predicts 1 box]
    a3 --> p3[Predicts 1 box]
```

At training time, each ground-truth object is assigned to the anchor with **best IoU** to its bounding box. That anchor is responsible for predicting it; the others should output $p_c \approx 0$. See [[anchor-boxes]] for the assignment mechanism.

## Loss function (slide 27)

$$\mathcal{L} = \lambda_\text{coord}\sum \mathbb{1}^\text{obj}\!\left[(x - \hat x)^2 + (y - \hat y)^2\right] + \lambda_\text{coord}\sum \mathbb{1}^\text{obj}\!\left[(\sqrt{w} - \sqrt{\hat w})^2 + (\sqrt{h} - \sqrt{\hat h})^2\right]$$

$$+ \sum \mathbb{1}^\text{obj}(C - \hat C)^2 + \lambda_\text{noobj}\sum \mathbb{1}^\text{noobj}(C - \hat C)^2 + \sum \mathbb{1}^\text{obj}\sum_c (p(c) - \hat p(c))^2$$

Five terms grouped into three roles:

| Role | Terms |
|---|---|
| Bounding-box regression | first two — squared error on centre, squared root of width/height |
| Object presence/absence | third and fourth — squared error on $p_c$, scaled to weight presence vs absence |
| Class probability | last — squared error per class (modern variants use cross-entropy here) |

The square-root on $w, h$ shrinks the penalty on large boxes — a 5-pixel error matters more for a 20-pixel object than for a 200-pixel one.

$\lambda_\text{coord}$ (typically 5) and $\lambda_\text{noobj}$ (typically 0.5) trade off the relative importance of the terms — most cells have no object, so without $\lambda_\text{noobj}$ the loss would be dominated by background.

## Architecture (slide 25)

YOLOv1: a custom CNN backbone — repeated `Conv + MaxPool` stages, ending in two FC layers that produce a flat output of shape $S \times S \times (B \cdot 5 + K)$. Modern YOLOs use ResNet- or CSPDarknet-style backbones.

Conceptually, **just Conv + Pool**, but with an output head shaped to produce the per-cell prediction tensor.

## Inference: NMS deduplication (slide 30)

Two cells may both fire on the same object. After raw prediction:

1. Sort all boxes by confidence.
2. Take the highest-confidence box.
3. Remove any box whose IoU with it exceeds some threshold (e.g. 0.5).
4. Repeat until no boxes remain.

See [[non-maximum-suppression]] for details.

## Trade-offs

- **Pro**: extremely fast (real-time at 30+ FPS even on modest GPUs).
- **Pro**: simple and end-to-end — one network, one loss.
- **Pro**: well-supported via Ultralytics with pretrained weights for many tasks (detect, segment, classify, pose, OBB, track).
- **Con**: less accurate than two-stage methods (Faster R-CNN) on small objects.
- **Con**: anchor sizes need to match the dataset's typical object shapes — slightly fragile.
- **Con**: each grid cell only predicts $B$ boxes; very crowded scenes can saturate.

## Related

- [[anchor-boxes]] — the per-cell shape priors
- [[non-maximum-suppression]] — post-processing
- [[iou]] — used in anchor assignment and NMS
- [[detr]] — the transformer-based alternative without anchors or NMS
- [[cv-tasks]] — task taxonomy

content.newText:
---
name: Anchor boxes
description: Pre-defined box shapes per spatial location that let one detector cell predict multiple objects of different aspect ratios
type: concept
courses: [Computer-Vision]
source: [18_segmentation_and_object_detection.pdf]
created: 2026-04-26
tags: [concept, deep-learning, detection]
---

# Anchor boxes

Pre-defined bounding-box templates (a fixed set of widths and heights) attached to every spatial position in a detector's output grid. Anchors solve two related problems in object detection:

1. **Multiple objects in one cell** — what if two pedestrian centres land in the same grid cell?
2. **Variable aspect ratios** — a person, a car, and a sign have very different shapes; one prediction head can't easily output all of them.

## How they work (slide 29)

For each cell, define $B$ anchors, each a fixed (width, height) pair. The cell predicts **one bounding box per anchor**. At training time, each ground-truth object is assigned to the anchor with the best IoU to its true box — that anchor is *responsible* for predicting it; the others should output $p_c = 0$.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    cell[Grid cell] --> a1[Anchor 1: tall narrow]
    cell --> a2[Anchor 2: wide flat]
    cell --> a3[Anchor 3: square]
    obj1[GT object: pedestrian] --> match1[best IoU = anchor 1]
    obj2[GT object: car] --> match2[best IoU = anchor 2]
    a1 --> p1[Predicts pedestrian box]
    a2 --> p2[Predicts car box]
    a3 --> p3[Predicts no object]
```

## Output parametrisation

The network doesn't predict absolute box coordinates. It predicts **deltas** relative to the anchor:

| Predicted | Meaning |
|---|---|
| $b_x, b_y$ | sigmoid-squashed offset within the cell, in $[0, 1]$ |
| $b_h, b_w$ | exponentiated multiplier on anchor height/width — anchor size $\times e^{\hat w}$ |

This is more learnable than predicting raw coordinates because the network only has to predict small adjustments to a sensible starting shape.

## Choosing anchor sizes

Two strategies:

1. **Hand-picked**: pick a few aspect ratios (e.g. 1:1, 1:2, 2:1) and a few scales (e.g. 32, 64, 128 pixels) — gives ~9 anchors per cell. Used by Faster R-CNN.
2. **k-means on the dataset**: cluster the ground-truth box dimensions in the training set, use the cluster centres as anchor sizes. Used by YOLOv2 onwards. Better fit to dataset statistics.

## Multi-scale: anchors at different feature-map scales

Modern detectors don't use a single grid. Instead, the backbone produces multiple feature maps at different resolutions (Feature Pyramid Network, FPN). Each scale gets its own anchors:

| FPN level | Spatial size | Stride | Anchors |
|---|---|---|---|
| P3 | $H/8 \times W/8$ | 8 | small (32 px) |
| P4 | $H/16 \times W/16$ | 16 | medium (64 px) |
| P5 | $H/32 \times W/32$ | 32 | large (128 px) |
| P6 | $H/64 \times W/64$ | 64 | extra-large (256 px) |

Small objects are detected on high-resolution maps; large objects on low-resolution maps where each cell already covers a big region.

## Pitfalls

- **Anchor mismatch**: if the dataset has unusual aspect ratios (e.g. very long thin objects in satellite imagery), default anchors fail. Re-cluster.
- **Anchor count vs detection density**: $B$ anchors per cell × $S^2$ cells = total predictions. Many anchors → many "no-object" predictions → loss dominated by background → balanced via $\lambda_\text{noobj}$ or focal loss.
- **Sensitivity to assignment threshold**: standard rule is "best IoU" + an additional positive threshold (e.g. IoU > 0.5) and a "negative below 0.4" threshold. The middle gets ignored in the loss.

## Anchor-free alternatives

Modern detectors increasingly avoid anchors:

- **CenterNet / FCOS**: predict box centre as a heatmap, plus distances to the four edges from the centre. No anchors.
- **DETR**: predicts a set of $N$ boxes via transformer queries; bipartite matching replaces anchor assignment. See [[detr]] and [[hungarian-matching]].

These avoid the dataset-specific tuning anchors require but trade it for other complexity.

## Related

- [[yolo]] — the canonical anchor-based detector
- [[iou]] — used for anchor assignment
- [[non-maximum-suppression]] — post-processes anchor-based predictions
- [[detr]] — anchor-free transformer alternative

Pre-defined bounding-box templates (a fixed set of widths and heights) attached to every spatial position in a detector's output grid. Anchors solve two related problems in object detection:

1. **Multiple objects in one cell** — what if two pedestrian centres land in the same grid cell?
2. **Variable aspect ratios** — a person, a car, and a sign have very different shapes; one prediction head can't easily output all of them.

## How they work (slide 29)

For each cell, define $B$ anchors, each a fixed (width, height) pair. The cell predicts **one bounding box per anchor**. At training time, each ground-truth object is assigned to the anchor with the best IoU to its true box — that anchor is *responsible* for predicting it; the others should output $p_c = 0$.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    cell[Grid cell] --> a1[Anchor 1: tall narrow]
    cell --> a2[Anchor 2: wide flat]
    cell --> a3[Anchor 3: square]
    obj1[GT object: pedestrian] --> match1[best IoU = anchor 1]
    obj2[GT object: car] --> match2[best IoU = anchor 2]
    a1 --> p1[Predicts pedestrian box]
    a2 --> p2[Predicts car box]
    a3 --> p3[Predicts no object]
```

## Output parametrisation

The network doesn't predict absolute box coordinates. It predicts **deltas** relative to the anchor:

| Predicted | Meaning |
|---|---|
| $b_x, b_y$ | sigmoid-squashed offset within the cell, in $[0, 1]$ |
| $b_h, b_w$ | exponentiated multiplier on anchor height/width — anchor size $\times e^{\hat w}$ |

This is more learnable than predicting raw coordinates because the network only has to predict small adjustments to a sensible starting shape.

## Choosing anchor sizes

Two strategies:

1. **Hand-picked**: pick a few aspect ratios (e.g. 1:1, 1:2, 2:1) and a few scales (e.g. 32, 64, 128 pixels) — gives ~9 anchors per cell. Used by Faster R-CNN.
2. **k-means on the dataset**: cluster the ground-truth box dimensions in the training set, use the cluster centres as anchor sizes. Used by YOLOv2 onwards. Better fit to dataset statistics.

## Multi-scale: anchors at different feature-map scales

Modern detectors don't use a single grid. Instead, the backbone produces multiple feature maps at different resolutions (Feature Pyramid Network, FPN). Each scale gets its own anchors:

| FPN level | Spatial size | Stride | Anchors |
|---|---|---|---|
| P3 | $H/8 \times W/8$ | 8 | small (32 px) |
| P4 | $H/16 \times W/16$ | 16 | medium (64 px) |
| P5 | $H/32 \times W/32$ | 32 | large (128 px) |
| P6 | $H/64 \times W/64$ | 64 | extra-large (256 px) |

Small objects are detected on high-resolution maps; large objects on low-resolution maps where each cell already covers a big region.

## Pitfalls

- **Anchor mismatch**: if the dataset has unusual aspect ratios (e.g. very long thin objects in satellite imagery), default anchors fail. Re-cluster.
- **Anchor count vs detection density**: $B$ anchors per cell × $S^2$ cells = total predictions. Many anchors → many "no-object" predictions → loss dominated by background → balanced via $\lambda_\text{noobj}$ or focal loss.
- **Sensitivity to assignment threshold**: standard rule is "best IoU" + an additional positive threshold (e.g. IoU > 0.5) and a "negative below 0.4" threshold. The middle gets ignored in the loss.

## Anchor-free alternatives

Modern detectors increasingly avoid anchors:

- **CenterNet / FCOS**: predict box centre as a heatmap, plus distances to the four edges from the centre. No anchors.
- **DETR**: predicts a set of $N$ boxes via transformer queries; bipartite matching replaces anchor assignment. See [[detr]] and [[hungarian-matching]].

These avoid the dataset-specific tuning anchors require but trade it for other complexity.

## Related

- [[yolo]] — the canonical anchor-based detector
- [[iou]] — used for anchor assignment
- [[non-maximum-suppression]] — post-processes anchor-based predictions
- [[detr]] — anchor-free transformer alternative

content.newText:
---
name: Hungarian matching (assignment problem)
description: Polynomial-time algorithm for optimal one-to-one matching between two sets given a cost matrix
type: concept
courses: [Computer-Vision]
source: [18_segmentation_and_object_detection.pdf]
created: 2026-04-26
tags: [concept, algorithm, optimisation]
---

# Hungarian matching

An algorithm (Kuhn 1955) for the **linear assignment problem**: given a cost matrix $C \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$, find a one-to-one assignment of rows to columns that minimises the total cost. Runs in $O(n^3)$ — much faster than the brute-force $n!$ permutations.

In computer vision, used in **DETR-style detectors** ([[detr]]) and tracking algorithms to match a set of predictions to a set of ground-truth labels.

## The setup (slides 34–36)

- $n$ predicted boxes (slots): $A, B, C, \ldots$
- $m$ ground-truth boxes: $1, 2, 3, \ldots$ (pad with "no object" if $m < n$)
- Cost $C_{ij}$: how bad would it be to match prediction $j$ with ground-truth $i$?
- Goal: a permutation $\sigma$ (one prediction per ground truth) that minimises $\sum_i C_{i, \sigma(i)}$.

For object detection, the cost is typically a weighted combination:

$$C_{ij} = \alpha\,\mathrm{CE}(c_i, \hat c_j) + \beta\,\|b_i - \hat b_j\|_1 + \gamma\,(1 - \mathrm{IoU}(b_i, \hat b_j))$$

— class agreement + box distance + box overlap. See [[detr]] for context.

## Worked sketch

| | Pred A | Pred B | Pred C |
|---|---|---|---|
| GT 1 | $\mathrm{IoU}(1, A) + \mathrm{CE}(1, A)$ | $\mathrm{IoU}(1, B) + \mathrm{CE}(1, B)$ | $\mathrm{IoU}(1, C) + \mathrm{CE}(1, C)$ |
| GT 2 | $\mathrm{IoU}(2, A) + \mathrm{CE}(2, A)$ | $\mathrm{IoU}(2, B) + \mathrm{CE}(2, B)$ | $\mathrm{IoU}(2, C) + \mathrm{CE}(2, C)$ |
| GT 3 | $\mathrm{IoU}(3, A) + \mathrm{CE}(3, A)$ | $\mathrm{IoU}(3, B) + \mathrm{CE}(3, B)$ | $\mathrm{IoU}(3, C) + \mathrm{CE}(3, C)$ |

Hungarian finds the minimum-cost permutation — say $1 \to B$, $2 \to A$, $3 \to C$ — and that becomes the matching used to compute the per-prediction loss.

## In Python

`scipy.optimize.linear_sum_assignment` is the standard implementation:

```python
from scipy.optimize import linear_sum_assignment

cost = np.array([
    [5, 8, 6, 7],
    [9, 5, 7, 8],
    [5, 9, 8, 4],
    [6, 3, 5, 9],
])
row_ind, col_ind = linear_sum_assignment(cost)
# row_ind[i], col_ind[i] gives the (row, column) pairs in the optimal matching.
total_cost = cost[row_ind, col_ind].sum()
```

For non-square cost matrices, pad with a large "no object" cost.

## Why it matters in DETR

DETR predicts a fixed set of $N$ candidate boxes per image (e.g. 100). The image actually contains, say, 4 objects. Without matching:

- The loss can't tell which of the 100 predictions should match GT object 1.
- Naïve nearest-neighbour matching is greedy and can give suboptimal pairings.

Hungarian matching gives the **globally optimal** one-to-one pairing under the chosen cost. The remaining $96$ predictions are matched to "no object" ($\varnothing$) and trained to predict empty class.

This single mechanism replaces the entire **anchor + NMS** pipeline of YOLO-style detectors. The loss is computed on the matched pairs; nothing about anchors, grids, or NMS is needed at inference time.

## Generalisations

- **Bipartite graph matching** is the same problem; "Hungarian" is one algorithm for it.
- **Munkres algorithm** is an equivalent name for the same algorithm.
- For very large $n$ (thousands), specialised approximations exist; for typical $n \le 1000$ the $O(n^3)$ exact algorithm is fast enough.

## Related

- [[detr]] — primary application in computer vision
- [[iou]] — typical component of the assignment cost
- [[loss-functions]] — what the matching feeds into
- [[yolo]] — the alternative paradigm (anchor + NMS instead of Hungarian)

content.newText:
---
name: DETR (Detection Transformer)
description: Transformer-based object detector that outputs a fixed-size set of boxes — no anchors, no NMS, Hungarian matching at training
type: concept
courses: [Computer-Vision]
source: [18_segmentation_and_object_detection.pdf]
created: 2026-04-26
tags: [concept, deep-learning, detection, transformer]
---

# DETR (DEtection TRansformer)

A 2020 detector (Carion et al, FAIR) that re-frames object detection as **set prediction**. Replaces the anchor-grid-NMS machinery of YOLO/Faster R-CNN with a transformer encoder-decoder that emits a fixed-size set of $N$ box predictions, each labelled (class) + located (4 box coordinates) directly.

## Pipeline (slide 33)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    img([Image]) --> cnn[CNN backbone]
    cnn --> feat[Feature map]
    feat --> enc[Transformer encoder]
    enc --> dec[Transformer decoder]
    queries[N learned object queries] --> dec
    dec --> set["Set of N predictions: c_i, b_i"]
    set --> match[Hungarian bipartite matching]
    gt([Ground truth boxes]) --> match
    match --> loss["Loss: classification CE + L1 + IoU"]
```

Five components:

1. **CNN backbone** (typically a ResNet) extracts a feature map.
2. **Transformer encoder** processes flattened feature-map tokens.
3. **N learned object queries** — $N$ learnable embeddings (e.g. $N = 100$) that act as "slots" for objects.
4. **Transformer decoder** uses cross-attention from queries to encoder tokens; each query produces an output embedding.
5. **Prediction heads** — small MLPs convert each query output into a class score + bounding box.

The output is always a set of $N$ predictions, regardless of how many objects are actually in the image. Predictions for non-existent objects are matched to a "no object" class ($\varnothing$).

## Hungarian matching at training time

The set is unordered — we don't know which of the $N$ slots should predict which ground-truth object. [[hungarian-matching]] solves this:

- Build a cost matrix $C_{ij}$ between every (predicted slot, GT object) pair using class agreement + L1 distance + 1 - IoU.
- Find the optimal one-to-one assignment.
- Compute the loss on the matched pairs; train unmatched slots to predict $\varnothing$.

This globally-optimal matching replaces:
- Anchor generation
- Anchor-to-GT assignment by IoU thresholds
- NMS at inference

## Loss

After matching, the per-pair loss is:

$$\mathcal{L} = \sum_i \left[-\log \hat p_{\sigma(i)}(c_i) + \mathbb{1}_{c_i \neq \varnothing}\,\mathcal{L}_\text{box}(b_i, \hat b_{\sigma(i)})\right]$$

where $\mathcal{L}_\text{box} = \lambda_\text{L1}\|b - \hat b\|_1 + \lambda_\text{IoU}(1 - \mathrm{IoU}(b, \hat b))$.

## Why no NMS

Because the bipartite matching enforces one-to-one between predictions and GT, the model is *trained* to make each ground-truth object emerge from exactly one of the $N$ slots — no duplicates by construction. So the post-processing NMS step that all anchor-based detectors require is unnecessary.

This is the central architectural simplification.

## Trade-offs

- **Pro**: clean end-to-end pipeline; no hand-designed anchors; no NMS hyperparameters; transformer's global receptive field handles long-range relationships.
- **Pro**: same architecture extends to panoptic segmentation, instance segmentation, etc. (MaskFormer, Mask2Former are descendants).
- **Con**: **slow training**. Original DETR took 500 epochs to match Faster R-CNN, mostly because the bipartite matching is unstable early in training. Deformable DETR fixes this with 50 epochs.
- **Con**: weaker on small objects compared to multi-scale anchor methods. Deformable / RT-DETR address this by adding multi-scale features.

## Variants

- **Deformable DETR**: sparse attention focused on a few sampled points per query → 10× faster training, better small-object accuracy.
- **DINO** (Detection Transformer with Improved deNoising anchors): matches or beats two-stage detectors at smaller scale.
- **RT-DETR**: real-time variant competitive with YOLO on speed and accuracy.

## Related

- [[hungarian-matching]] — the assignment algorithm at training time
- [[transformer-encoder]] — the body of DETR
- [[yolo]] — anchor-based contrast
- [[non-maximum-suppression]] — what DETR removes
- [[iou]] — used in matching cost and loss

content.newText:
---
name: SAM (Segment Anything Model)
description: 2023 foundation model for promptable segmentation — given a point/box/mask prompt, output a segmentation mask
type: concept
courses: [Computer-Vision]
source: [18_segmentation_and_object_detection.pdf]
created: 2026-04-26
tags: [concept, deep-learning, segmentation, foundation-model]
---

# SAM (Segment Anything Model)

A 2023 foundation model (Meta AI / FAIR). Trained on **1+ billion masks** across 11 million images. Re-frames segmentation as a **promptable** task: given an image and a prompt (a point, a box, a rough mask, or text), output a precise segmentation mask.

## The task (slide 38)

Traditional segmentation models output a fixed set of class labels. SAM is conditioned on a prompt:

| Prompt type | Example use |
|---|---|
| Point prompt | "Click here — segment whatever object I clicked on." |
| Box prompt | "Segment the object inside this rough box." |
| Mask prompt | "Refine this rough mask into a precise one." |
| Text prompt | "Segment all cats." (less reliable than visual prompts in the original SAM) |

The output is a binary mask. SAM can produce up to three plausible masks per ambiguous prompt (e.g. clicking on a person's shirt could mean the shirt, the torso, or the whole person).

## Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    img([Image]) --> ie[Image encoder ViT]
    ie --> emb[Image embedding]
    prompt[Point box or mask prompt] --> pe[Prompt encoder]
    pe --> pemb[Prompt embedding]
    emb --> dec[Lightweight mask decoder]
    pemb --> dec
    dec --> mask([Valid mask])
```

Three components:

1. **Image encoder**: heavy ViT (ViT-H is largest, ~636M params). Encodes the image once into a feature map. Slow but only runs once per image.
2. **Prompt encoder**: lightweight, encodes points / boxes / coarse masks into a small set of tokens.
3. **Mask decoder**: small transformer that combines image embedding + prompt tokens via cross-attention to produce the final mask. Fast — many prompts can be processed against one image embedding.

This separation is the practical magic: encode the image once (slow), then react to interactive prompts in real time (fast).

## The data engine (slide 38c)

The 1B-mask dataset SA-1B was built with a **model-in-the-loop** annotation process:

1. Train initial SAM on a small human-annotated set.
2. Use that SAM to suggest masks for new images.
3. Annotators correct or accept; corrections feed back into training.
4. Iterate, with the model gradually carrying more of the labelling load.

Final SA-1B has 1.1B masks, 11M images. This is the largest segmentation dataset by orders of magnitude.

## Why it matters

- **Zero-shot generalisation**: works on previously-unseen domains (medical, satellite, microscopy) better than expected from a model trained on natural images.
- **Promptable interface**: integrates naturally with downstream pipelines that need quick mask generation from coarse signals.
- **Demonstrates the foundation-model shift in computer vision**: pretrain at massive scale on a flexible task, then specialise via prompts or light fine-tuning.

## Limitations

- **Segments without semantics**: SAM outputs masks but not *labels* — it doesn't tell you whether the masked region is a cat, a hat, or a chair. Pair with a classifier (e.g. CLIP) for labels.
- **Sometimes oversegments / undersegments** based on prompt ambiguity. The 3-mask output partially handles this.
- **High inference cost** on the image encoder — not real-time on a single GPU for full ViT-H.

## Variants

- **SAM 2** (2024): adds video segmentation with memory across frames.
- **MobileSAM, EfficientSAM**: distilled smaller image encoders for edge deployment.
- **HQ-SAM**: refined boundaries with extra training data.

## Related

- [[semantic-segmentation]] — the task family
- [[vision-transformer]] — the image encoder backbone
- [[transformer-encoder]] — the mask decoder primitive
- [[unet]] — the older specialised alternative

content.newText:
- **Soft-NMS**: instead of dropping overlapping detections, *down-weight* their scores by overlap — useful when objects legitimately overlap (crowds).
- **Per-class NMS**: only suppress within the same class; otherwise different classes can co-locate.

## In object detection (lecture 18, slide 30)

YOLO-style detectors emit overlapping boxes for the same object — adjacent grid cells / multiple anchors all fire. NMS deduplicates:

1. Sort all detections by confidence score.
2. Take the highest-confidence box; add to keep-list.
3. Compute IoU between this box and every remaining lower-confidence box.
4. Remove any box whose IoU exceeds threshold (typically 0.5).
5. Repeat from step 2 until no boxes remain.

This is a practical example of the generic algorithm above with `overlap = IoU` and `score = confidence`. NMS is what allows anchor-based detectors to make many overlapping guesses cheaply and still output a clean per-object detection at inference time.

[[detr]] eliminates this step: bipartite matching at training time forces one-to-one prediction-to-object, so the output is already deduplicated.

## Related

- [[canny-edge-detector]] — uses NMS along the gradient direction
- [[hough-transform]] — uses NMS on the accumulator
- [[yolo]] — uses IoU-based NMS for bounding-box deduplication
- [[iou]] — the overlap metric used in detection NMS
- [[detr]] — the alternative that removes the need for NMS

content.newText:
CNNs were SOTA until ~2021; transformers now lead on most large-scale benchmarks but require significantly more data. CNNs remain competitive and easier to train on smaller datasets.

## Three flavours of segmentation (lecture 18, slide 6)

Within the segmentation tier, three sub-tasks differ in how they treat instances and "stuff":

| Variant | Two cats labelled as | Sky / road labelled? |
|---|---|---|
| **Semantic** | both "cat" — same colour | yes |
| **Instance** | distinct IDs (cat 1, cat 2) | no — only countable objects ("things") |
| **Panoptic** | distinct IDs and sky/road labelled | yes — things + stuff together |

See [[semantic-segmentation]] for details.

## Loss functions per task

| Task | Typical loss(es) |

content.newText:
---
tags: [flashcards, review]
course: Computer-Vision
lecture: 18
source: 18_segmentation_and_object_detection.pdf
created: 2026-04-26
---

# Lecture 18 — Segmentation & object detection (flashcards)

What's the difference between semantic, instance, and panoptic segmentation?
?
**Semantic**: per-pixel class label, instances of the same class are not distinguished. **Instance**: distinguishes individual objects (cat 1 vs cat 2) but only labels "things," not "stuff." **Panoptic**: distinguishes instances and labels both things and stuff (sky, road) — strictly more general.

What's the difference between "things" and "stuff" in segmentation?
?
**Things** are countable foreground objects (people, cars, animals) — they get instance IDs. **Stuff** is uncountable amorphous regions (sky, road, grass) — labelled by class but not individually tracked.

Why does naive sliding-window segmentation fail in practice?
?
For every pixel, you'd run a CNN classifier on a patch centred there — $O(HW)$ forward passes per image. Adjacent patches share most pixels, so 99% of compute is duplicated. A fully convolutional network (FCN) does the same prediction in one forward pass.

What's the architectural change that turns a CNN classifier into a segmentation network?
?
Remove the fully-connected layers entirely. Replace the final FC + softmax with a $1\times 1$ conv that produces $K$ output channels (one per class). The output is now $K \times H \times W$ — a per-pixel class score map. Argmax over channels gives the class id per pixel.

Why pure FCN designs are not used in practice?
?
With no downsampling, the receptive field grows only by $k - 1$ per layer — you'd need ~50 conv layers to see a 100-pixel object. Compute is also expensive at full resolution. Encoder-decoder architectures (e.g. U-Net) downsample first to grow receptive field, then upsample back.

What's the central architectural innovation of U-Net?
?
**Skip connections from encoder to decoder at matching resolutions**, concatenated along the channel dim. The decoder upsample provides deep semantic context; the encoder skip provides fine spatial detail; concatenation preserves both for the next conv to combine.

Why does U-Net use **concatenation** for skip connections instead of **addition** like ResNet?
?
The two paths carry very different content (deep semantics vs raw spatial detail). Addition would force them into a sum and destroy that distinction. Concatenation preserves both so the next conv layer can learn how to combine them.

Name the four upsampling methods covered, distinguishing learnable from non-learnable.
?
**Non-learnable**: nearest neighbour (replicate values), bed of nails (place values, zero rest), max-unpooling (place at stored argmax indices). **Learnable**: transposed convolution (each input scatters its kernel-weighted contribution).

Why is transposed convolution sometimes called "deconvolution," and why is the name misleading?
?
It produces a larger output from a smaller input — visually the inverse of conv's downsampling. But it's not a true mathematical inverse of convolution — it just transposes the connectivity pattern of conv (hence "transposed"). The kernel is learned, not analytically derived.

What causes "checkerboard artefacts" in transposed convolution, and how do you avoid them?
?
When kernel size is not divisible by stride, output positions receive uneven numbers of kernel contributions, producing visible grid patterns. Fixes: use kernel size divisible by stride (e.g. stride 2, kernel 4), or use **resize + conv** (nearest-neighbour upsample followed by stride-1 conv) instead.

Define IoU and give its formula.
?
$\mathrm{IoU} = |A \cap B| / |A \cup B| = |A \cap B| / (|A| + |B| - |A \cap B|)$. Range $[0, 1]$: 0 = disjoint, 1 = identical. Used as a metric, not a loss (not differentiable when applied to thresholded masks).

Why is IoU **not** used as a loss function directly?
?
The threshold operation that produces the binary masks isn't differentiable. The continuous (soft) variant is the **Dice loss**: $\mathcal{L}_\text{Dice} = 1 - 2|A \cap B| / (|A| + |B|)$, evaluated on probabilities, not thresholded masks.

Write the Dice coefficient formula and explain when to use Dice loss over cross-entropy.
?
$\mathrm{Dice} = 2|A \cap B| / (|A| + |B|)$. Use Dice loss when you have severe class imbalance (e.g. small foreground regions in medical imaging) — it's roughly scale-invariant in foreground size, while CE is dominated by the background majority.

Why does pixel-wise cross-entropy fail on heavily imbalanced segmentation data, and what's the simplest fix?
?
The model can score 99% accuracy by predicting "background everywhere" if 99% of pixels are background — the foreground gradient is dwarfed. Fix: weighted CE, where each class's contribution is divided by its pixel count (or the foreground class is upweighted).

What's the difference between a single-stage and a two-stage object detector?
?
**Single-stage** (YOLO, DETR): predicts classes + boxes in one forward pass; faster, slightly less accurate. **Two-stage** (Faster R-CNN): first generates region proposals, then classifies each — more accurate, slower.

What's the central idea of YOLO?
?
Divide the image into an $S \times S$ grid. Each cell predicts $B$ bounding boxes (one per anchor) and class probabilities. The cell containing the *centre* of an object is responsible for predicting it. All in one forward pass.

What's encoded in YOLO's per-anchor output vector $Y = (p_c, b_x, b_y, b_h, b_w, c_1, c_2, \ldots)$?
?
$p_c$: probability an object's centre is in this cell. $(b_x, b_y)$: object centre position relative to the cell, in $[0, 1]$. $(b_h, b_w)$: object size as a multiple of the anchor's height and width. $c_1, c_2, \ldots$: class probabilities.

Why does YOLO's loss take the **square root** of width and height in the bbox regression term?
?
A 5-pixel error matters more for a 20-pixel object than for a 200-pixel object. Squaring the square-root of size makes the penalty scale-aware: small boxes are more sensitive to size error than large ones.

What problem do anchor boxes solve in YOLO, and how are they assigned at training time?
?
A grid cell may contain centres of multiple objects. Solution: each cell predicts $B$ boxes against $B$ pre-defined anchor shapes. At training, each ground-truth object is assigned to the anchor with the **best IoU** to its true box; only that anchor predicts it ($p_c \approx 1$), the others predict $p_c \approx 0$.

What's the standard way to set anchor sizes for a new dataset?
?
**k-means cluster** the ground-truth bounding-box dimensions in the training set, and use the cluster centres as anchor (width, height) pairs. Used since YOLOv2; better than hand-picked aspect ratios.

What's NMS and what overlap threshold is conventional in object detection?
?
Non-maximum suppression: sort predicted boxes by confidence; greedily keep the highest, remove any other box with IoU > threshold (typically 0.5) against it; repeat. Eliminates duplicate detections of the same object from adjacent cells / anchors.

How does DETR avoid using anchor boxes and NMS?
?
DETR predicts a **fixed set** of $N$ box predictions via transformer queries. **Hungarian matching** at training time pairs each prediction with at most one ground-truth object (others matched to "no object") globally optimally. The model is trained to make each GT object emerge from exactly one slot — no duplicates by construction, so NMS is unnecessary.

What is the Hungarian (linear-sum-assignment) algorithm computing?
?
Given an $n \times n$ cost matrix, find the one-to-one assignment of rows to columns that minimises total cost. $O(n^3)$ — much faster than the brute-force $n!$ permutations. In DETR the cost combines class CE, L1 box distance, and 1 - IoU.

In the DETR loss matching cost, what three components are typically combined and why?
?
Classification cross-entropy (does the prediction get the class right?), L1 box distance (do the coordinates roughly agree?), and $1 - \mathrm{IoU}$ (do the boxes actually overlap?). All three are needed: L1 alone is scale-sensitive, IoU alone is zero for non-overlapping boxes (no gradient signal).

What's the main practical drawback of original DETR vs anchor-based detectors?
?
Slow training. Original DETR took ~500 epochs to match Faster R-CNN, mostly because the bipartite matching is unstable early in training. Deformable DETR fixes this with sparse attention → ~50 epochs.

What's SAM and what makes it a "foundation model" for segmentation?
?
**Segment Anything Model** (Meta, 2023): trained on 1B+ masks across 11M images. Promptable: given an image and a prompt (point, box, or rough mask), output a precise segmentation mask. Generalises zero-shot to many domains it wasn't directly trained on.

What's the architectural separation in SAM that makes it usable interactively?
?
**Image encoder**: heavy ViT, runs once per image (slow). **Prompt encoder + mask decoder**: lightweight, runs per prompt (fast). So a user can click many times on one image and get masks back in real time — the expensive image encoding is amortised.

What does SAM **not** give you?
?
Class labels. SAM produces masks but doesn't tell you whether the masked region is a cat, hat, or chair. Pair it with a classifier (CLIP, etc.) for labels — or fine-tune for a specific labelled task.

What's nnU-Net and why does it dominate medical-image segmentation benchmarks?
?
**nnU-Net** is an automatic configuration system that detects dataset properties (image resolution, modality, class balance) and picks patch size, network topology, augmentation, and loss automatically. Removes most hyperparameter tuning, making U-Net competitive across many medical datasets without manual fiddling.

Name three loss-function families for segmentation and one example from each.
?
**Distribution-based**: pixel-wise cross-entropy, weighted CE, focal loss. **Region-based**: Dice, Tversky, IoU/Jaccard, Lovasz. **Boundary-based**: Boundary loss, Hausdorff distance loss. Compound losses (DiceFocal, Combo, DiceTopK) mix families and often outperform any single one.

content.text: ### Lecture 19 — Self-supervised & contrastive learning

### Lecture 19 — Self-supervised & contrastive learning file plan **Lecture note:** `lecture-19-self-supervised-and-contrastive.md` **New concepts (9):** `self-supervised-learning`, `contrastive-learning`, `siamese-network`, `triplet-loss`, `simclr`, `clip`, `few-shot-learning`, `zero-shot-learning`, `face-recognition` **Extended concepts (1):** `transfer-learning` (link to SSL pretraining) **Flashcards:** ~25 **Flagship Mermaids:** SimCLR pipeline (slide 18): ```mermaid flowchart LR x[Image x] --> a1["augment t ~ T"] x --> a2["augment t' ~ T"] a1 --> v1[View x_i] a2 --> v2[View x_j] v1 --> f1[Encoder f] v2 --> f2[Encoder f] f1 --> h1[Repr h_i] f2 --> h2[Repr h_j] h1 --> g1[Projection g] h2 --> g2[Projection g] g1 --> z1[z_i] g2 --> z2[z_j] z1 --> loss[NT-Xent loss] z2 --> loss loss --> note["maximise agreement on positive pair
vs all other batch pairs as negatives"] ``` CLIP training + zero-shot (slide 35, 37): ```mermaid flowchart LR img[Image batch I_1..I_N] --> ie[Image encoder] txt[Caption batch T_1..T_N] --> te[Text encoder] ie --> iemb[Image embeddings] te --> temb[Text embeddings] iemb --> sim[N x N similarity matrix] temb --> sim sim --> ntx["NT-Xent: diagonal entries = positive pairs
off-diagonal = negative pairs"] ``` Writing files now.

content.newText:
--- tags: [lecture] course: Computer-Vision source: 19_self_supervised_and_contrastive_learning.pdf lecture: 19 created: 2026-04-26 --- # Lecture 19 — Self-supervised & contrastive learning ## Overview How to learn useful image representations **without labels**. Two related paradigms: - **Self-supervised learning (SSL)**: solve a pretext task whose labels come from the data itself (predict masked tokens, predict the rotation angle, generate the next pixel, etc.). The pretext task is the means; the goal is a useful representation that transfers to downstream tasks. - **Contrastive learning**: shape the embedding space so that similar inputs are close and dissimilar inputs are far. Can be supervised (need pair labels) or self-supervised (use augmentations to create positive pairs). The lecture covers: 1. **Self-supervised paradigm** — pretext tasks; next-token prediction, generative, transformation prediction, contrastive. 2. **Siamese networks** — twin towers with shared weights producing a pair of embeddings. 3. **Pairwise contrastive losses** — margin loss (positive vs negative pair), triplet loss (anchor + positive + negative), NT-Xent (positive vs all batch members). 4. **Triplet mining** — use only "hard" triplets where $d(z_p, z_n)$ is below the margin. 5. **Self-supervised contrastive frameworks** — SimCLR (NT-Xent on augmentation pairs), SimSiam (no negatives, stop-gradient), DINO (self-distillation). 6. **Few-shot / one-shot prediction** — k-NN against a small "support set" of pre-computed embeddings. 7. **Face recognition** — detect + align + embed (contrastive) → verification or identification via similarity search. 8. **Zero-shot learning** — classify into classes never seen at training time, via class descriptions. 9. **CLIP** — joint image + text contrastive pretraining; enables zero-shot classification by phrasing classes as text prompts. ## Key concepts - [[self-supervised-learning]] — the paradigm; pretext tasks - [[contrastive-learning]] — pull positives together, push negatives apart - [[siamese-network]] — twin shared-weight encoders - [[triplet-loss]] — covers margin loss, triplet loss, NT-Xent / InfoNCE - [[simclr]] — canonical self-supervised contrastive framework - [[clip]] — image + text contrastive pretraining - [[few-shot-learning]] — predict from few examples via similarity in embedding space - [[zero-shot-learning]] — predict for classes never seen at training time - [[face-recognition]] — verification vs identification via embeddings ## Equations ### Margin / contrastive loss (slide 13) $$L(z_1, z_2) = \begin{cases} d(z_1, z_2) & \text{positive pair (similar)} \\ \max(0,\, M - d(z_1, z_2)) & \text{negative pair (dissimilar)} \end{cases}$$ Positives are pulled to distance 0; negatives are pushed past margin $M$. $d$ is Euclidean or cosine. ### Triplet loss (slide 14) $$L(z_a, z_p, z_n) = \max(0,\, d(z_a, z_p) - d(z_a, z_n) + M)$$ The triplet $(z_a, z_p, z_n)$ contains anchor / positive / negative. The loss is non-zero only when $d(z_a, z_n) < d(z_a, z_p) + M$ — triplets where the negative is *not yet* far enough from the anchor relative to the positive. ### NT-Xent (Normalised Temperature-scaled Cross Entropy, slide 16) For all $i \in \{1, \ldots, 2N\}, j \in \{1, \ldots, 2N\}$ with $i \neq j$: $$s_{i, j} = \frac{z_i^\top z_j}{\|z_i\|\,\|z_j\|}$$ For a positive pair $(i, j)$: $$\ell(i, j) = -\log\frac{\exp(s_{i, j} / \tau)}{\sum_{k=1, k \neq i}^{2N} \exp(s_{i, k} / \tau)}$$ $$\mathcal{L} = \frac{1}{2N}\sum_{k=1}^{N}\left[\ell(2k-1,\, 2k) + \ell(2k,\, 2k-1)\right]$$ Cosine similarity, scaled by temperature $\tau$, fed into a softmax cross-entropy that treats the matched augmentation pair as the positive class and all other batch members as negatives. ## Diagrams ### Self-supervised pretext-task families (slide 5) ```mermaid flowchart TD ssl[Self-supervised learning] --> next[Next-token / masked prediction] ssl --> gen[Generative models] ssl --> trans[Transformation prediction] ssl --> contr[Contrastive methods] next --> ne["Predict masked tokens
e.g. GPT, BERT, MAE"] gen --> ge["Reconstruct the input
e.g. autoencoders, GANs"] trans --> te["Predict the transform applied
e.g. rotation 0/90/180/270, jigsaw"] contr --> ce["Pull augmented views together,
push other images apart
e.g. SimCLR, MoCo, DINO"] ``` ### Siamese network with shared weights (slide 11) ```mermaid flowchart LR x1[Image 1] --> e1[Encoder shared weights] x2[Image 2] --> e2[Encoder shared weights] e1 --> z1[Embedding z_1] e2 --> z2[Embedding z_2] z1 --> sim[similarity z_1, z_2] z2 --> sim sim --> loss[Contrastive loss] ``` In PyTorch you implement this by **defining one model and applying it twice** to two different inputs — the weights are shared by construction. ### Triplet loss — what it minimises (slide 14) ```mermaid flowchart LR a[Anchor] --> dap[d a p] p[Positive same class] --> dap a --> dan[d a n] n[Negative different class] --> dan dap --> obj[Want d a p smaller than d a n by margin M] dan --> obj ``` If $d(a, n) > d(a, p) + M$, the triplet contributes 0 to the loss. Otherwise, gradient pushes $a, p$ closer and $a, n$ further apart. ### SimCLR pipeline (slide 18) ```mermaid flowchart LR x[Image x] --> a1["augment t ~ T"] x --> a2["augment t' ~ T"] a1 --> v1[View x_i] a2 --> v2[View x_j] v1 --> f1[Encoder f] v2 --> f2[Encoder f] f1 --> h1[Repr h_i] f2 --> h2[Repr h_j] h1 --> g1[Projection g] h2 --> g2[Projection g] g1 --> z1[z_i] g2 --> z2[z_j] z1 --> loss[NT-Xent loss] z2 --> loss loss --> note[positive pair x_i and x_j;
negatives are all other 2N-2 batch entries] ``` After training, throw away the projection head $g$ and use $h$ for downstream tasks. ### SimSiam — asymmetry replaces negatives (slide 19) ```mermaid flowchart LR x[Image x] --> a1[augment] x --> a2[augment] a1 --> e1[Encoder f] a2 --> e2[Encoder f] e1 --> p[Predictor h] e2 --> sg["stop-gradient"] p --> sim[similarity] sg --> sim sim --> loss ``` No negatives needed. The asymmetry — predictor on one side, stop-gradient on the other — prevents collapse to a constant. ### DINO — self-distillation with no labels (slide 20) ```mermaid flowchart LR x[Image x] --> v1[view 1] x --> v2[view 2] v1 --> stu[Student g] v2 --> tea[Teacher g, EMA of student] stu --> p1[softmax] tea --> ce[centring + softmax + sg] p1 --> loss["-p2 log p1"] ce --> loss loss --> updstu[update student] updstu -.-> updtea[teacher EMA update] ``` Student and teacher are the same architecture with **different parameters**: teacher is an exponential moving average of the student. Stop-gradient on the teacher; softmax + centring on its output prevents collapse. Emergent property: DINO learns to segment foreground objects without any segmentation labels. ### Few-shot via k-NN on a support set (slides 22–23) ```mermaid flowchart LR sup[Support set few examples per new class] --> enc1[Pretrained encoder f] enc1 --> emb[Embeddings averaged per class] query[Query image] --> enc2[Same encoder f] enc2 --> qemb[Query embedding] qemb --> cos[Cosine similarity to each class centroid] emb --> cos cos --> pred[Predicted label = argmax] ``` Same encoder, no extra training. The encoder must already produce representations where same-class examples cluster — typically achieved via [[contrastive-learning]] or large-scale pretraining. ### Face recognition pipeline (slides 24–30) ```mermaid flowchart LR img[Photo] --> det[Face detection - YOLO style] det --> align[Align - perspective transform] align --> enc[Deep CNN feature extractor - contrastively trained] enc --> emb[Face embedding] emb --> q{Task} q -->|verification| ver[Compare to claimed identity in DB] q -->|identification| iden[Compare to all DB entries] ``` Verification: "is this person Juan Pérez?" — binary, one DB lookup. Identification: "who is this?" — N comparisons against the DB; output the best match (or "unknown" if no match passes threshold). ### CLIP training (slides 35–37) ```mermaid flowchart LR img[Image batch I_1..I_N] --> ie[Image encoder] txt[Caption batch T_1..T_N] --> te[Text encoder] ie --> iemb[Image embeddings] te --> temb[Text embeddings] iemb --> sim[N x N cosine similarity matrix] temb --> sim sim --> ntx[NT-Xent: diagonal entries = matched pairs;
off-diagonal = mismatched] ``` Trained on 400M image-caption pairs (WIT). The image and text encoders learn to produce embeddings in a **shared latent space** where matched (image, caption) pairs are close. ### CLIP zero-shot classification (slide 37) ```mermaid flowchart LR classes[Class names: plane, car, dog, ...] --> prompt["Wrap each as 'A photo of a '"] prompt --> tenc[CLIP text encoder] tenc --> tembs[Text embeddings T_1..T_K] img[New image] --> ienc[CLIP image encoder] ienc --> iemb[Image embedding I] iemb --> sim[cosine similarity to each T_k] tembs --> sim sim --> argmax[argmax = predicted class] ``` No training needed for the new task — just write the class names as text and run CLIP. Strong zero-shot performance because of the massive image-text training corpus. ## Open questions - SimSiam doesn't need negatives; SimCLR does. What does SimSiam's stop-gradient + predictor combo do that *replaces* negatives? - DINO learns to segment foreground objects with **no segmentation labels**. Why does that emerge from a self-distillation contrastive setup? - CLIP's zero-shot accuracy on ImageNet matches a fully-supervised ResNet-50 — but the gap widens dramatically on out-of-distribution variants (ImageNet Sketch, Rendition). Why does the contrastive image-text objective generalise better than supervised classification? ## Connections to other lectures - **Pretext tasks generalise [[transfer-learning]] (lecture 17)** — instead of pretraining on labelled ImageNet, pretrain on a label-free task and transfer the encoder. - The Siamese network is the original deep-learning use of [[matrix-multiplication]] (lecture 14) for similarity — `cos(z1, z2)`. - [[data-augmentation]] (lecture 16) is the *engine* that creates positive pairs in self-supervised contrastive methods. - CLIP's image encoder is a [[vision-transformer]] (lecture 17); the next lecture (autoencoders) covers another representation-learning paradigm.

---
name: Self-supervised learning (SSL)
description: Train on a pretext task whose labels are derived from the data itself; learn representations that transfer to downstream tasks
type: concept
courses: [Computer-Vision]
source: [19_self_supervised_and_contrastive_learning.pdf]
created: 2026-04-26
tags: [concept, deep-learning, representation-learning]
---

# Self-supervised learning (SSL)

A learning paradigm where models learn from **unlabelled** data by solving a **pretext task** whose labels are derived automatically from the data itself. The pretext task is the means; the goal is a useful representation that transfers to downstream supervised tasks (classification, detection, segmentation) — typically via fine-tuning a small head on a small labelled set.

## Why SSL exists

Labelled data is expensive (1k–5k examples per class is a typical lower bound for training from scratch). Unlabelled data is essentially free — billions of images on the web, days of YouTube video. SSL converts that abundance into useful representations.

## The pretext task is a *means*, not the goal (slide 4)

> The goal is to learn useful representations that can be transferred to other tasks, **not** to perform well at the pretext task itself.

A model that perfectly predicts the rotation of an arbitrary image *must* understand image semantics enough to know what "right-side-up" means — but the rotation prediction itself is rarely useful. The encoder, however, transfers beautifully.

Exception: in language modelling, the pretext task (next-token prediction) is genuinely useful — chat models *are* the language model.

## Four families of pretext tasks (slide 5)

| Family | Examples | Where used |
|---|---|---|
| **Next-token / masked prediction** | predict a masked word, pixel, or patch | GPT (text), BERT (text), MAE (images), Image-GPT |
| **Generative** | reconstruct or generate the input | autoencoders, GANs, diffusion models |
| **Transformation prediction** | predict the augmentation applied (e.g. rotation 0/90/180/270) | RotNet, jigsaw-puzzle networks |
| **Contrastive** | pull augmented views together, push other images apart | SimCLR, MoCo, DINO |

## Examples covered in this course

### Rotation prediction (slide 8)

Apply rotation $\{0°, 90°, 180°, 270°\}$ to an image, train a CNN to classify which rotation was applied. The label comes for free from the augmentation. To do this well, the network must learn what "right-side-up" means for many object categories — i.e. learn semantics.

### Next-token / masked prediction (slide 6)

Mask a word from a sentence and predict it. In the visual analogue (Masked Autoencoder, MAE), mask 75% of image patches and reconstruct them. The encoder learns to encode global image context.

### Contrastive (slides 9–18)

Self-supervised contrastive: take two augmented views of the same image as a positive pair; treat all other images in the batch as negatives. Train the encoder so positive pairs are close in embedding space. See [[simclr]] and [[contrastive-learning]].

## Why SSL works

The hypothesis: a representation good enough to solve any *meaningful* pretext task on natural data must capture the underlying structure of that data. If you can predict masked patches, you must have learned object parts; if you can tell two augmented views are the same image, you must have learned to ignore lighting / viewpoint / colour.

Empirically: SSL pretraining followed by fine-tuning on a labelled task often **matches or beats** training from scratch on ImageNet, even when the downstream labelled dataset is small.

## SSL in practice

1. **Pretrain** an encoder $f$ on a large unlabelled corpus using an SSL objective.
2. **Discard** any auxiliary heads (the rotation classifier, the projection head, the predictor).
3. **Fine-tune** $f$ + a new task-specific head on the small labelled dataset, often at a low learning rate (see [[transfer-learning]]).

Or for very low-data tasks: skip fine-tuning and use the pretrained $f$ as a frozen feature extractor with a kNN classifier (see [[few-shot-learning]]).

## Related

- [[contrastive-learning]] — one of the four pretext-task families
- [[simclr]] — canonical contrastive SSL framework
- [[clip]] — multimodal SSL on image-text pairs
- [[transfer-learning]] — what SSL pretraining feeds into
- [[data-augmentation]] — the engine that creates positive pairs in contrastive SSL

# Contrastive learning

A representation-learning approach: shape the embedding space so that **similar examples are pulled close** and **dissimilar examples are pushed apart**. The result is an encoder that maps semantically related inputs to nearby vectors.

## Two regimes

| Regime | How "similar" pairs are defined | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Supervised contrastive** | Pair labels: same class / same instance / domain-specific equivalence | FaceNet, image retrieval, metric learning |
| **Self-supervised contrastive** | Two augmented views of the same image | SimCLR, MoCo, DINO, CLIP |

The loss machinery is the same; the only difference is how you obtain positive pairs.

## Architecture: shared encoder

A single encoder $f$ is applied to both inputs (or all inputs in the case of NT-Xent on a batch). Implemented as a [[siamese-network]]: define one model and run it twice, giving the two outputs in parallel. Weights are shared by construction.

The output is a low-dimensional embedding (typically 128–768 dimensions) and the loss compares pairs of embeddings.

## Loss families (covered in [[triplet-loss]])

| Loss | Inputs | Idea |
|---|---|---|
| Margin / contrastive loss | one positive or negative pair | pull positive together; push negative past margin $M$ |
| Triplet loss | (anchor, positive, negative) | $d(a, p) < d(a, n) + M$ |
| NT-Xent / InfoNCE | one positive pair + many negatives in a batch | softmax over all batch members; matches the positive |

## Why contrastive losses can collapse — and how to prevent it (slide 12)

If the loss only requires similar items to be close, the trivial solution is "embed everything as the constant zero vector" — distance between any two embeddings is zero, "loss" is minimised, but the embeddings are useless.

Three classes of fix:

1. **Add negatives** (margin / triplet / NT-Xent) — the negative pair pushes embeddings away from each other, preventing collapse to a constant.
2. **Asymmetric architecture + stop-gradient** (BYOL, SimSiam) — predictor on one side, target network with frozen gradient on the other. The asymmetry prevents the trivial solution.
3. **Centring + sharpening** (DINO) — the teacher's softmax is centred (subtract the running mean) and sharpened (low temperature), preventing collapse onto a single mode.

## Pull / push intuition

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    z1[Positive 1] --> sp[shared embedding space]
    z2[Positive 2] --> sp
    n1[Negative 1] --> sp
    n2[Negative 2] --> sp
    sp --> pull[Pull z1 and z2 together]
    sp --> push[Push n1, n2 away]
```

After training, "similar" inputs cluster in the embedding space — making downstream tasks (classification by k-NN, retrieval by cosine similarity, clustering, anomaly detection) easy.

## Use cases

- **Face recognition** ([[face-recognition]]) — same person → same cluster.
- **Image retrieval** — query image vs database, nearest neighbour by cosine similarity.
- **Few-shot classification** ([[few-shot-learning]]) — kNN on a tiny support set.
- **Self-supervised pretraining** — produce a transferable backbone without labels.
- **Multimodal alignment** ([[clip]]) — align image and text embeddings.

## Related

- [[siamese-network]] — the architectural pattern
- [[triplet-loss]] — the canonical loss family
- [[simclr]] — self-supervised contrastive framework
- [[clip]] — multimodal contrastive learning
- [[self-supervised-learning]] — broader paradigm
- [[face-recognition]] — supervised contrastive use case

content.newText:
---
name: Siamese network
description: Twin neural networks with shared weights that process two inputs in parallel and compare their embeddings
type: concept
courses: [Computer-Vision]
source: [19_self_supervised_and_contrastive_learning.pdf]
created: 2026-04-26
tags: [concept, deep-learning, architecture, contrastive]
---

# Siamese network

A network architecture used for **comparing two inputs**. Two encoders with **identical (shared) weights** process two inputs in parallel; the outputs are compared via a similarity score. The same weights — actually the same network applied twice — guarantee that semantically similar inputs map to similar embeddings.

The architecture can be any choice (CNN, transformer, MLP), not just CNNs.

## Diagram (slide 11)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    x1[Image 1] --> e1[Encoder shared weights]
    x2[Image 2] --> e2[Encoder shared weights]
    e1 --> z1[Embedding z_1]
    e2 --> z2[Embedding z_2]
    z1 --> sim["similarity z_1, z_2"]
    z2 --> sim
    sim --> out[Similarity score]
```

In PyTorch you implement this by **defining one model and applying it twice** — the weights are shared by construction:

```python
model = MyModel(*params)

z1 = model(x1)
z2 = model(x2)
similarity = similarity_function(z1, z2)
loss = 1 - similarity   # minimise this
```

There is no "two-headed network" — just a single network applied twice in the same forward pass.

## Why shared weights matter

If $f_1 \neq f_2$, the two encoders could learn to produce different-looking embeddings for the same input — making "similarity" between them ill-defined. Sharing weights enforces that "looking similar" is a property of the inputs, not of the network branch.

## Triplet variant

For [[triplet-loss]], extend the same idea to three inputs: anchor, positive, negative. The encoder is applied three times (still one shared model). The loss compares all three embeddings.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    a[Anchor] --> e1[Encoder]
    p[Positive] --> e2[Encoder]
    n[Negative] --> e3[Encoder]
    e1 --> za[z_a]
    e2 --> zp[z_p]
    e3 --> zn[z_n]
    za --> tl[Triplet loss]
    zp --> tl
    zn --> tl
```

## Inference: comparing two arbitrary inputs

After training, the encoder produces embeddings; the two-input architecture is no longer needed:

- For verification: encode both inputs, compute cosine similarity, threshold.
- For retrieval: precompute embeddings of a database; encode the query; nearest-neighbour search.
- For clustering: encode all inputs; cluster the embeddings.

The siamese architecture is just the *training-time* construction; at inference time you have a normal encoder.

## When to use

- **Verification tasks** (face recognition, signature matching, plagiarism detection).
- **Retrieval / similarity search** at scale (image and text retrieval).
- **Few-shot classification** ([[few-shot-learning]]) — encoder + kNN over support set.
- **Self-supervised contrastive learning** ([[simclr]]) — augmented views are the two inputs.

## Related

- [[contrastive-learning]] — the broader paradigm
- [[triplet-loss]] — the typical loss
- [[simclr]] — self-supervised version with augmented views
- [[face-recognition]] — primary supervised use case
- [[clip]] — generalisation: two encoders for image + text (not exactly siamese since the encoders differ, but the same training principle)

content.newText:
---
name: Triplet loss (and the contrastive-loss family)
description: Loss on (anchor, positive, negative) — make d a p smaller than d a n by margin M; covers margin and NT-Xent variants
type: concept
courses: [Computer-Vision]
source: [19_self_supervised_and_contrastive_learning.pdf]
created: 2026-04-26
tags: [concept, deep-learning, loss, contrastive]
---

# Triplet loss (and the contrastive-loss family)

Three loss functions used to train [[siamese-network]]s for [[contrastive-learning]]. They differ in how many examples participate in one loss term.

## Margin loss (also called "contrastive loss," slide 13)

For a pair of embeddings $(z_1, z_2)$ labelled as positive (similar) or negative (dissimilar):

$$L(z_1, z_2) = \begin{cases} d(z_1, z_2) & \text{positive pair} \\ \max(0,\, M - d(z_1, z_2)) & \text{negative pair} \end{cases}$$

- For positives, the loss is just the distance — minimise it (pull together).
- For negatives, the loss is non-zero only when the distance is less than margin $M$ — push them apart until they're at least $M$ apart, then stop pushing.

$d$ is typically Euclidean distance or $1 - \mathrm{cosine\ similarity}$.

**Drawback** (slide 15): margin loss favours absolute distances, pushing positive distances toward 0. Embeddings tend to shrink, making representation collapse easier.

## Triplet loss (slide 14)

Three inputs: anchor $a$, positive $p$ (same class as $a$), negative $n$ (different class).

$$L(z_a, z_p, z_n) = \max(0,\, d(z_a, z_p) - d(z_a, z_n) + M)$$

Want: $d(a, p) + M < d(a, n)$ — the negative should be at least $M$ further from the anchor than the positive is. Loss is non-zero only when this isn't yet true.

**Advantage over margin loss**: it cares about *relative* distances, not absolute. Embeddings don't collapse to small values.

## Triplet mining (slide 15)

If anchor and positive are very similar but the negative is very different, the loss is already 0 — the network learns nothing from this triplet. To train effectively, select **hard triplets** where the loss is positive:

| Triplet difficulty | $d(a, p)$ vs $d(a, n)$ | Loss | Useful? |
|---|---|---|---|
| Easy | $d(a, n) > d(a, p) + M$ | 0 | no |
| Semi-hard | $d(a, p) < d(a, n) < d(a, p) + M$ | small | yes |
| Hard | $d(a, n) < d(a, p)$ | large | yes (sometimes too hard early in training) |

**Mining strategies**:
- **Online mining**: in each batch, find all valid triplets and use the hardest ones.
- **Batch hard**: for each anchor in the batch, take its hardest positive and hardest negative.
- **Semi-hard**: pick negatives that are within the margin (not yet violating, but close).

FaceNet (Google, 2015) used hard-triplet mining and reached >99% accuracy on Labeled Faces in the Wild — see [[face-recognition]].

## NT-Xent / InfoNCE (slide 16)

Generalise triplet loss to **one positive vs. all other batch members as negatives**. Used in SimCLR, MoCo, CLIP.

For a batch of $2N$ embeddings (one positive pair per of $N$ original images), define cosine similarity:

$$s_{i, j} = \frac{z_i^\top z_j}{\|z_i\|\,\|z_j\|}$$

For a positive pair $(i, j)$ — typically two augmented views of image $k$:

$$\ell(i, j) = -\log\frac{\exp(s_{i, j} / \tau)}{\sum_{k=1, k \neq i}^{2N} \exp(s_{i, k} / \tau)}$$

Loss for the whole batch:

$$\mathcal{L} = \frac{1}{2N}\sum_{k=1}^{N}\!\left[\ell(2k-1,\, 2k) + \ell(2k,\, 2k-1)\right]$$

- **Numerator**: similarity of the positive pair.
- **Denominator**: sum over all other batch members (the implicit negatives).
- **Temperature $\tau$**: lower $\tau$ → sharper softmax → harder negatives matter more.

This is just **softmax cross-entropy on the similarity matrix**, treating the matched augmentation pair as the positive class. With a batch of 256, that's 510 negatives for free per anchor — dramatically more contrastive signal than triplet sampling can give.

## Comparison

| Loss | Inputs | Negatives | Strengths |
|---|---|---|---|
| Margin loss | one labelled pair | one explicit negative | simple |
| Triplet loss | (a, p, n) | one explicit negative per triplet | relative distances; needs mining |
| NT-Xent / InfoNCE | one positive pair + entire batch | all other batch members as implicit negatives | many negatives per anchor for free; standard for SSL |

## Related

- [[contrastive-learning]] — the broader paradigm
- [[siamese-network]] — the training architecture
- [[simclr]] — uses NT-Xent
- [[clip]] — uses NT-Xent on image-text pairs
- [[face-recognition]] — primary triplet-loss application

content.newText:
---
name: SimCLR
description: Self-supervised contrastive framework — augment each image twice, use NT-Xent with all other batch members as negatives
type: concept
courses: [Computer-Vision]
source: [19_self_supervised_and_contrastive_learning.pdf]
created: 2026-04-26
tags: [concept, deep-learning, self-supervised, contrastive]
---

# SimCLR

A 2020 self-supervised contrastive framework (Chen et al, Google). The simplest and most influential image-only SSL method. Trains a representation by maximising agreement between two augmented views of the same image, with all other images in the batch acting as implicit negatives.

## Pipeline (slide 18)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    x[Image x] --> a1["augment t ~ T"]
    x --> a2["augment t' ~ T"]
    a1 --> v1[View x_i]
    a2 --> v2[View x_j]
    v1 --> f1[Encoder f]
    v2 --> f2[Encoder f]
    f1 --> h1[Repr h_i]
    f2 --> h2[Repr h_j]
    h1 --> g1[Projection head g]
    h2 --> g2[Projection head g]
    g1 --> z1[z_i]
    g2 --> z2[z_j]
    z1 --> loss[NT-Xent loss]
    z2 --> loss
```

## Five components

1. **Augmentation family $\mathcal{T}$**: random crop + resize, colour jitter, Gaussian blur, random horizontal flip. Sample two augmentations $t, t' \sim \mathcal{T}$ and apply them to the same image.
2. **Encoder $f$**: a CNN (typically ResNet-50) producing a representation $h$.
3. **Projection head $g$**: a small 2-layer MLP that maps $h$ to a lower-dimensional space $z$ where the contrastive loss is applied. After training, $g$ is **discarded**.
4. **NT-Xent loss** on the projections $z_i, z_j$ — see [[triplet-loss]].
5. **Large batch size** for many negatives per anchor.

## NT-Xent recap

For batch size $N$, two augmented views per image gives $2N$ embeddings. For each positive pair $(i, j)$:

$$\ell(i, j) = -\log\frac{\exp(s_{i, j} / \tau)}{\sum_{k=1, k \neq i}^{2N} \exp(s_{i, k} / \tau)}$$

Total loss averages over both directions of every positive pair. The other $2N - 2$ batch members are negatives.

## Why each design choice matters

| Choice | Why |
|---|---|
| Strong augmentations | Different views must look different on the surface but encode the same semantic content. The encoder learns invariance to the augmentations applied. |
| Random crop + colour jitter | Most important pair — accounts for most of the gain. |
| Projection head $g$ before loss | The representation $h$ ends up better than $z$ for downstream tasks. Imposes the contrastive structure on $z$ rather than on $h$, leaving $h$ free to retain richer information. |
| Discard $g$ at inference | $z$ is shaped for the contrastive task; $h$ is shaped for general utility. |
| Large batch (4k+) | More negatives = harder contrastive task = better representations. SimCLR's gains scale with batch size up to ~8k. |
| Long training (800–1000 epochs) | SSL needs more compute than supervised training to reach equivalent representation quality. |

## Performance

After SSL pretraining, freeze the encoder and train a linear classifier on ImageNet labels:

- SimCLR (ResNet-50, 4× width): ~76% top-1 on ImageNet — within 2-3 points of fully supervised ResNet-50.
- After fine-tuning the entire network on 1% of ImageNet labels: SimCLR matches the fully-supervised baseline trained on 100% of labels.

## Variants

- **MoCo (v1, v2, v3)**: maintain a large queue of negatives from past batches; doesn't need huge batches.
- **SimSiam (slide 19)**: removes negatives entirely; uses asymmetric architecture (predictor + stop-gradient) to prevent collapse.
- **BYOL**: similar to SimSiam, target network as EMA of online network.
- **DINO (slide 20)**: self-distillation with a teacher that's an EMA of the student; emergent property of segmenting foreground objects without any segmentation labels.

## Related

- [[self-supervised-learning]] — the broader paradigm
- [[contrastive-learning]] — the loss family
- [[triplet-loss]] — covers NT-Xent
- [[siamese-network]] — the architectural pattern (with shared encoder)
- [[data-augmentation]] — what generates the positive pairs
- [[clip]] — multimodal extension of the same contrastive idea
