```mermaid
    a1 --> v1[View x_i]
    a2 --> v2[View x_j]
    v1 --> f1[Encoder f]
    v2 --> f2[Encoder f]
    f1 --> h1[Repr h_i]
    f2 --> h2[Repr h_j]
    h1 --> g1[Projection head g]
    h2 --> g2[Projection head g]
    g1 --> z1[z_i]
    g2 --> z2[z_j]
    z1 --> loss[NT-Xent loss]
    z2 --> loss
```

## Five components

1. **Augmentation family $\mathcal{T}$**: random crop + resize, colour jitter, Gaussian blur, random horizontal flip. Sample two augmentations $t, t' \sim \mathcal{T}$ and apply them to the same image.
2. **Encoder $f$**: a CNN (typically ResNet-50) producing a representation $h$.
3. **Projection head $g$**: a small 2-layer MLP that maps $h$ to a lower-dimensional space $z$ where the contrastive loss is applied. After training, $g$ is **discarded**.
4. **NT-Xent loss** on the projections $z_i, z_j$ — see [[triplet-loss]].
5. **Large batch size** for many negatives per anchor.

## NT-Xent recap

For batch size $N$, two augmented views per image gives $2N$ embeddings. For each positive pair $(i, j)$:

$$\ell(i, j) = -\log\frac{\exp(s_{i, j} / \tau)}{\sum_{k=1, k \neq i}^{2N} \exp(s_{i, k} / \tau)}$$

Total loss averages over both directions of every positive pair. The other $2N - 2$ batch members are negatives.

## Why each design choice matters

| Choice | Why |
|---|---|
| Strong augmentations | Different views must look different on the surface but encode the same semantic content. The encoder learns invariance to the augmentations applied. |
| Random crop + colour jitter | Most important pair — accounts for most of the gain. |
| Projection head $g$ before loss | The representation $h$ ends up better than $z$ for downstream tasks. Imposes the contrastive structure on $z$ rather than on $h$, leaving $h$ free to retain richer information. |
| Discard $g$ at inference | $z$ is shaped for the contrastive task; $h$ is shaped for general utility. |
| Large batch (4k+) | More negatives = harder contrastive task = better representations. SimCLR's gains scale with batch size up to ~8k. |
| Long training (800–1000 epochs) | SSL needs more compute than supervised training to reach equivalent representation quality. |

## Performance

After SSL pretraining, freeze the encoder and train a linear classifier on ImageNet labels:

- SimCLR (ResNet-50, 4× width): ~76% top-1 on ImageNet — within 2-3 points of fully supervised ResNet-50.
- After fine-tuning the entire network on 1% of ImageNet labels: SimCLR matches the fully-supervised baseline trained on 100% of labels.

## Variants

- **MoCo (v1, v2, v3)**: maintain a large queue of negatives from past batches; doesn't need huge batches.
- **SimSiam (slide 19)**: removes negatives entirely; uses asymmetric architecture (predictor + stop-gradient) to prevent collapse.
- **BYOL**: similar to SimSiam, target network as EMA of online network.
- **DINO (slide 20)**: self-distillation with a teacher that's an EMA of the student; emergent property of segmenting foreground objects without any segmentation labels.

## Related

- [[self-supervised-learning]] — the broader paradigm
- [[contrastive-learning]] — the loss family
- [[triplet-loss]] — covers NT-Xent
- [[siamese-network]] — the architectural pattern (with shared encoder)
- [[data-augmentation]] — what generates the positive pairs
- [[clip]] — multimodal extension of the same contrastive idea

content.newText:
--- name: CLIP (Contrastive Language-Image Pretraining) description: Joint image and text contrastive pretraining on 400M image-caption pairs — enables zero-shot classification by phrasing tasks as text prompts type: concept courses: [Computer-Vision] source: [19_self_supervised_and_contrastive_learning.pdf] created: 2026-04-26 tags: [concept, deep-learning, multimodal, foundation-model] --- # CLIP (Contrastive Language-Image Pretraining) A 2021 multimodal model from OpenAI. Trained contrastively on **400 million (image, caption) pairs** scraped from the web. Produces a **shared image-text embedding space** where matched (image, caption) pairs are close. The big payoff: any new image classification task can be solved **zero-shot** — without further training — by phrasing class names as text prompts. See [[zero-shot-learning]]. ## Architecture Two encoders mapping into a shared $d$-dimensional embedding space: - **Image encoder**: a vision model (originally ResNet, modern variants use [[vision-transformer]]). - **Text encoder**: a transformer that reads a caption. A linear projection on each encoder's output places both into the same space, then $\ell_2$-normalises so cosine similarity equals dot product. ## Training (slide 35) ```mermaid flowchart LR img[Image batch I_1..I_N] --> ie[Image encoder] txt[Caption batch T_1..T_N] --> te[Text encoder] ie --> iemb[Image embeddings] te --> temb[Text embeddings] iemb --> sim[N x N cosine similarity matrix] temb --> sim sim --> ntx[NT-Xent loss: diagonal entries are positives;
off-diagonal are negatives] ``` For a batch of $N$ image-text pairs, compute the $N \times N$ similarity matrix $S_{ij} = I_i \cdot T_j$. The objective: rows $i$ should match column $i$ (diagonal = positives), rows $i$ should not match column $j \neq i$ (off-diagonal = negatives). Apply NT-Xent / softmax cross-entropy in both directions (image-to-text and text-to-image). ## Training data: WIT (slide 36) The Wikipedia-based Image Text dataset: 400M image-caption pairs (the "100M images at 4 captions each" approximation). Quality is lower than ImageNet labels but quantity is two orders of magnitude greater. The breadth of captions — common-sense descriptions, named entities, attributes — is what gives CLIP its semantic generality. ## Zero-shot classification (slides 37–39) To classify an image into one of $K$ classes, **without any training**: ```mermaid flowchart LR classes[Class names: plane, car, dog, ...] --> prompt["Wrap as 'A photo of a .'"] prompt --> tenc[CLIP text encoder] tenc --> tembs[Text embeddings T_1..T_K] img[New image] --> ienc[CLIP image encoder] ienc --> iemb[Image embedding I] iemb --> sim[cosine similarity to each T_k] tembs --> sim sim --> argmax[argmax = predicted class] ``` The class with the highest cosine similarity to the image embedding wins. **No fine-tuning, no extra data.** Empirically, CLIP zero-shot reaches ~76% top-1 on ImageNet — matching a fully-supervised ResNet-50 trained on the actual ImageNet labels (slide 33). ## Why CLIP generalises better than ImageNet-trained ResNet (slide 33) On the ImageNet test set, CLIP and ResNet-50 score the same. But on **distribution shifts** (slides 33–34): | Dataset | ImageNet ResNet-50 | CLIP ViT-L | |---|---|---| | ImageNet | 76% | 76% | | ImageNet V2 | 64% | 70% | | ImageNet Rendition (paintings, drawings) | 38% | 89% | | ObjectNet (objects in unusual poses) | 33% | 72% | | ImageNet Sketch (line drawings) | 25% | 60% | | ImageNet Adversarial | 3% | 77% | The contrastive image-text objective forces the encoder to learn what makes a banana a banana **across many depictions** — photographic, drawn, sketched, in unusual poses. ImageNet supervised training only sees photos, so the model latches onto photographic-specific features. ## Other uses - **Zero-shot image search**: encode a database of images once; query with text prompts via cosine similarity. - **Zero-shot detection**: pair CLIP embeddings with a region proposer (e.g. OWL-ViT, GLIP). - **As a feature extractor**: the image encoder transfers well to downstream tasks via linear probes. - **As a building block**: feeds DALL-E, Stable Diffusion, image-text retrieval systems. ## Limitations (slide 41) CLIP zero-shot is strong on natural-image benchmarks (Stanford Cars +29, Food101 +23 vs ImageNet baseline) but weaker on: - Specialised / scientific datasets (MNIST, satellite imagery, medical scans) — not in WIT. - Tasks requiring fine-grained counting or precise spatial reasoning. - Classes that aren't easily described in natural language. ## Related - [[contrastive-learning]] — the loss - [[triplet-loss]] — NT-Xent in particular - [[zero-shot-learning]] — the inference paradigm CLIP enables - [[self-supervised-learning]] — broader family (CLIP is multimodal SSL) - [[vision-transformer]] — the image encoder backbone in modern CLIP variants

content.newText:
--- name: Few-shot / one-shot learning description: Predict on classes with only K labelled examples each, typically via k-NN in a pretrained embedding space type: concept courses: [Computer-Vision] source: [19_self_supervised_and_contrastive_learning.pdf] created: 2026-04-26 tags: [concept, deep-learning, transfer-learning] --- # Few-shot / one-shot learning Predicting on **new classes** for which only a handful of labelled examples (typically 1–10 per class) are available. **K-shot prediction** = "you've seen $K$ examples of each class." Distinct from training a fresh model on small data — few-shot leverages a pretrained representation and skips most of the training. ## The k-NN recipe (slides 22–23) ```mermaid flowchart LR sup["Support set: K examples per new class"] --> enc1[Pretrained encoder f] enc1 --> emb["Embeddings averaged per class
= class prototypes"] query[Query image] --> enc2[Same encoder f] enc2 --> qemb[Query embedding] qemb --> cos[Cosine similarity to each class prototype] emb --> cos cos --> pred["Predicted class = argmax"] ``` Three steps: 1. **Encode the support set** ($K$ examples per new class) using a pretrained encoder $f$. Take the mean embedding per class as the **class prototype**. 2. **Encode the query** with the same $f$. 3. **Predict** the nearest-prototype class by cosine similarity (or Euclidean distance). No training. No fine-tuning. The encoder $f$ doesn't change. The support set replaces what would normally be a labelled training set. It's small enough that a human can curate it on the spot. ## Why this works only with the right encoder The encoder must already produce embeddings where same-class examples cluster — typically achieved by: - [[contrastive-learning]] (e.g. SimCLR, [[clip]] image encoder). - Large-scale supervised pretraining on a related task (ImageNet classification gives a usable encoder for many natural-image tasks, though not as good as contrastive). - [[self-supervised-learning]] in general (DINO, MAE). A randomly-initialised encoder would give nonsense distances. The whole approach assumes a **good representation pre-exists**. ## Variants - **Mean prototype** (Prototypical Networks, Snell et al 2017): average per class. - **k-NN classifier**: rank query against every support example individually, vote. - **Match by L2 with learned metric** (Matching Networks, Vinyals 2016): tweak a small parametric layer to be the metric. ## Typical accuracy On miniImageNet (a 5-way 1-shot benchmark): a CLIP-trained or DINO-trained encoder + nearest prototype reaches 70-80%+ accuracy on classes never seen during training. Fine-tuning a network from scratch on 1 example would not move past random. ## Why not fine-tuning instead? | Few-shot k-NN | Few-shot fine-tuning | |---|---| | No training step at all | Train a head (or low LR backbone) on the support set | | 0 GPU seconds | A few minutes per task | | Stable, no overfitting risk | Easy to overfit on $K = 1$ | | Limited by encoder quality | Can specialise the encoder if support set is bigger | For very small support sets ($K \le 5$), k-NN often **outperforms** fine-tuning because there isn't enough data to fine-tune meaningfully without overfitting. ## Use cases - Image retrieval where users provide a few example "good" images. - Domain adaptation where the new domain has a few labelled examples. - Quick prototyping before annotating a full training set. - [[face-recognition]] enrolment: 1 photo per person added to the database. ## Related - [[contrastive-learning]] — produces the embedding space few-shot relies on - [[clip]] — strong zero-shot and few-shot encoder - [[face-recognition]] — practical example - [[transfer-learning]] — broader fine-tuning paradigm - [[zero-shot-learning]] — extreme case ($K = 0$)

content.newText:
---
name: Zero-shot learning
description: Predict on classes with zero labelled examples — by leveraging semantic descriptions or text embeddings of the class names
type: concept
courses: [Computer-Vision]
source: [19_self_supervised_and_contrastive_learning.pdf]
created: 2026-04-26
tags: [concept, deep-learning, transfer-learning, multimodal]
---

# Zero-shot learning

Predicting class labels for data **never seen at training time** — and never seen *as labelled examples either*. Bridges known and unknown classes via **semantic relationships**: a description of the class (a name, an attribute vector, a textual definition) replaces labelled examples.

The key idea: a human can recognise a "zebra" from the description "horse-like animal with black and white stripes" without ever seeing one. Deep models can do the same if the description is encoded into a vector compatible with the model's embedding space.

## Two approaches

### Attribute-based (older, pre-CLIP)

Each class is described by an attribute vector (e.g. has-stripes, has-fur, four-legs, can-fly) hand-engineered from the dataset's class hierarchy. The model is trained to predict attributes from images. At test time, predict attributes for a new image and match to the unseen class with closest attribute vector.

Limited by the quality and coverage of the attribute schema. Used in academic benchmarks; rare in practice now.

### CLIP-style: text encoder as the class describer (slides 35–38)

Modern zero-shot. A multimodal model ([[clip]]) is pretrained on image-caption pairs to produce a **shared image-text embedding space**. At test time:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classes[Class names: plane, car, dog, ...] --> prompt["Wrap each as 'A photo of a <class>.'"]
    prompt --> tenc[CLIP text encoder]
    tenc --> tembs[Text embeddings T_1..T_K]
    img[New image] --> ienc[CLIP image encoder]
    ienc --> iemb[Image embedding I]
    iemb --> sim[cosine similarity to each T_k]
    tembs --> sim
    sim --> argmax[argmax = predicted class]
```

The class with the highest cosine similarity to the image wins. **No training step.** Wholly new classes, dictated only by text prompts.

## Beyond classification

The same trick extends to other tasks (slides 42–43):

- **Zero-shot semantic search**: encode an image database once. Query with text. Retrieve top images by similarity. (Reverse: encode a text database, query with images.)
- **Zero-shot object detection**: pair CLIP with a region proposer (OWL-ViT, GLIP). Predict whether each proposed region matches any text prompt.
- **Open-vocabulary segmentation**: similar idea for masks (MaskCLIP, OpenSeg).

## Why CLIP-style zero-shot beats traditional supervised models on distribution shifts (slide 33)

| Dataset | ResNet-50 supervised | CLIP zero-shot |
|---|---|---|
| ImageNet | 76% | 76% |
| ImageNet Rendition | 38% | 89% |
| ObjectNet | 33% | 72% |
| ImageNet Sketch | 25% | 60% |
| ImageNet Adversarial | 3% | 77% |

The supervised model has overfit to ImageNet's photographic style. CLIP, trained on 400M diverse captioned images, has learned what makes a banana a banana across many depictions.

## Limitations

- **Domain mismatch**: if the target classes don't appear in pretraining data (medical specifics, scientific instruments, niche flora), zero-shot will fail.
- **Prompt sensitivity**: "a photo of a {class}" vs "a {class}" can differ by several percentage points on some benchmarks.
- **Fine-grained classes**: distinguishing "Honda Accord 2012" from "Honda Accord 2013" is harder zero-shot than coarse categories.
- **Counting, spatial layout, exact attributes**: CLIP-style models are weak here; they encode rough semantics, not precise structured properties.

## Related

- [[clip]] — the canonical zero-shot model
- [[contrastive-learning]] — the training paradigm
- [[few-shot-learning]] — the regime with $K > 0$ examples
- [[self-supervised-learning]] — broader pretraining paradigm

content.newText:
---
name: Face recognition
description: Detect, align, and embed faces; verification and identification via similarity in the embedding space
type: concept
courses: [Computer-Vision]
source: [19_self_supervised_and_contrastive_learning.pdf]
created: 2026-04-26
tags: [concept, deep-learning, computer-vision, contrastive]
---

# Face recognition

The canonical industrial application of [[contrastive-learning]]. Built on three stages:

1. **Detect** the face in an image (object detection, often [[yolo]]).
2. **Align** the detected crop using facial landmarks (rotate/scale to a canonical pose).
3. **Embed** the aligned face with a contrastively-trained encoder, producing a feature vector.
4. **Compare** that embedding to one or more reference embeddings via cosine similarity.

## Pipeline (slide 26)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    img[Photo] --> det[Face detection - YOLO style]
    det --> align[Align - perspective transform]
    align --> enc[Deep CNN feature extractor - contrastively trained]
    enc --> emb[Face embedding]
    emb --> q{Task}
    q -->|verification| ver[Compare to claimed identity in DB]
    q -->|identification| iden[Compare to all DB entries]
```

## Identification vs verification (slides 27–29)

| | Identification | Verification |
|---|---|---|
| Question | "Who is this?" | "Is this Juan Pérez?" |
| Output | one of $K$ class IDs | binary yes/no |
| Architecture | classifier with $K$ outputs | similarity threshold against one reference |
| Adding a new person | requires re-training (or extending the head and fine-tuning) | just enrol (encode and store the embedding) |

**Identification via verification** (slide 29): run verification against every entry in the database; return the best match (or "unknown" if no match passes a similarity threshold). This is how production face recognition usually works — it scales to new identities without re-training.

## Why classifier-based identification doesn't scale

A classifier with a softmax over $K$ identities has $K$ output neurons. To add a person you have to:

- Add a neuron to the output layer.
- Collect labelled examples and fine-tune.
- Risk catastrophic forgetting on existing identities.

A similarity-based system enrols in O(1): encode the new person's reference photo and store the embedding. The encoder itself never changes.

## Training the embedding extractor

Trained contrastively on a face dataset where same-person pairs are positives and different-person pairs are negatives. Standard losses:

- **Triplet loss** (FaceNet, slide 31): $L = \max(0, d(a, p) - d(a, n) + M)$.
- **Margin loss** on pairs.
- **NT-Xent / InfoNCE** style on batches.

See [[triplet-loss]] for the formal definitions.

## FaceNet (slide 31)

Google, 2015. Trained a deep CNN with **triplet loss** and **online hard-triplet mining** (selecting triplets where the negative is too close to the anchor). Reached >99% accuracy on the Labeled Faces in the Wild (LFW) benchmark. Established the contrastive-embedding approach to face recognition that's still standard.

## Datasets

- **CelebA**: 10k celebrities, 200k images, with attribute labels (smiling, glasses, hat).
- **Labeled Faces in the Wild (LFW)**: classic verification benchmark.
- **MS-Celeb-1M, CASIA-WebFace**: million-scale training corpora.
- **VGGFace, VGGFace2**: large research datasets.

## Face landmarks (slide 25)

Localising 68 (or 478) keypoints on a face — eyes, nose tip, mouth corners, jawline. Used for alignment (rotate the face so the eyes are level), 3D modelling, and downstream tasks like emotion recognition. Modern landmarkers (e.g. Mediapipe Face Landmarker) run in real time on mobile devices.

## Privacy and ethics

Face recognition raises serious surveillance and consent issues. Several jurisdictions ban or restrict it:

- EU AI Act classifies facial recognition for surveillance as high-risk.
- Several US cities (San Francisco, Boston) ban government use.
- GDPR treats face embeddings as biometric data.

Consider these implications before building or deploying.

## Related

- [[contrastive-learning]] — the training paradigm
- [[siamese-network]] — the architecture
- [[triplet-loss]] — the training loss (FaceNet)
- [[few-shot-learning]] — closely related (k-NN at enrolment time)
- [[yolo]] — used for the detection stage

# Face recognition

The canonical industrial application of [[contrastive-learning]]. Built on three stages:

1. **Detect** the face in an image (object detection, often [[yolo]]).
2. **Align** the detected crop using facial landmarks (rotate/scale to a canonical pose).
3. **Embed** the aligned face with a contrastively-trained encoder, producing a feature vector.
4. **Compare** that embedding to one or more reference embeddings via cosine similarity.

## Pipeline (slide 26)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    img[Photo] --> det[Face detection - YOLO style]
    det --> align[Align - perspective transform]
    align --> enc[Deep CNN feature extractor - contrastively trained]
    enc --> emb[Face embedding]
    emb --> q{Task}
    q -->|verification| ver[Compare to claimed identity in DB]
    q -->|identification| iden[Compare to all DB entries]
```

## Identification vs verification (slides 27–29)

| | Identification | Verification |
|---|---|---|
| Question | "Who is this?" | "Is this Juan Pérez?" |
| Output | one of $K$ class IDs | binary yes/no |
| Architecture | classifier with $K$ outputs | similarity threshold against one reference |
| Adding a new person | requires re-training (or extending the head and fine-tuning) | just enrol (encode and store the embedding) |

**Identification via verification** (slide 29): run verification against every entry in the database; return the best match (or "unknown" if no match passes a similarity threshold). This is how production face recognition usually works — it scales to new identities without re-training.

## Why classifier-based identification doesn't scale

A classifier with a softmax over $K$ identities has $K$ output neurons. To add a person you have to:

- Add a neuron to the output layer.
- Collect labelled examples and fine-tune.
- Risk catastrophic forgetting on existing identities.

A similarity-based system enrols in O(1): encode the new person's reference photo and store the embedding. The encoder itself never changes.

## Training the embedding extractor

Trained contrastively on a face dataset where same-person pairs are positives and different-person pairs are negatives. Standard losses:

- **Triplet loss** (FaceNet, slide 31): $L = \max(0, d(a, p) - d(a, n) + M)$.
- **Margin loss** on pairs.
- **NT-Xent / InfoNCE** style on batches.

See [[triplet-loss]] for the formal definitions.

## FaceNet (slide 31)

Google, 2015. Trained a deep CNN with **triplet loss** and **online hard-triplet mining** (selecting triplets where the negative is too close to the anchor). Reached >99% accuracy on the Labeled Faces in the Wild (LFW) benchmark. Established the contrastive-embedding approach to face recognition that's still standard.

## Datasets

- **CelebA**: 10k celebrities, 200k images, with attribute labels (smiling, glasses, hat).
- **Labeled Faces in the Wild (LFW)**: classic verification benchmark.
- **MS-Celeb-1M, CASIA-WebFace**: million-scale training corpora.
- **VGGFace, VGGFace2**: large research datasets.

## Face landmarks (slide 25)

## Privacy and ethics

Face recognition raises serious surveillance and consent issues. Several jurisdictions ban or restrict it:

- EU AI Act classifies facial recognition for surveillance as high-risk.
- Several US cities (San Francisco, Boston) ban government use.
- GDPR treats face embeddings as biometric data.

Consider these implications before building or deploying.

## Related

- [[contrastive-learning]] — the training paradigm
- [[siamese-network]] — the architecture
- [[triplet-loss]] — the training loss (FaceNet)
- [[few-shot-learning]] — closely related (k-NN at enrolment time)
- [[yolo]] — used for the detection stage

content.newText:
This is **feature extraction** as written. To do fine-tuning, leave the `for` loop out (so all parameters trainable) and use a low LR for the backbone.

## Where the pretrained backbone comes from

Two common sources:

- **Supervised pretraining** on a labelled corpus (typically ImageNet). Most `torchvision.models` come this way.
- **Self-supervised pretraining** on a large unlabelled corpus (see [[self-supervised-learning]]). Models like DINOv2, MAE, SimCLR. Increasingly the default since SSL representations transfer better to distant domains than ImageNet-supervised ones.

For very small target datasets, a self-supervised backbone (or a [[clip]]-style multimodal one) often beats an ImageNet-supervised backbone of the same size.

## Related

- [[data-augmentation]] — critical when fine-tuning on small datasets
- [[loss-functions]] — same losses; what changes is the head, not the loss
- [[cnn-architecture]] — typical backbones to start from
- [[vision-transformer]] — increasingly common as a fine-tuning starting point
- [[self-supervised-learning]] — alternative pretraining source
- [[few-shot-learning]] — extreme case (frozen encoder + kNN, no fine-tuning)

content.newText:
---
tags: [flashcards, review]
course: Computer-Vision
lecture: 19
source: 19_self_supervised_and_contrastive_learning.pdf
created: 2026-04-26
---

# Lecture 19 — Self-supervised & contrastive learning (flashcards)

What is self-supervised learning, and how does it differ from unsupervised learning?
?
SSL trains a supervised model on a **pretext task whose labels are derived from the data itself** — no human annotation needed. Differs from purely unsupervised (e.g. clustering) in that there's still a supervised classification/regression objective; the labels just come for free from the data.

In SSL, what is the relationship between the pretext task and the goal?
?
The pretext task is a **means**, not the goal. The goal is to learn useful representations that transfer to downstream tasks. The pretext task itself (e.g. predicting image rotation) is rarely useful on its own — but if a model can solve it, the encoder must have learned semantically useful structure.

Name the four families of SSL pretext tasks.
?
1) Next-token / masked prediction (GPT, BERT, MAE). 2) Generative (autoencoders, GANs). 3) Transformation prediction (rotation, jigsaw). 4) Contrastive (SimCLR, MoCo, DINO).

What is a Siamese network and how is it implemented in PyTorch?
?
Two encoders with **shared weights** that process two inputs in parallel and produce embeddings to compare. In PyTorch you implement it by **defining one model and applying it twice** — `z1 = model(x1); z2 = model(x2)` — weights are shared by construction.

Write the margin (contrastive) loss for a labelled pair.
?
$L(z_1, z_2) = d(z_1, z_2)$ if positive pair; $\max(0,\, M - d(z_1, z_2))$ if negative pair. Pulls positives to 0 distance; pushes negatives past margin $M$.

Write the triplet loss formula and explain each component.
?
$L(z_a, z_p, z_n) = \max(0, d(z_a, z_p) - d(z_a, z_n) + M)$. Anchor $a$, positive $p$ (same class), negative $n$ (different class). Want $d(a, p) + M < d(a, n)$ — the negative should be at least $M$ further from the anchor than the positive is.

Why does triplet loss tend to work better than margin loss?
?
Margin loss favours **absolute** distances close to 0, encouraging embeddings to shrink and risking representation collapse. Triplet loss focuses on **relative** distances — only requires $d(a, n) > d(a, p) + M$, regardless of absolute scales — so embeddings have more flexibility.

What's "triplet mining" and why is it necessary?
?
Selecting **hard triplets** during training. If anchor and positive are very similar but the negative is very different, $d(a,n) > d(a,p) + M$ already and the loss is 0 — no learning signal. Mining filters for triplets where the loss is positive (hard or semi-hard), so each step actually trains the encoder.

Why can a Siamese network using only positive pairs collapse, and how does triplet loss prevent this?
?
With only positives, the trivial solution is "embed everything as the same constant vector" — distance between any two embeddings is 0, loss is minimised. Triplet loss adds a negative term ($d(a, n)$) that pushes embeddings apart and prevents this collapse.

Write the NT-Xent loss for a positive pair $(i, j)$ in a batch of $2N$ embeddings.
?
$\ell(i, j) = -\log\!\frac{\exp(s_{i,j}/\tau)}{\sum_{k\neq i}^{2N}\exp(s_{i,k}/\tau)}$, where $s_{i,j}$ is cosine similarity and $\tau$ is temperature. Numerator: positive pair. Denominator: sum over all batch members. It's just softmax cross-entropy with the matched augmentation pair as the positive class.

Why does SimCLR work without explicit class labels?
?
Two augmented views of the same image act as the **positive pair**; all other images in the batch are **implicit negatives**. The encoder must learn invariance to the augmentations (crop, colour jitter, blur) — and to do so, it has to encode the underlying semantic content of the image.

What's the role of SimCLR's projection head $g$, and what happens to it after training?
?
$g$ is a small MLP applied to the encoder output before the contrastive loss. Imposes the contrastive structure on $z = g(h)$ rather than on $h$ directly. After training, $g$ is **discarded**; the representation $h$ is what's used downstream.

Why does SimCLR need very large batch sizes?
?
The denominator of NT-Xent is the sum over all batch members → more batch members = more negatives per positive pair = harder contrastive task = better representations. SimCLR's gains scale with batch size up to ~8k.

How does SimSiam avoid the need for negative pairs?
?
By introducing **architectural asymmetry**: a predictor MLP on one branch and a stop-gradient on the other. Gradients only flow through the predictor side; the asymmetry prevents the trivial collapse to a constant embedding even without negatives.

What is DINO and what's its emergent property?
?
A self-supervised contrastive method using **self-distillation**: a student network and a teacher network with the same architecture but different parameters (teacher is an exponential moving average of the student). Stop-gradient + centring + sharpening prevents collapse. Emergent property: DINO learns to **segment foreground objects** without any segmentation labels.

How does few-shot prediction work in practice with a pretrained encoder?
?
1) Encode each support-set example with frozen pretrained $f$. 2) Compute one prototype per class (mean of its support embeddings). 3) Encode the query. 4) Predict the class with highest cosine similarity to its prototype. **No training step.**

What's the difference between identification and verification in face recognition?
?
**Identification**: "who is this?" — output is one of $K$ identity IDs. **Verification**: "is this Juan Pérez?" — binary yes/no against one reference. Production systems usually do "identification via verification": run verification against every database entry and pick the best match (or "unknown").

Why does similarity-based face recognition scale better than classifier-based identification?
?
A classifier has $K$ output neurons — adding a new identity requires extending the head and fine-tuning. A similarity-based system enrols a new person in O(1): encode their reference photo, store the embedding. The encoder never changes.

What is FaceNet, and what loss did it use?
?
A 2015 face-recognition system from Google. Trained a deep CNN with **triplet loss** and online **hard-triplet mining**. Reached >99% accuracy on the LFW benchmark — established the contrastive-embedding approach to face recognition that's still standard.

What is zero-shot learning?
?
Predicting classes that were **never seen at training time** by leveraging semantic relationships — a description of the class (text prompt or attribute vector) replaces labelled examples. CLIP-style zero-shot uses a text encoder to convert class names into the same embedding space as image features.

How does CLIP enable zero-shot image classification at inference time?
?
1) Wrap each class name as a text prompt ("A photo of a {class}."). 2) Encode each prompt with the text encoder → $T_1, \ldots, T_K$. 3) Encode the test image with the image encoder → $I$. 4) Predicted class = $\arg\max_k \cos(I, T_k)$. No training step.

How was CLIP trained, and on what dataset?
?
On 400M (image, caption) pairs scraped from the web (WIT-style). For each batch of $N$ pairs, build the $N \times N$ cosine similarity matrix and apply NT-Xent: diagonal = positive (matched) pairs; off-diagonal = negatives. Both image-to-text and text-to-image directions of the loss.

Why does CLIP zero-shot generalise better than supervised ResNet on distribution shifts?
?
Supervised ResNet trained on ImageNet learns photographic-specific features. CLIP, trained on 400M diverse captioned images (paintings, sketches, weird poses), learns **what makes a banana a banana across many depictions**. So on ImageNet-Sketch, ImageNet-Rendition, etc., CLIP's accuracy stays high while supervised models collapse.

What's a key practical limitation of CLIP zero-shot?
?
Domain mismatch: classes that don't appear in the pretraining data (specific medical conditions, niche scientific instruments, unusual flora) won't be recognised. Also weak on fine-grained tasks (distinguishing 2012 vs 2013 Honda Accord), counting, and precise spatial reasoning.

Why does the same NT-Xent loss apply to both SimCLR and CLIP?
?
Both are contrastive. SimCLR's positive pair = two augmented views of one image. CLIP's positive pair = matched (image, caption). In both cases the loss is "make the positive pair's similarity large vs all other batch members." The difference is what counts as a positive pair, not the loss form.

What's the difference between few-shot and zero-shot learning?
?
**Few-shot** ($K \ge 1$): you have $K$ labelled examples per new class; predict via k-NN against support-set embeddings. **Zero-shot** ($K = 0$): no labelled examples; predict via similarity to text prompts of class names (CLIP) or attribute vectors. Both rely on a strong pretrained representation.

content.text: ### Lecture 20 — Autoencoders

### Lecture 20 — Autoencoders file plan

**Lecture note:** `lecture-20-autoencoders.md`  
**New concepts (6):** `autoencoder`, `denoising-autoencoder`, `variational-autoencoder`, `kl-divergence`, `reparameterization-trick`, `latent-space`  
**Flashcards:** ~20

**Flagship Mermaids:**

VAE with reparameterization (slide 20):
```mermaid
flowchart LR
    x[Input x] --> e[Encoder e]
    e --> mu[mu]
    e --> sigma[sigma]
    eps["epsilon ~ N(0, I)"] --> mul[sigma * eps]
    sigma --> mul
    mu --> add["mu + sigma * eps"]
    mul --> add
    add --> z[Latent z]
    z --> d[Decoder d]
    d --> xhat[Reconstruction x hat]
    xhat --> rec[MSE x, x hat]
    x --> rec
    mu --> kl["KL N mu sigma vs N 0 1"]
    sigma --> kl
```

Writing files now.

content.newText:
--- tags: [lecture] course: Computer-Vision source: 20_autoencoders.pdf lecture: 20 created: 2026-04-26 --- # Lecture 20 — Autoencoders ## Overview Autoencoders are the unsupervised cousin of the encoder-decoder networks from lecture 18. **Compress** the input to a low-dimensional latent representation, then **reconstruct** the input from that representation. The bottleneck forces the network to keep only the most important features. This lecture builds up from the plain autoencoder to the variational autoencoder (VAE), the first proper generative-model architecture covered in the course. 1. **Plain autoencoder** — encoder–bottleneck–decoder, trained to reconstruct the input. Acts as non-linear PCA. 2. **Denoising autoencoder** — corrupt the input; train the network to recover the clean original. More robust representations. 3. **Autoencoders as generative models** — sample random latent vectors and decode → new images. Latent-space arithmetic and interpolation work surprisingly well. 4. **Why plain AEs fail as generative models** — the latent space is **not continuous**: random points often map to nonsense. 5. **Variational autoencoder (VAE)** — encode each input as a *distribution*, not a point. Impose a $\mathcal{N}(0, I)$ prior on the latent space via KL divergence. Get a smooth, continuous latent space that supports random sampling. 6. **The reparameterization trick** — express $z = \mu + \sigma \odot \varepsilon$ with $\varepsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I)$, making sampling differentiable. ## Key concepts - [[autoencoder]] — encoder + bottleneck + decoder, MSE reconstruction loss - [[denoising-autoencoder]] — robustness via input corruption - [[latent-space]] — interpolation, arithmetic, manifolds - [[variational-autoencoder]] — distribution-valued encoder + smooth latent space - [[kl-divergence]] — measures distance between distributions; the regulariser in VAEs - [[reparameterization-trick]] — makes stochastic sampling differentiable ## Equations ### Plain AE reconstruction loss (slide 4) $$L(x) = \|x - \hat x\|^2$$ where $\hat x = d(e(x))$ — encode then decode. ### KL divergence between Gaussians (slide 16) For $\mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)$ vs $\mathcal{N}(0, 1)$: $$D_\text{KL}\bigl(\mathcal{N}(\mu_i, \sigma_i)\,\|\,\mathcal{N}(0, I)\bigr) = -\frac{1}{2}\sum_i\!\left(1 + \log(\sigma_i^2) - \mu_i^2 - \sigma_i^2\right)$$ ### VAE total loss (slide 18) $$\mathcal{L}(x_i, \hat x_i, \mu_i, \sigma_i) = \mathrm{MSE}(x_i, \hat x_i) + \beta\, D_\text{KL}\!\left(\mathcal{N}(\mu_i, \sigma_i)\,\|\,\mathcal{N}(0, I)\right)$$ $\beta$ trades off reconstruction quality vs latent-space smoothness. $\beta > 1$ produces a more disentangled latent (β-VAE). ### Reparameterization (slide 19–20) $$z = \mu + \sigma \odot \varepsilon,\qquad \varepsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I)$$ Replaces a non-differentiable "sample from $\mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)$" with a deterministic operation on $(\mu, \sigma)$ and an external noise input. ## Diagrams ### Plain autoencoder (slide 4) ```mermaid flowchart LR x[Input x] --> enc[Encoder] enc --> z[Latent z bottleneck] z --> dec[Decoder] dec --> xhat[Reconstruction x hat] x --> loss["MSE x minus x hat squared"] xhat --> loss ``` The bottleneck dimension is much smaller than the input dimension. To minimise reconstruction error through the bottleneck, the encoder must keep only the most informative features. The decoder learns to invert that compression. ### Denoising autoencoder (slide 7) ```mermaid flowchart LR x[Original x] --> noise["Corrupt: x_tilde
(mask, noise, blur)"] noise --> enc[Encoder E] enc --> c[Bottleneck c] c --> dec[Decoder D] dec --> xprime[Reconstruction x prime] x --> loss["loss x vs x prime"] xprime --> loss ``` The network never sees the clean input — only the corrupted version. To minimise loss, the encoder must learn to *ignore* the noise and encode only the signal. Yields more robust representations than a plain AE. ### Latent-space arithmetic (slides 9–10) ```mermaid flowchart LR smile[smiling woman avg] --> sub neutw[neutral woman avg] --> sub((minus)) sub --> direction[smile direction in latent] direction --> add neutm[neutral man avg] --> add((plus)) add --> result[smiling man latent] result --> dec[Decoder] dec --> img([smiling man image]) ``` Average the latents of "smiling woman" minus "neutral woman" gives the **smile** direction. Add it to "neutral man" → decode → "smiling man." Works because well-trained autoencoders learn approximately *linear* attribute directions in the latent space. ### Why plain AEs fail as generative models (slide 12) ```mermaid flowchart TD train[Training: encode each image to one point] train --> result[Latent space has gaps and irregular regions] result --> sample[Random latent point] sample --> q{Inside the data manifold?} q -->|"yes"| good[Decoder produces a sensible image] q -->|"no, in a gap"| bad[Decoder produces nonsense] ``` Standard AE training only cares about reconstruction. Nothing forces nearby regions of the latent space to decode into sensible images, so random sampling produces garbage most of the time. ### VAE: encode as distribution, not point (slide 13) ```mermaid flowchart LR x[Input] --> ae[AE encoder maps to a single point] x --> vae[VAE encoder maps to a Gaussian distribution] ae --> sharp[Sharp delta in latent space] vae --> smooth[Smooth Gaussian in latent space] smooth --> note[Overlapping Gaussians create continuity
between training examples] ``` Each input gets a $(\mu_x, \sigma_x)$. At training time, $z$ is sampled from $\mathcal{N}(\mu_x, \sigma_x)$ and fed to the decoder. The KL term pulls every $(\mu_x, \sigma_x)$ towards $\mathcal{N}(0, I)$ — a shared global prior. As a result, training-time Gaussians overlap, giving the latent space continuity. ### VAE architecture with reparameterization (slide 20) ```mermaid flowchart LR x[Input x] --> e[Encoder e] e --> mu[mu_x] e --> sigma[sigma_x] eps["epsilon ~ N 0 I"] --> mul[sigma_x times epsilon] sigma --> mul mu --> add[mu_x plus sigma_x times epsilon] mul --> add add --> z[Latent z] z --> d[Decoder d] d --> xhat[Reconstruction x hat] xhat --> rec[MSE x, x hat] x --> rec mu --> kl[KL N mu sigma vs N 0 I] sigma --> kl rec --> total[Total loss = MSE + beta KL] kl --> total ``` The split into $(\mu_x, \sigma_x)$ + external noise $\varepsilon$ is the reparameterization trick (see [[reparameterization-trick]]) — making the stochastic sampling differentiable so backprop can flow through. ### KL divergence intuition (slide 17) A measure of how different distribution $A$ is from distribution $B$. For $\mathcal{N}(0, 1)$ vs $\mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)$: - $\mu = 0, \sigma = 1$: distributions match, KL = 0. - $\mu \neq 0$ or $\sigma \neq 1$: positive KL, growing as the distributions separate. In the VAE, the KL term penalises the encoder for outputs that don't look like the standard normal prior. The reconstruction term competes against this — the encoder wants to spread distinct inputs to distant means, the KL wants to pull all means to 0. ## Open questions - Why do random points in a VAE's latent space decode to plausible images, while random points in a plain AE's latent space don't? - $\beta$ in the VAE loss trades off reconstruction quality vs latent-space smoothness. β-VAE (Higgins et al, 2017) found that $\beta > 1$ produces *disentangled* representations — separate axes of the latent encode separate semantic factors. Why does increasing the KL weight cause this? - VAE outputs are typically blurry compared to GAN or diffusion outputs. Why? (The MSE reconstruction loss averages over plausible outputs.) ## Connections to other lectures - The encoder-decoder structure is the same as [[unet]] (lecture 18), but here both ends produce the **same** type of object (image-to-image) instead of image-to-mask. - VAEs are a generative model — predecessors to today's diffusion models. They share the encoder-decoder framing with [[clip]]'s image+text encoders, but here the goal is **density modelling**, not joint embedding. - Autoencoders are a form of [[self-supervised-learning]] (lecture 19) — generative pretext task with reconstruction as the supervisory signal. - The [[kl-divergence]] used here also appears as the loss in modern distillation methods (DINO uses a KL-style cross-entropy between student and teacher).

content.newText:
---
name: Autoencoder
description: Encoder-bottleneck-decoder network trained to reconstruct its input — non-linear PCA for representation learning
type: concept
courses: [Computer-Vision]
source: [20_autoencoders.pdf]
created: 2026-04-26
tags: [concept, deep-learning, representation-learning, generative]
---

# Autoencoder

A neural network trained to **reproduce its input**. Architecture: an **encoder** maps the input $x$ to a low-dimensional **latent code** $z$ (the "bottleneck"); a **decoder** maps $z$ back to a reconstruction $\hat x$. Trained to minimise reconstruction error $\|x - \hat x\|^2$.

The bottleneck is the point of the architecture: forcing the network to compress means it must learn a feature representation that captures the input's most important structure.

## Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    x[Input x] --> enc[Encoder]
    enc --> z[Latent z bottleneck]
    z --> dec[Decoder]
    dec --> xhat[Reconstruction x hat]
    x --> loss["MSE: x minus x hat squared"]
    xhat --> loss
```

The bottleneck dimension $\dim(z)$ is much smaller than the input dimension. For MNIST, $\dim(z) = 2$ to $32$ is typical; for natural images, $\dim(z) = 64$ to $1024$.

## Why it learns useful features

> In order to compress data, we need to pay attention to the key features only, ignoring the less important ones.

If the encoder kept everything, the decoder couldn't reconstruct from a small $z$. So the encoder is forced to discard noise, keep semantically important structure, and find a *manifold* of low-dimensional codes that preserves enough information to recover the input.

Similar inputs end up with similar representations — a useful side effect.

## Like non-linear PCA

PCA finds the linear projection that minimises reconstruction error after compression to $k$ dimensions. An autoencoder does the same thing — but with non-linear encoders and decoders, so it can capture curved manifolds PCA can't.

In fact, a 1-hidden-layer autoencoder with linear activations and MSE loss recovers exactly PCA. Adding non-linear activations is what makes autoencoders strictly more expressive.

## Limitations of the plain autoencoder

- **Sensitive to small input changes**: a slight perturbation can produce a wildly different latent.
- **No latent regularisation**: the latent space is shaped only by reconstruction. Random points usually decode to nonsense (see [[latent-space]]).
- **No explicit prior**: there's no notion of "where in latent space new examples should live."
- **Not directly useful for generation**: see [[variational-autoencoder]].

## Use cases

- **Dimensionality reduction**: encode the dataset, run downstream ML (clustering, classification) on the lower-dimensional embeddings.
- **Anomaly detection**: train an AE on "normal" data; high reconstruction error on a new sample suggests it's anomalous (the AE never learned to compress that pattern).
- **Pretraining initialisation**: train an AE on unlabelled data, transfer the encoder to a supervised task. Predates contrastive SSL but used the same intuition.
- **Latent-space arithmetic** ([[latent-space]]): even plain AEs support some attribute manipulation if the bottleneck is large enough.

## Variants

- **Denoising autoencoder** ([[denoising-autoencoder]]) — corrupt the input; reconstruct the clean original.
- **Sparse autoencoder** — add an L1 penalty on $z$ to encourage few activations.
- **Variational autoencoder** ([[variational-autoencoder]]) — encode each input as a distribution; KL-regularised toward $\mathcal{N}(0, I)$ for a smooth latent space.
- **Masked autoencoder (MAE)** — mask 75% of patches; reconstruct them. Strong self-supervised pretraining for ViTs.

## Related

- [[denoising-autoencoder]] — robustness variant
- [[variational-autoencoder]] — generative variant
- [[latent-space]] — what the bottleneck produces
- [[unet]] — same encoder-decoder structure but for image-to-mask, not image-to-image
- [[self-supervised-learning]] — autoencoding is a generative pretext task

- **No explicit prior**: there's no notion of "where in latent space new examples should live."
- **Not directly useful for generation**: see [[variational-autoencoder]].

--- name: Denoising autoencoder (DAE) description: Autoencoder trained to recover the clean input from a corrupted version — yields more robust representations type: concept courses: [Computer-Vision] source: [20_autoencoders.pdf] created: 2026-04-26 tags: [concept, deep-learning, representation-learning] --- # Denoising autoencoder (DAE) A variant of the [[autoencoder]] where the input is **deliberately corrupted** before being fed to the encoder, but the network is trained to reconstruct the **clean original**. Yields representations more robust to noise and small perturbations than a plain AE. ## Architecture (slide 7) ```mermaid flowchart LR x[Original x] --> noise["Corrupt to x_tilde
(mask, noise, blur)"] noise --> enc[Encoder E] enc --> c[Bottleneck c] c --> dec[Decoder D] dec --> xprime[Reconstruction x prime] x --> loss["loss between x and x prime"] xprime --> loss ``` The network never sees the clean input on the encoder side. The training target is the original $x$; the input is the corrupted $\tilde x$. The encoder must learn to **ignore the noise** and recover the underlying signal. ## Common corruption types | Corruption | What it does | Why useful | |---|---|---| | **Pixel masking** | Replace a fraction of pixels with 0 | Forces the encoder to use spatial context to fill in gaps | | **Gaussian noise** | Add $\varepsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$ | Robustness to sensor noise | | **Salt-and-pepper** | Replace a fraction with 0 or 1 | Robustness to outlier pixels | | **Block masking** | Mask large rectangular regions | Forces longer-range reasoning (the inspiration for MAE) | ## Why it produces better representations A plain AE can "cheat" by routing input pixels straight through the bottleneck — useful features may not actually emerge. A DAE can't: noise pixels carry no useful signal and would corrupt the reconstruction. The encoder must *understand* the input structure to denoise. The training also implicitly learns the data manifold: the network's output is, in effect, "the closest plausible clean input given this noisy observation." ## Connection to score-based / diffusion models A DAE trained on Gaussian noise approximates the **score** $\nabla_x \log p(x)$ of the data distribution. This is the foundation of denoising diffusion probabilistic models (DDPMs) and modern generative diffusion: a sequence of denoising steps progressively transforms pure noise into a clean sample. So denoising autoencoders are a direct conceptual ancestor of state-of-the-art generative models. ## Use cases - **Image denoising** as a literal task — train on (clean, corrupted) pairs. - **Pretraining** for downstream supervised tasks (predates MAE, similar idea). - **Anomaly detection** — anomalous inputs reconstruct poorly even after "denoising." - **Image inpainting** — train with block masks; the trained network fills in missing regions. ## Related - [[autoencoder]] — the base architecture - [[data-augmentation]] — corruption is a kind of augmentation applied at training time - [[variational-autoencoder]] — orthogonal idea (could be combined into a denoising VAE) - [[self-supervised-learning]] — DAE is a classic SSL approach

--- name: Latent space description: The low-dimensional space of bottleneck codes; supports interpolation and arithmetic when smooth and continuous type: concept courses: [Computer-Vision] source: [20_autoencoders.pdf] created: 2026-04-26 tags: [concept, deep-learning, representation-learning] --- # Latent space The space of low-dimensional codes produced by an encoder. In an [[autoencoder]], the latent vector $z$ is the bottleneck. In a [[variational-autoencoder]], it's the sampled $z$ from the predicted distribution. A useful latent space has three properties: 1. **Compactness** — encodes the data manifold efficiently. 2. **Continuity** — small moves in $z$ produce small changes in the decoded output. 3. **Disentanglement** — separate axes correspond to separate semantic factors. Plain autoencoders typically achieve (1), partially (3), but **not (2)**. VAEs achieve all three (with tuning). ## Latent-space interpolation (slide 11) If the latent space is continuous, we can pick two latents $z_a$ (encoded from image A) and $z_b$ (from image B) and decode the line between them: ```mermaid flowchart LR a[Image A bulldog] --> e1[Encoder] b[Image B wolf] --> e2[Encoder] e1 --> za[z_a] e2 --> zb[z_b] za --> interp["Linear interpolation
z = 1-t z_a + t z_b"] zb --> interp interp --> dec[Decoder] dec --> seq[Sequence of images:
bulldog, half-wolf, wolf] ``` The intermediate $z$ values decode to images that smoothly transition between A and B. Useful for animation, face morphing, exploring "what if" variants. In a VAE: this works reliably. In a plain AE: only when the path between $z_a$ and $z_b$ stays inside the data manifold; off-manifold points often decode to nonsense. ## Latent-space arithmetic (slides 9–10) If the encoder is good enough, **attribute directions** become approximately linear in the latent space: $$z_\text{smiling-man} \approx z_\text{neutral-man} + (z_\text{smiling-woman avg} - z_\text{neutral-woman avg})$$ The vector $(z_\text{smiling-woman} - z_\text{neutral-woman})$ encodes the "smile" direction. Adding it to a neutral man's latent and decoding produces a smiling man. This works because well-trained encoders learn approximately disentangled representations: separate dimensions (or directions) of the latent space correspond to separate semantic factors (smile, gender, age, lighting, pose). Famous early demos: - "King − Man + Woman ≈ Queen" in word embeddings (Mikolov 2013). - "Smiling woman − Neutral woman + Neutral man ≈ Smiling man" in face VAE/GAN latents. ## Why plain autoencoders are not good generative models (slide 12) Sampling a random $z$ from a wide range and decoding it usually produces nonsense. Reasons: - The trained encoder sends inputs to **a discrete set of points** — one per training example. - There's no incentive for nearby latents to decode to similar (and meaningful) images. - Most of the latent space is "empty" — random samples land in regions the decoder was never trained on. Visually: a t-SNE plot of trained AE latents shows tight class clusters with **gaps** between them. Random sampling falls in the gaps. ## Why VAEs fix this VAEs' KL term pulls every encoded distribution towards a shared $\mathcal{N}(0, I)$ prior. The Gaussians overlap, filling the gaps. The decoder is therefore trained on the entire neighbourhood of every encoded distribution — random samples from $\mathcal{N}(0, I)$ land in regions the decoder has seen. See [[variational-autoencoder]]. ## Visualising Two standard tools for inspecting latent spaces: - **t-SNE / UMAP** — non-linear dimensionality reduction down to 2D for plotting. Reveals cluster structure but distorts global geometry. - **PCA** — linear; preserves global geometry but not local structure. For a 2D latent (rare but pedagogically useful), plot directly. ## Related - [[autoencoder]] — produces the latent space - [[variational-autoencoder]] — produces a *smooth* latent space - [[clip]] — joint image-text latent space; arithmetic still works ("painting" + "of" + "cat") - [[contrastive-learning]] — alternative way to shape an embedding space

content.newText:
---
name: Variational autoencoder (VAE)
description: Autoencoder that encodes each input as a Gaussian distribution; KL-regularised toward N(0, I) for a smooth, sampleable latent space
type: concept
courses: [Computer-Vision]
source: [20_autoencoders.pdf]
created: 2026-04-26
tags: [concept, deep-learning, generative]
---

# Variational autoencoder (VAE)

A generative variant of the [[autoencoder]] (Kingma & Welling, 2014) that fixes the plain AE's biggest weakness: an irregular latent space full of holes. VAEs encode each input as a **probability distribution** over the latent, not a single point, and add a KL-divergence term that pulls every encoded distribution toward a shared $\mathcal{N}(0, I)$ prior.

The result: a smooth, continuous latent space where random samples decode to plausible outputs.

## VAE vs plain AE

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ae[Plain autoencoder]
        x1[x] --> e1[Encoder] --> z1[Single z point] --> d1[Decoder] --> xh1[x hat]
    end
    subgraph vae[Variational autoencoder]
        x2[x] --> e2[Encoder] --> mu[mu]
        e2 --> sigma[sigma]
        mu --> samp[Sample z from N mu sigma]
        sigma --> samp
        samp --> z2[z]
        z2 --> d2[Decoder] --> xh2[x hat]
    end
```

The encoder predicts $(\mu_x, \sigma_x)$ instead of a single $z$. At training time, $z$ is **sampled** from $\mathcal{N}(\mu_x, \sigma_x)$ and fed to the decoder.

## Loss function

Two terms:

$$\mathcal{L}(x, \hat x, \mu, \sigma) = \underbrace{\|x - \hat x\|^2}_{\text{reconstruction}} + \beta \cdot \underbrace{D_\text{KL}\!\left(\mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma)\,\|\,\mathcal{N}(0, I)\right)}_{\text{prior matching}}$$

- **Reconstruction (MSE)**: pushes the decoded $\hat x$ toward $x$. Without this, the encoder could output any garbage.
- **KL divergence to $\mathcal{N}(0, I)$**: pushes every encoded distribution toward the standard normal. Without this, the encoder would minimise reconstruction by collapsing $\sigma \to 0$ (back to a plain AE) and using arbitrary $\mu$.

For the Gaussian-vs-Gaussian case (slide 16):

$$D_\text{KL}\bigl(\mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma)\,\|\,\mathcal{N}(0, I)\bigr) = -\frac{1}{2}\sum_i \!\left(1 + \log(\sigma_i^2) - \mu_i^2 - \sigma_i^2\right)$$

See [[kl-divergence]].

## Why a Gaussian prior?

- **Smooth and analytically tractable**: KL between two Gaussians is a closed-form expression. No numerical integration needed.
- **Sampleable**: drawing from $\mathcal{N}(0, I)$ is trivial.
- **Sufficient for most data**: even when the true posterior is non-Gaussian, the Gaussian prior + non-linear decoder can fit complex distributions.

You can use other priors (mixture of Gaussians, Gumbel-softmax for discrete), but $\mathcal{N}(0, I)$ is the default.

## The reparameterization trick (slide 19)

Sampling $z$ from $\mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma)$ is **not differentiable** with respect to $(\mu, \sigma)$ — gradients can't flow through randomness.

The trick:

$$z = \mu + \sigma \odot \varepsilon, \qquad \varepsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I)$$

Express the random sample as a deterministic function of $(\mu, \sigma)$ and an external noise input $\varepsilon$. Now backprop can flow through $\mu$ and $\sigma$ — $\varepsilon$ is just a constant input from the noise generator. See [[reparameterization-trick]].

## Full pipeline (slide 20)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    x[Input x] --> e[Encoder e]
    e --> mu[mu]
    e --> sigma[sigma]
    eps["epsilon ~ N 0 I"] --> mul[sigma times epsilon]
    sigma --> mul
    mu --> add[mu plus sigma times epsilon]
    mul --> add
    add --> z[Latent z]
    z --> d[Decoder d]
    d --> xhat[x hat]
    xhat --> rec[MSE x, x hat]
    x --> rec
    mu --> kl[KL N mu sigma vs N 0 I]
    sigma --> kl
    rec --> total[Total loss = MSE + beta KL]
    kl --> total
```

## Generation

After training, throw away the encoder. To generate new samples:

```python
z = torch.randn(batch_size, latent_dim)   # sample from N(0, I)
samples = decoder(z)
```

Random samples from $\mathcal{N}(0, I)$ now decode to plausible outputs because the KL term forced every encoded distribution to overlap with that prior.

## β-VAE: trading reconstruction for disentanglement

When $\beta > 1$, the KL term dominates. The latent is forced into a *very* regular structure → individual axes of $z$ tend to encode separate semantic factors (face: pose, lighting, age, smile, glasses).

Trade-off: stronger disentanglement → blurrier reconstructions. Tune $\beta$ to taste.

## VAE vs other generative models

| Model | Pros | Cons |
|---|---|---|
| **VAE** | smooth latent, fast sampling, easy training | blurry samples |
| **GAN** | sharp, high-quality samples | hard to train, mode collapse |
| **Diffusion** | very high quality | slow sampling, expensive |
| **Autoregressive** (e.g. PixelCNN) | exact likelihood | slow sampling |

VAEs remain useful when you specifically want a **structured latent space** for downstream tasks (interpolation, conditional generation, attribute editing). For pure image quality, diffusion has largely won.

## Why VAE samples are blurry

The MSE reconstruction loss penalises the model for deviating from the *mean* of plausible reconstructions. When multiple sharp images are equally plausible reconstructions of the same $z$, the optimal MSE prediction is their average — which is blurry.

GANs and diffusion models avoid this by either using adversarial losses (GANs) or noise-prediction losses (diffusion), which don't average over plausible outputs.

## Related

- [[autoencoder]] — the deterministic precursor
- [[kl-divergence]] — the regularisation term
- [[reparameterization-trick]] — makes sampling differentiable
- [[latent-space]] — what the VAE produces (smooth + sampleable)
- [[denoising-autoencoder]] — the conceptual ancestor of diffusion models

content.newText:
---
name: KL divergence
description: Asymmetric measure of how distribution P differs from distribution Q — used as the prior-matching term in VAEs
type: concept
courses: [Computer-Vision]
source: [20_autoencoders.pdf]
created: 2026-04-26
tags: [concept, probability, information-theory, deep-learning]
---

# KL divergence

The Kullback-Leibler divergence $D_\text{KL}(P\,\|\,Q)$ measures how distribution $P$ differs from distribution $Q$. Used in VAEs as the regularisation term that pulls the encoder's output toward a chosen prior.

## Definition

For continuous $P, Q$ with densities $p, q$ on the same support:

$$D_\text{KL}(P\,\|\,Q) = \int p(x) \log\frac{p(x)}{q(x)}\,dx$$

Discrete version:

$$D_\text{KL}(P\,\|\,Q) = \sum_x P(x)\log\frac{P(x)}{Q(x)}$$

## Properties

- **Non-negative**: $D_\text{KL}(P\,\|\,Q) \ge 0$, with equality iff $P = Q$ almost everywhere.
- **Asymmetric**: $D_\text{KL}(P\,\|\,Q) \neq D_\text{KL}(Q\,\|\,P)$ in general. So KL is **not** a metric — there's no triangle inequality.
- **Sensitive to support mismatch**: if $P(x) > 0$ where $Q(x) = 0$, KL is infinite. Practical implementations smooth $Q$.

The asymmetry matters: $D_\text{KL}(P\,\|\,Q)$ "averages over $P$" — it penalises mass-of-$P$ that's not covered by $Q$. The reverse $D_\text{KL}(Q\,\|\,P)$ penalises mass-of-$Q$ not covered by $P$.

## Closed form for two Gaussians

For $P = \mathcal{N}(\mu_1, \Sigma_1)$ and $Q = \mathcal{N}(\mu_2, \Sigma_2)$ in $d$ dimensions:

$$D_\text{KL}(P\,\|\,Q) = \frac{1}{2}\!\left[\mathrm{tr}(\Sigma_2^{-1}\Sigma_1) + (\mu_2 - \mu_1)^\top\Sigma_2^{-1}(\mu_2 - \mu_1) - d + \log\!\frac{\det\Sigma_2}{\det\Sigma_1}\right]$$

Specialising to $P = \mathcal{N}(\mu, \mathrm{diag}(\sigma^2))$ and $Q = \mathcal{N}(0, I)$ — the VAE case (slide 16):

$$D_\text{KL}\bigl(\mathcal{N}(\mu, \mathrm{diag}(\sigma^2))\,\|\,\mathcal{N}(0, I)\bigr) = -\frac{1}{2}\sum_i\!\left(1 + \log(\sigma_i^2) - \mu_i^2 - \sigma_i^2\right)$$

This is a **closed-form, differentiable expression** in $(\mu, \sigma)$ — exactly what's needed for backprop in a VAE.

## Visual intuition (slide 17)

For two Gaussians on the real line:

- $\mathcal{N}(0, 1)$ vs $\mathcal{N}(0, 1)$: identical, KL = 0.
- $\mathcal{N}(0, 1)$ vs $\mathcal{N}(0.5, 1)$: slightly off, small KL.
- $\mathcal{N}(0, 1)$ vs $\mathcal{N}(-2.5, 0.5^2)$: very different mean and variance, KL ≈ 13.

The further apart the means and the more different the variances, the larger the KL.

## In a VAE

The encoder predicts $(\mu_x, \sigma_x)$ for each input $x$. The KL term

$$D_\text{KL}\!\left(\mathcal{N}(\mu_x, \sigma_x)\,\|\,\mathcal{N}(0, I)\right)$$

penalises encoded distributions that don't look like the standard normal. This pulls every encoded distribution toward the same prior, ensuring:

- $\mu$ values stay near 0 (no clusters drifting off to infinity).
- $\sigma$ values stay near 1 (no collapse to deterministic encoding).

The competing reconstruction term wants distinct inputs to map to distinct $\mu$. The balance gives a smooth, structured latent space.

## Why $\beta$ matters in β-VAE

The full VAE loss is:

$$\mathcal{L} = \mathrm{MSE}(x, \hat x) + \beta \cdot D_\text{KL}(\cdots)$$

- $\beta = 1$: standard VAE.
- $\beta > 1$: KL dominates → very smooth latent → disentangled representations but blurrier reconstructions.
- $\beta < 1$: reconstruction dominates → sharper outputs but irregular latent (closer to plain AE).

## KL elsewhere in deep learning

- **Cross-entropy = KL + entropy of the data**, so minimising cross-entropy is equivalent to minimising KL between the data distribution and the model's predicted distribution. This is why classification with `nn.CrossEntropyLoss` is the same as fitting via KL minimisation.
- **DINO** (lecture 19) uses a softmax cross-entropy between student and teacher distributions — effectively a KL divergence, with the teacher's distribution as $Q$.
- **Distillation** trains a student to match a teacher's softened predictions via KL.
- **Variational inference** more broadly uses KL to fit approximate posteriors.

## Related

- [[variational-autoencoder]] — primary application
- [[loss-functions]] — cross-entropy is closely related
- [[reparameterization-trick]] — what makes the KL term trainable end-to-end

The encoder predicts $(\mu_x, \sigma_x)$ for each input $x$. The KL term

$$D_\text{KL}\!\left(\mathcal{N}(\mu_x, \sigma_x)\,\|\,\mathcal{N}(0, I)\right)$$

penalises encoded distributions that don't look like the standard normal. This pulls every encoded distribution toward the same prior, ensuring:

- $\mu$ values stay near 0 (no clusters drifting off to infinity).
- $\sigma$ values stay near 1 (no collapse to deterministic encoding).

The competing reconstruction term wants distinct inputs to map to distinct $\mu$. The balance gives a smooth, structured latent space.

## Why $\beta$ matters in β-VAE

The full VAE loss is:

$$\mathcal{L} = \mathrm{MSE}(x, \hat x) + \beta \cdot D_\text{KL}(\cdots)$$

- $\beta = 1$: standard VAE.
- $\beta > 1$: KL dominates → very smooth latent → disentangled representations but blurrier reconstructions.
- $\beta < 1$: reconstruction dominates → sharper outputs but irregular latent (closer to plain AE).

## KL elsewhere in deep learning

- **Cross-entropy = KL + entropy of the data**, so minimising cross-entropy is equivalent to minimising KL between the data distribution and the model's predicted distribution. This is why classification with `nn.CrossEntropyLoss` is the same as fitting via KL minimisation.
- **DINO** (lecture 19) uses a softmax cross-entropy between student and teacher distributions — effectively a KL divergence, with the teacher's distribution as $Q$.
- **Distillation** trains a student to match a teacher's softened predictions via KL.
- **Variational inference** more broadly uses KL to fit approximate posteriors.

## Related

- [[variational-autoencoder]] — primary application
- [[loss-functions]] — cross-entropy is closely related
- [[reparameterization-trick]] — what makes the KL term trainable end-to-end

---
name: Reparameterization trick
description: Express a sample from N mu sigma as mu + sigma times epsilon — making stochastic sampling differentiable for backprop
type: concept
courses: [Computer-Vision]
source: [20_autoencoders.pdf]
created: 2026-04-26
tags: [concept, deep-learning, generative]
---

# Reparameterization trick

A technique that lets gradient descent train networks whose computation involves a **random sample**. Used in VAEs, normalising flows, and any model where backprop must flow through a stochastic node.

## The problem (slide 19)

A VAE encoder outputs $(\mu, \sigma)$. The decoder needs a latent $z$ sampled from $\mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma)$. But:

> Sampling is **not a differentiable operation**.

You can't backprop through "draw a random number" — there's no derivative of $z$ with respect to $\mu$ or $\sigma$ if $z$ is just a black-box random variable.

## The trick

Rewrite the sampling as a deterministic function of $(\mu, \sigma)$ plus an external random input:

$$z = \mu + \sigma \odot \varepsilon, \qquad \varepsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I)$$

Now:

- $\varepsilon$ is sampled **once per forward pass** from a fixed distribution that doesn't depend on the model parameters.
- $z$ is a deterministic function of $\mu$, $\sigma$, and $\varepsilon$.
- Gradients flow normally through $\mu$ and $\sigma$. $\varepsilon$ is just a constant noise input.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph before[Before reparameterization]
        b1[Encoder produces mu sigma] --> b2[Sample z from N mu sigma]
        b2 --> b3[Decoder]
        b1 -.->|"no gradient through random sampling"| b2
    end
    subgraph after[After reparameterization]
        a1[Encoder produces mu sigma] --> a2[Combine z = mu + sigma times epsilon]
        a3[External epsilon ~ N 0 I] --> a2
        a2 --> a4[Decoder]
        a1 -->|"gradient flows through mu, sigma"| a2
    end
```

## Why it works

The chain rule needs $\partial z / \partial \mu$ and $\partial z / \partial \sigma$. With reparameterization:

- $\partial z / \partial \mu = 1$ (multiplied by appropriate Jacobian shape).
- $\partial z / \partial \sigma = \varepsilon$ (different per sample, but a known number for this forward pass).

Both are well-defined. The randomness sits in $\varepsilon$, which is **outside** the gradient flow.

## Generalisation

The trick works for any distribution that can be expressed as a deterministic transform of a fixed-distribution noise:

| Distribution | Reparameterization |
|---|---|
| $\mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)$ | $\mu + \sigma \cdot \varepsilon$, $\varepsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 1)$ |
| Uniform $[a, b]$ | $a + (b-a) \cdot u$, $u \sim U[0, 1]$ |
| Exponential | $-\log(u) / \lambda$, $u \sim U[0, 1]$ (inverse CDF) |
| Gumbel | $-\log(-\log(u))$ |
| Categorical (discrete) | Gumbel-softmax: $\mathrm{softmax}((\log\pi + g) / \tau)$ where $g$ is Gumbel noise |

For some distributions (e.g. mixture of Gaussians with discrete component selection), no exact reparameterization exists. Workarounds include the score-function estimator (REINFORCE) — higher variance but more general.

## In PyTorch

```python
class VAEEncoder(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, ...):
        super().__init__()
        # ... layers ...
        self.fc_mu = nn.Linear(hidden, latent_dim)
        self.fc_logvar = nn.Linear(hidden, latent_dim)

    def forward(self, x):
        h = self.trunk(x)
        mu = self.fc_mu(h)
        logvar = self.fc_logvar(h)
        return mu, logvar

def reparameterize(mu, logvar):
    std = torch.exp(0.5 * logvar)
    eps = torch.randn_like(std)
    return mu + eps * std
```

Predicting `logvar` (log of variance) instead of $\sigma$ directly is a numerical-stability convention — it lets $\sigma$ stay positive without a softplus and gives more uniform gradient magnitudes.

## Related

- [[variational-autoencoder]] — the primary application
- [[backpropagation]] — what reparameterization enables to flow through
- [[kl-divergence]] — the loss term that wouldn't be trainable without this trick

---
tags: [flashcards, review]
course: Computer-Vision
lecture: 20
source: 20_autoencoders.pdf
created: 2026-04-26
---

# Lecture 20 — Autoencoders (flashcards)

What's the basic structure of an autoencoder, and what does it minimise?
?
**Encoder** maps input $x$ to a low-dimensional latent $z$ (the bottleneck). **Decoder** maps $z$ back to a reconstruction $\hat x$. Trained to minimise reconstruction error $\|x - \hat x\|^2$. The bottleneck forces the network to compress information to its most essential form.

Why is an autoencoder sometimes called "non-linear PCA"?
?
With linear activations and MSE loss, a 1-hidden-layer AE recovers exactly PCA. Adding non-linear activations lets the AE capture curved manifolds PCA can't, so it generalises PCA to non-linear dimensionality reduction.

What's the difference between a denoising autoencoder and a plain autoencoder?
?
A DAE feeds a **corrupted** version of the input to the encoder, but the loss compares the reconstruction to the **clean** original. Forces the encoder to learn to ignore noise and focus on the underlying signal — yielding more robust representations than a plain AE.

Why are denoising autoencoders considered the conceptual ancestor of diffusion models?
?
A DAE trained on Gaussian noise approximates the score $\nabla_x \log p(x)$ of the data distribution. Modern diffusion models do exactly this — sequential denoising of progressively cleaner samples, each step trained as a denoising autoencoder.

In the latent space of a well-trained autoencoder, what kind of operations work?
?
**Interpolation**: linearly interpolate between two latents and decode → smooth transitions between images. **Arithmetic**: well-disentangled latents support attribute directions, e.g. $z_\text{smiling-man} \approx z_\text{neutral-man} + (z_\text{smiling-woman} - z_\text{neutral-woman})$.

Why are plain autoencoders usually not good generative models?
?
Their training only optimises reconstruction; nothing forces continuity between encoded points. Random samples in the latent space land in **gaps** (regions the decoder was never trained on) and produce nonsense. The latent space is densely populated only at the training-set encodings.

What's the main architectural change from an AE to a VAE?
?
Instead of producing a single latent $z$, the encoder outputs $(\mu_x, \sigma_x)$ — the parameters of a Gaussian. At training time, $z$ is **sampled** from $\mathcal{N}(\mu_x, \sigma_x)$ and fed to the decoder.

Write the VAE total loss.
?
$\mathcal{L}(x, \hat x, \mu, \sigma) = \mathrm{MSE}(x, \hat x) + \beta \cdot D_\text{KL}\!\left(\mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma)\,\|\,\mathcal{N}(0, I)\right)$. Reconstruction term + KL prior-matching term, balanced by $\beta$.

What's the role of each of the two terms in the VAE loss?
?
**MSE reconstruction**: makes the decoder useful — without it, the encoder could output anything. **KL divergence to $\mathcal{N}(0, I)$**: regularises the latent — without it, the encoder collapses to $\sigma = 0$ (back to a plain AE). The two together produce a latent that's both expressive and smooth.

Why does the VAE pull encoded distributions toward a $\mathcal{N}(0, I)$ prior?
?
To make the latent space **smooth and continuous**. If every input's encoded Gaussian overlaps with a common $\mathcal{N}(0, I)$, neighbouring points decode to similar (and meaningful) outputs. Random samples from $\mathcal{N}(0, I)$ then land in regions the decoder has been trained on.

Write the KL divergence between $\mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma)$ and $\mathcal{N}(0, 1)$.
?
$D_\text{KL}\bigl(\mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma)\,\|\,\mathcal{N}(0, 1)\bigr) = -\frac{1}{2}\sum_i\!\left(1 + \log(\sigma_i^2) - \mu_i^2 - \sigma_i^2\right)$. Closed-form, differentiable in $(\mu, \sigma)$ — no numerical integration needed.

State two key properties of KL divergence.
?
1) **Non-negative** with equality iff the distributions are identical. 2) **Asymmetric**: $D_\text{KL}(P\,\|\,Q) \neq D_\text{KL}(Q\,\|\,P)$ in general — KL is not a metric.

What problem does the reparameterization trick solve in VAE training?
?
Sampling from $\mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma)$ is not differentiable — backprop can't flow through randomness. The trick rewrites $z = \mu + \sigma \odot \varepsilon$ with $\varepsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I)$. Now $z$ is a deterministic function of $(\mu, \sigma, \varepsilon)$, gradients flow through $(\mu, \sigma)$, and $\varepsilon$ is just an external noise input.

After training a VAE, how do you generate new samples?
?
Throw away the encoder. Sample $z \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I)$, run through the decoder. Random samples now decode to plausible outputs because the KL term forced the encoded distributions to overlap with $\mathcal{N}(0, I)$.

Why does $\beta$ in $\beta$-VAE produce more disentangled representations?
?
Larger $\beta$ makes the KL term dominate. The latent is forced into a very regular structure with limited capacity per dimension → the network learns to use separate axes for separate semantic factors (face: smile, gender, age, lighting). Trade-off: blurrier reconstructions.

Why are VAE samples typically blurrier than GAN or diffusion samples?
?
The MSE reconstruction loss penalises deviation from the **mean** of plausible outputs. When multiple sharp outputs are equally plausible, the optimal MSE prediction is their average — which is blurry. GANs (adversarial loss) and diffusion (noise prediction) avoid this averaging.

Why do you typically predict `log_variance` instead of `sigma` directly in a VAE?
?
Numerical stability. `sigma` must stay positive — predicting it directly requires a softplus or similar. Predicting `log_variance` is unconstrained (can be any real number), and `sigma = exp(0.5 * log_variance)` stays positive automatically. Gradients are also more uniform.

Difference in latent-space output between a plain AE and a VAE for the same input?
?
Plain AE: a **single point** $z$ in the latent space. VAE: a **Gaussian distribution** $\mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma)$. At training time, the VAE samples $z$ from this distribution before passing to the decoder.

What's the relationship between an autoencoder and self-supervised learning?
?
Autoencoding is one of the four families of SSL pretext tasks (generative). The model learns useful representations by reconstructing its input — no labels needed. Modern variants (MAE, masked autoencoders) are state-of-the-art SSL methods for ViTs.

Why would you use a VAE instead of a GAN or diffusion model?
?
For tasks that need a **structured, continuous latent space**: interpolation between examples, conditional generation by manipulating attributes, anomaly detection by reconstruction error. GANs and diffusion produce sharper images but their latent spaces are less explicit and less interpretable.

## Step 5 — Final summary All 11 lectures (10–20) ingested. 100 atomic concepts now in `20-Concepts/`, all 11 lecture notes and all 11 review files present. | Lecture | New concepts | Extended concepts | Flashcards | Mermaid diagrams | Image embeds | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | 15 — Intro to DL (resumed) | 8 (loss-functions, mini-batch-sgd, backpropagation, vanishing-exploding-gradients, weight-initialization, batch-normalization, residual-connection, pytorch-dataloader) | 0 | 25 | 9 | 0 | | 16 — CNNs | 10 (convolution-operation, inductive-bias, feature-map, padding-stride, receptive-field, pooling, cnn-architecture, data-augmentation, cv-tasks, depthwise-separable-conv) | 0 | 25 | 7 | 0 | | 17 — Advanced architectures | 12 (vggnet, inception-module, densenet, squeeze-excitation, self-attention, multi-head-attention, positional-encoding, transformer-encoder, vision-transformer, swin-transformer, convnext, transfer-learning) | 1 (residual-connection) | 30 | 12 | 0 | | 18 — Segmentation & detection | 12 (semantic-segmentation, fully-convolutional-network, upsampling, transposed-convolution, unet, iou, dice-loss, yolo, anchor-boxes, hungarian-matching, detr, sam) | 3 (cv-tasks, non-maximum-suppression, loss-functions) | 30 | 9 | 0 | | 19 — Self-supervised & contrastive | 9 (self-supervised-learning, contrastive-learning, siamese-network, triplet-loss, simclr, clip, few-shot-learning, zero-shot-learning, face-recognition) | 1 (transfer-learning) | 26 | 11 | 0 | | 20 — Autoencoders | 6 (autoencoder, denoising-autoencoder, latent-space, variational-autoencoder, kl-divergence, reparameterization-trick) | 0 | 20 | 7 | 0 | **Image embeds**: zero. All diagrams were Mermaid per CLAUDE.md's "Mermaid first, always" rule. The image-embed allowlist (filter kernels, photographic examples, plotted data, real-photo geometry, Fourier on real data) wasn't a fit for any of these lectures' content — they're all architecture/algorithm/concept material. **Mermaid format compliance**: lectures 16–17's first batch were written before the user's mid-stream rules clarification (no blockquotes, no markdown emphasis, no `$LaTeX$` in unquoted nodes, no list markers, no HTML comments inside diagrams). A separate agent is fixing those retroactively. Lectures 18–20 were written following the new rules: equations live in surrounding prose, only HTML emphasis (``, ``) and `
` inside quoted node labels, subgraphs to chunk complexity, and `mermaid` (not `mehrmaid`) for everything. **Flashcard format**: all 11 review files are in the multi-line `?` format. CLAUDE.md's "When generating flashcards" section was updated to specify multi-line format and preserve `` comments. **Cross-linking**: every new concept links to existing related concepts; every lecture note has a "Connections to other lectures" section that points back into earlier material.
